topics.title: History of HACCP, HACCP Principles, HACCP Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT868 - HACCP PLANNING AND IMPLEMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** History of HACCP, HACCP Principles, HACCP Plan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition of HACCP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points)** is a systematic, science-based, and preventive food safety management system that identifies, evaluates, and controls hazards which are significant for food safety. It is internationally recognized under **Codex Alimentarius** and follows seven (7) governing principles that focus on prevention rather than end-product testing.

The Codex Alimentarius Commission defines HACCP as:

> *"A system which identifies, evaluates, and controls hazards which are significant for food safety."*

This system shifts the paradigm from **reactive inspection** (testing the final product) to **proactive control** (preventing hazards at every stage of the food chain — from raw material production, harvesting, processing, manufacturing, distribution, to point of consumption).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Highway Toll-Booth System**

Imagine a long highway connecting a village (raw material) to a city market (consumer). Cars (food products) travel through multiple checkpoints. Traditional food safety was like checking cars **only at the destination** — if a faulty car reached the market, it was recalled. HACCP, however, is like installing **smart toll-booths (CCPs)** at specific strategic points along the highway. Each toll-booth:

- **Identifies** cars carrying dangerous goods (hazard identification)
- **Checks** specific parameters like weight, brake condition, fuel type (critical limits)
- **Stops or diverts** unsafe vehicles immediately (corrective actions)
- **Records** every check (documentation)

Thus, hazards are intercepted **before** they can harm the consumer. This is the essence of HACCP.

### 1.3 Origin and Etymology

| Term | Expansion |
|------|-----------|
| **H** | Hazard |
| **A** | Analysis |
| **C** | Critical |
| **C** | Control |
| **P** | Points |

A **Hazard** is any biological, chemical, or physical agent in food with the potential to cause an adverse health effect. A **Critical Control Point (CCP)** is a step at which control can be applied and is essential to prevent or eliminate a food safety hazard, or to reduce it to an acceptable level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics & Constants (KTU High-Yield)**
> - Number of HACCP Principles: **7**
> - Number of Implementation Steps (Codex): **12** (5 preparatory + 7 principles)
> - Hazard Categories: **3** (Biological, Chemical, Physical)
> - Codex Alimentarius Year Established: **1963**
> - HACCP Concept Origin: **1959** (Pillsbury / NASA Collaboration)
> - First Formal HACCP Document: **1971** (Pillsbury/USDA/NASA Conference)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 History of HACCP — Chronological Evolution

The development of HACCP is one of the most important milestones in modern food safety history. Let us trace its evolution stage by stage:

#### **Phase 1: The Space Race Genesis (1959–1971)**

- **1959** — The National Aeronautics and Space Administration (**NASA**), the U.S. Army Natick Laboratories, and **Pillsbury Company** collaborated to develop foods for the **space program**.
- The challenge: ensure **zero microbial contamination** in food eaten by astronauts (since illness in zero gravity would be catastrophic).
- The traditional end-product testing was found inadequate because of **statistical sampling limitations** — if 1 in 10,000 cans is contaminated, one would need to test thousands.
- Pillsbury developed a **Zero Defects Program**, then evolved it into HACCP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HACCP Was Invented:** Pillsbury's statisticians calculated that to guarantee 100% safety using end-product testing alone, **every single can** would have to be tested — economically impossible. Hence, prevention at the source became the only viable solution.

#### **Phase 2: Public Domain Dissemination (1971–1985)**

- **1971** — Pillsbury presented HACCP at the **National Conference on Food Protection** (USDA-sponsored), introducing the concept to the wider food industry.
- **1973** — Pillsbury published the first comprehensive HACCP documentation in the *Journal of Food Protection*.
- **1985** — The **National Academy of Sciences (NAS)** published a landmark report *"An Evaluation of the Role of Microbiological Criteria for Foods and Food Ingredients"*, strongly endorsing HACCP over end-product testing.

#### **Phase 3: Regulatory Adoption (1989–1996)**

- **1989** — The **National Advisory Committee on Microbiological Criteria for Foods (NACMCF)** standardized the **7 principles** of HACCP.
- **1993** — The **Codex Alimentarius Commission** (FAO/WHO) issued the *Codex Guidelines for the Application of the HACCP System*, formally internationalizing HACCP.
- **1996** — NACMCF revised the document, formally recognizing the **12-task approach** (5 preliminary tasks + 7 principles).

#### **Phase 4: Mandatory Global Implementation (1997–Present)**

- **1997** — The U.S. **FDA** mandated HACCP for seafood (21 CFR Part 123).
- **1998** — USDA **FSIS** mandated HACCP for meat and poultry products (9 CFR Part 304).
- **2001** — Codex updated the *Codex Alimentarius Food Hygiene Basic Texts* with the latest HACCP framework.
- **2004–2011** — The European Union (EU Regulation 852/2004) made HACCP mandatory for all food businesses.
- **2011** — **FSSC 22000** (Foundation FSSC) scheme was launched, integrating HACCP with ISO 22000.
- **Present Day** — HACCP is the **global benchmark** for food safety, integrated with ISO 22000, BRC, SQF, and FSSC 22000 schemes.

### 2.2 The Three Categories of Hazards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favorite: Memorize the 3 Hazard Categories and their examples**

| Hazard Type | Definition | Common Examples |
|-------------|------------|-----------------|
| **Biological** | Living organisms or their toxins | *Salmonella spp.*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *E. coli O157:H7*, *Clostridium botulinum*, Norovirus, parasites, prions (BSE) |
| **Chemical** | Chemical contaminants (natural or added) | Pesticide residues, heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, As), veterinary drug residues, food additives (excess), cleaning agents, allergens (e.g., peanut, gluten) |
| **Physical** | Foreign physical objects | Glass fragments, metal shavings, bone pieces, stones, plastic, hair, insect fragments |

### 2.3 The Seven Principles of HACCP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Critical — The 7 Principles are the heart of the course. Memorize verbatim:**

| Principle # | Principle Name | Description |
|-------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Principle 1** | **Hazard Analysis** | Identify potential hazards (B/C/P), assess severity (S) and likelihood (L), and determine preventive measures |
| **Principle 2** | **Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs)** | Identify the steps in the process where control is essential to prevent/eliminate a hazard or reduce it to acceptable level |
| **Principle 3** | **Establish Critical Limits** | Set maximum/minimum values (e.g., temperature ≥ 75°C for 2 min) for each CCP |
| **Principle 4** | **Establish Monitoring Procedures** | Define what to monitor, how, frequency, and who is responsible |
| **Principle 5** | **Establish Corrective Actions** | Define actions to be taken when monitoring shows a CCP deviation |
| **Principle 6** | **Establish Verification Procedures** | Confirm that the HACCP system is working effectively (audits, reviews, testing) |
| **Principle 7** | **Establish Documentation and Record-Keeping** | Maintain records of CCP monitoring, deviations, corrective actions, and verification activities |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Decision Logic

While HACCP is qualitative rather than purely mathematical, the **Hazard Analysis** step uses a **Risk Assessment Matrix** based on severity and likelihood:

$$\text{Risk Score (R)} = \text{Severity (S)} \times \text{Likelihood (L)}$$

Where:
- **Severity (S)** is typically rated **1 (Low)**, **2 (Medium)**, **3 (High)**
- **Likelihood (L)** is rated **1 (Rare)**, **2 (Occasional)**, **3 (Frequent)**

**Risk Decision Matrix:**

| S \ L | 1 (Rare) | 2 (Occasional) | 3 (Frequent) |
|-------|----------|----------------|---------------|
| **3 (High)** | Medium | High | **Critical** |
| **2 (Medium)** | Low | Medium | High |
| **1 (Low)** | Low | Low | Medium |

The **CCP Decision Tree** (Codex) is the primary tool to distinguish CCPs from non-CCPs:

| Question (CCP Decision Tree) | Answer | Implication |
|------------------------------|--------|-------------|
| Q1. Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard? | YES → Q2 ; NO → Modify process / stop |
| Q2. Does this step eliminate or reduce the likely occurrence of the hazard to an acceptable level? | YES → **CCP** |
| Q3. Could contamination with the identified hazard(s) occur in excess of acceptable level(s), or could it increase to unacceptable level(s)? | YES → Q4 |
| Q4. Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard to an acceptable level? | YES → **Not a CCP** ; NO → **CCP** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This logic tree is used by HACCP auditors worldwide. ISO 22000:2018, FSSC 22000 v6, and BRC v9 all require its application during HACCP plan development.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 The 12 Implementation Steps (Codex HACCP)

The Codex Alimentarius methodology expands the 7 principles into **12 implementation steps** — 5 preliminary (preparatory) tasks followed by the 7 principles. This is a frequent KTU question worth **7–14 marks**.

#### **Phase A: Preliminary Steps (Tasks 1–5)**

**Step 1: Assemble the HACCP Team**

A multi-disciplinary team should be formed with expertise in:
- Product quality & safety (Food Technologist/Microbiologist)
- Production/operations (Plant Manager)
- Engineering (Equipment/Process Engineer)
- Quality Assurance (QA/QC Manager)
- Sanitation (Housekeeping)
- Procurement (Raw materials)
- Storage & Distribution (Logistics)

$$\text{HACCP Team Composition} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Discipline}_i$$

**Step 2: Describe the Product**

A full product description includes:
- Product name, formulation, ingredients
- Physical/chemical properties (pH, $a_w$, moisture)
- Processing methods (heat treatment, freezing)
- Packaging (vacuum, MAP, retort)
- Shelf-life and storage conditions
- Distribution method (chilled, ambient, frozen)
- Intended consumer (general public, infants, immunocompromised)

**Step 3: Identify Intended Use**

Define how the consumer will use the product:
- Ready-to-eat (RTE) or requires cooking?
- Will it be consumed by vulnerable groups (pregnant women, infants, elderly)?
- Where will it be consumed (home, hospital, cafeteria)?

**Step 4: Construct the Process Flow Diagram**

A schematic representation of every step from raw material receipt to distribution. Each step becomes a candidate for CCP identification.

**Step 5: On-Site Confirmation of Flow Diagram**

The HACCP team physically walks through the plant during operational hours to verify the flow diagram's accuracy. Any discrepancies (e.g., rework loops, hidden cross-contamination routes) must be corrected.

#### **Phase B: The 7 Principles (Steps 6–12)**

**Step 6: Principle 1 — Conduct Hazard Analysis**

For every step in the flow diagram, list:
1. All potential hazards (B/C/P)
2. Their severity (S) — consequence of the hazard
3. Their likelihood (L) — probability of occurrence
4. Risk Score (R = S × L)
5. Preventive control measures

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example: Pasteurized Milk**

For the **pasteurization step** (72°C × 15 sec) of milk:

| Hazard | Type | Severity (S) | Likelihood (L) | Risk (S×L) | Preventive Measure |
|--------|------|--------------|----------------|------------|---------------------|
| *Listeria monocytogenes* | Biological | 3 | 3 | **9** | Pasteurization at 72°C × 15 sec |
| Antibiotic residue | Chemical | 3 | 2 | **6** | Supplier HACCP + milk testing |
| Metal fragments | Physical | 2 | 1 | **2** | In-line metal detector at filler |

**Step 7: Principle 2 — Determine CCPs**

Apply the **Codex Decision Tree** (4 questions, Q1–Q4). A CCP must satisfy at least one of:
- The step **eliminates** a hazard (e.g., retort sterilization)
- The step **prevents** a hazard (e.g., allergen segregation)
- The step **reduces** the hazard to acceptable level (e.g., pasteurization)

For pasteurized milk, CCPs would typically be:
- **CCP-1:** Pasteurization (Biological control)
- **CCP-2:** Metal detection (Physical control)

**Step 8: Principle 3 — Establish Critical Limits**

Each CCP must have measurable critical limits — values that separate safe from unsafe product.

**Example Critical Limits:**
- Pasteurization: **Temperature ≥ 72°C and Holding Time ≥ 15 sec**
- Metal detection: **Fe ≤ 2.0 mm ; Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm ; SS ≤ 3.0 mm**
- pH control: **pH ≤ 4.6** (to prevent *Clostridium botulinum* growth)
- Water activity ($a_w$): **$a_w \leq 0.85$** (inhibits most pathogens)

$$T_{\text{min}} \geq T_{\text{critical limit}}$$

**Step 9: Principle 4 — Establish Monitoring Procedures**

Define the **4 Ws and 1 H**:
- **What** is monitored (e.g., temperature)
- **How** is it monitored (e.g., calibrated digital thermometer)
- **When** (frequency — continuous or periodic)
- **Who** is responsible (job title, not individual name)
- **How** is data recorded (logbook, electronic)

**Step 10: Principle 5 — Establish Corrective Actions**

For every CCP and its critical limit, define:
- **What to do** when deviation occurs (e.g., reject batch, rework, hold product)
- **Who** is responsible (e.g., Shift Supervisor)
- **Disposal procedure** for affected product
- **Root cause investigation** (5-Why analysis)

**Step 11: Principle 6 — Establish Verification Procedures**

Verification confirms that the HACCP system *as a whole* is functioning. Methods include:
- Internal audits (annual or semi-annual)
- End-product microbiological testing (e.g., APC, coliform, *Salmonella* testing)
- Calibration of monitoring instruments
- Review of records (weekly/monthly)
- Validation studies (e.g., heat penetration studies for retort)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation vs Verification Distinction (KTU Favorite Question)**
> - **Validation** — done *before* implementation: *"Are we doing the right things?"* (scientific proof the CCP controls the hazard)
> - **Verification** — done *after* implementation: *"Are we doing what we said?"* (audits, reviews, testing)

**Step 12: Principle 7 — Establish Documentation and Record-Keeping**

Records must be retained typically for the product shelf-life plus **1 year minimum**, or as per regulation (e.g., 2 years in the EU). Records include:
- CCP monitoring records
- Corrective action records
- Verification records
- Calibration records
- Training records
- Audit reports

### 3.2 HACCP Plan Structure

A complete **HACCP Plan** contains the following components, and KTU examiners often ask to "list and briefly explain the contents of a HACCP plan":

$$\text{HACCP Plan} = \{\text{SOP-1}, \text{SOP-2}, \ldots, \text{PRP}\} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{CCP}_i + \text{Records}$$

**Components of a HACCP Plan:**

| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **1. Scope of HACCP Plan** | Product, process, site, and regulatory scope |
| **2. HACCP Team** | Members, roles, responsibilities |
| **3. Product Description & Intended Use** | Comprehensive product characterization |
| **4. Process Flow Diagram** | Verified schematic |
| **5. Hazard Analysis Worksheet** | Hazard, severity, likelihood, risk score, preventive measures |
| **6. CCP Decision Tree Application** | Justification for each CCP designation |
| **7. CCP Summary Table** | CCP #, step, critical limit, monitoring, corrective action, verification |
| **8. Verification Schedule** | Frequency and method |
| **9. Record-Keeping System** | Document control and retention policy |
| **10. Prerequisite Programs (PRPs)** | GMP, sanitation, pest control, allergen management, training |

### 3.3 Prerequisites Programs (PRPs) — The Foundation of HACCP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** HACCP cannot work in isolation. It must be built on solid **Prerequisite Programs (PRPs)**. Without PRPs, HACCP fails.

PRPs cover:

1. **Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP)** — facility, equipment, personnel hygiene
2. **Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures (SSOPs)** — cleaning and sanitation
3. **Pest Control Program**
4. **Allergen Management Program**
5. **Recall Program**
6. **Supplier Approval Program**
7. **Training Program**
8. **Calibration Program**
9. **Chemical Control Program**
10. **Waste Management**

### 3.4 Decision Logic for CCP Determination — Step-by-Step Walkthrough

Let us apply the Codex Decision Tree to a **Canned Soup** processing line, step by step:

**Step: Retort Sterilization (121.1°C, 15 min)**

- **Q1: Do preventive control measures exist for identified biological hazard (e.g., *C. botulinum*)?** → **YES** (retort is the control)
- **Q2: Does this step eliminate or reduce the likely occurrence of the hazard to an acceptable level?** → **YES** (121.1°C kills *C. botulinum* spores)

$$\therefore \text{Retort} = \textbf{CCP-1} \checkmark$$

**Step: Mixing of Ingredients**

- **Q1: Do preventive measures exist for hazard (cross-contamination by allergens)?** → **YES** (validated cleaning)
- **Q2: Does mixing eliminate/reduce the hazard to acceptable level?** → **NO**
- **Q3: Could contamination occur in excess of acceptable level?** → **YES**
- **Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate/reduce the hazard?** → **NO** (no later kill step for allergen)

$$\therefore \text{Mixing} = \textbf{CCP-2} \text{ (allergen control)} \checkmark$$

**Step: Cooling Water Contact**

- **Q1: Do preventive measures exist? (Microbial contamination from cooling water)** → **YES** (chlorination)
- **Q2: Does cooling eliminate hazard?** → **NO**
- **Q3: Could contamination exceed acceptable level?** → **YES**
- **Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate? (e.g., can seaming ensures no recontamination)** → **YES**

$$\therefore \text{Cooling} = \textbf{Not a CCP (CP may apply)} \times$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Diagram: Historical Timeline of HACCP Development

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["1959: NASA Pillsbury Army Collaboration for Space Food Safety"] --> B["1971: HACCP Introduced at US National Conference on Food Protection"]
    B --> C["1973: First HACCP Publication in Journal of Food Protection"]
    C --> D["1985: NAS Endorses HACCP over End Product Testing"]
    D --> E["1989: NACMCF Defines the 7 Principles"]
    E --> F["1993: Codex Alimentarius Adopts HACCP Guidelines Globally"]
    F --> G["1996: 12 Task Approach Standardized by NACMCF"]
    G --> H["1997: FDA Mandates HACCP for Seafood"]
    H --> I["1998: USDA FSIS Mandates HACCP for Meat and Poultry"]
    I --> J["2004: EU Regulation 852 2004 Makes HACCP Mandatory"]
    J --> K["2011: FSSC 22000 Launched Integrating HACCP and ISO 22000"]
    K --> L["Present: HACCP as Global Benchmark ISO 22000 BRC SQF"]
```

### 4.2 Diagram: The 12 Implementation Steps (Codex Framework)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PHASE_A["PHASE A: PRELIMINARY STEPS"]
        S1["Step 1: Assemble HACCP Team"]
        S2["Step 2: Describe the Product"]
        S3["Step 3: Identify Intended Use"]
        S4["Step 4: Construct Process Flow Diagram"]
        S5["Step 5: On Site Confirmation of Flow Diagram"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE_B["PHASE B: 7 HACCP PRINCIPLES"]
        P1["Principle 1: Conduct Hazard Analysis"]
        P2["Principle 2: Determine Critical Control Points CCPs"]
        P3["Principle 3: Establish Critical Limits"]
        P4["Principle 4: Establish Monitoring Procedures"]
        P5["Principle 5: Establish Corrective Actions"]
        P6["Principle 6: Establish Verification Procedures"]
        P7["Principle 7: Establish Documentation and Record Keeping"]
    end

    PRPs["Prerequisite Programs PRPs: GMP SSOP Pest Control Allergen Management Training Calibration"]

    PRPs --> PHASE_A
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    S5 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7
```

### 4.3 Diagram: HACCP Plan Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT MODULE"]
        PD["Product Description"]
        IU["Intended Use"]
        FD["Flow Diagram"]
        TM["HACCP Team Composition"]
    end

    subgraph HAZ["HAZARD ANALYSIS ENGINE"]
        HL["Hazard List Biological Chemical Physical"]
        SA["Severity Assessment"]
        LA["Likelihood Assessment"]
        RSM["Risk Score Matrix S x L"]
        PC["Preventive Control Measures"]
    end

    subgraph CCP["CCP DETERMINATION MODULE"]
        CCPDT["Codex Decision Tree Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4"]
        CCPID["Identify CCPs"]
        CL["Critical Limits Specified"]
    end

    subgraph MONITOR["MONITORING AND CONTROL"]
        MP["Monitoring Plan What How When Who"]
        CA["Corrective Action Protocol"]
        VR["Verification Activities Audit Test Review"]
    end

    subgraph DOC["DOCUMENTATION MODULE"]
        RR["Records and Logs"]
        TR["Training Records"]
        AR["Audit Reports"]
    end

    INPUTS --> HAZ
    HAZ --> RSM --> PC
    PC --> CCPDT --> CCPID --> CL
    CL --> MP --> CA --> VR
    MP --> DOC
    CA --> DOC
    VR --> DOC
```

### 4.4 Diagram: CCP Decision Tree (Codex Logic Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Potential Hazard at Process Step"] --> B{"Q1: Do Preventive Control Measures Exist for This Hazard?"}
    B -- NO --> C["Modify Process or Stop. Not a CCP"]
    B -- YES --> D{"Q2: Does This Step Eliminate or Reduce Hazard to Acceptable Level?"}
    D -- YES --> CCP1["CCP: Critical Control Point Identified"]
    D -- NO --> E{"Q3: Could Contamination Occur Above Acceptable Level?"}
    E -- NO --> F["Not a CCP. Move to Next Hazard"]
    E -- YES --> G{"Q4: Will a Subsequent Step Eliminate or Reduce the Hazard?"}
    G -- YES --> F
    G -- NO --> CCP2["CCP: Critical Control Point Identified"]
```

### 4.5 Diagram: Risk Assessment Matrix Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R_LOW["LOW RISK 1 to 2"]
        L1["Monitor as CP"]
    end
    subgraph R_MED["MEDIUM RISK 3 to 4"]
        M1["Consider Control Measures"]
    end
    subgraph R_HIGH["HIGH RISK 6"]
        H1["Implement Control as CCP"]
    end
    subgraph R_CRIT["CRITICAL RISK 9"]
        C1["MANDATORY CCP"]
    end

    S3_L1["S=3 L=1"] --> R_MED
    S3_L2["S=3 L=2"] --> R_HIGH
    S3_L3["S=3 L=3"] --> R_CRIT
    S2_L3["S=2 L=3"] --> R_HIGH
    S1_L3["S=1 L=3"] --> R_MED
    S2_L2["S=2 L=2"] --> R_MED
    S1_L2["S=1 L=2"] --> R_LOW
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. Define HACCP. Briefly explain its origin and significance in the food industry.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points) is a systematic, science-based, preventive food safety management system that identifies, evaluates, and controls hazards — biological, chemical, and physical — which are significant for food safety. It is based on the seven principles defined by Codex Alimentarius.

**Origin (1 mark):** HACCP was developed in **1959** by the **Pillsbury Company** in collaboration with **NASA** and the **U.S. Army Natick Laboratories** during the U.S. space program. The goal was to produce **zero-defect foods** for astronauts, since traditional end-product testing was statistically inadequate to ensure 100% safety.

**Significance (1 mark):** HACCP shifts food safety from **end-product testing** to **process control and prevention**. It is now a **mandatory global standard** (FDA, USDA-FSIS, EU Regulation 852/2004, Codex Alimentarius) and is the foundation of modern food safety management schemes such as **ISO 22000**, **FSSC 22000**, **BRC**, and **SQF**.

---

**Q2. List the seven principles of HACCP.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.43 marks each, 3 marks for listing all 7 + brief mention):**

1. **Principle 1:** Conduct a hazard analysis.
2. **Principle 2:** Determine the Critical Control Points (CCPs).
3. **Principle 3:** Establish critical limit(s).
4. **Principle 4:** Establish monitoring procedures.
5. **Principle 5:** Establish corrective actions.
6. **Principle 6:** Establish verification procedures.
7. **Principle 7:** Establish documentation and record-keeping.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the **historical development of HACCP** from its origin to its present-day global application. Highlight the major milestones and the role of Codex Alimentarius in internationalizing HACCP. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the **twelve implementation steps** of the Codex HACCP system. Differentiate between the **five preliminary steps** and the **seven principles**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Q(a) (7 marks)

The historical development of HACCP is rooted in the collaboration between **NASA, the U.S. Army Natick Laboratories, and the Pillsbury Company** in **1959**. During the U.S. space program, the critical requirement was to produce **"zero-defect" food** for astronauts — the rationale being that any foodborne illness in zero gravity would be catastrophic and could compromise mission success.

(1 mark) **Initial Phase (1959–1971):** Pillsbury recognized that traditional end-product microbiological testing was statistically inadequate. For instance, to detect contamination at a 1-in-10,000 rate with 95% confidence, **hundreds of samples** per lot would have to be tested, making it economically and logistically infeasible. Therefore, Pillsbury developed a **preventive control system** later named HACCP.

(1 mark) **Public Dissemination (1971–1985):** In **1971**, Pillsbury presented HACCP at the **National Conference on Food Protection** in the USA, marking its introduction to the broader food industry. In **1973**, the first formal HACCP documentation was published. The **National Academy of Sciences (NAS)** report of **1985** strongly endorsed HACCP over end-product testing, catalyzing regulatory interest.

(1 mark) **Standardization (1989–1996):** The **National Advisory Committee on Microbiological Criteria for Foods (NACMCF)** in **1989** formalized the **7 principles of HACCP**. The **Codex Alimentarius Commission** (FAO/WHO) adopted HACCP guidelines in **1993**, providing the global benchmark. The 1996 NACMCF revision expanded these into the **12-task approach** (5 preliminary steps + 7 principles).

(1 mark) **Regulatory Mandates (1997–2011):** The U.S. **FDA** mandated HACCP for seafood (**21 CFR Part 123, 1997**), and the **USDA-FSIS** mandated HACCP for meat and poultry (**9 CFR Part 304, 1998**). The **European Union** made HACCP mandatory through **Regulation (EC) No. 852/2004**. **FSSC 22000** was launched in 2011, integrating HACCP with ISO 22000.

(2 marks) **Role of Codex Alimentarius & Present Day:** Codex Alimentarius (Latin for "Food Code") was established in **1963** by FAO and WHO to protect consumer health and ensure fair food trade practices. In **1993**, Codex published *"Codex Guidelines for the Application of the Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point (HACCP) System"* — providing an internationally harmonized framework. Today, HACCP is the cornerstone of every global food safety management system, including **ISO 22000:2018**, **BRC v9**, **SQF Edition 9**, and **FSSC 22000 v6**. It has been adopted in over **180 countries** and is mandatory in most developed economies.

---

### Model Answer — Q(b) (7 marks)

The **Codex Alimentarius Commission** expanded the seven principles of HACCP into **12 implementation steps**, which consist of **5 preliminary tasks** followed by the **7 principles**.

**(3.5 marks) Five Preliminary Steps:**

1. **Assemble the HACCP Team:** A multidisciplinary team is constituted, comprising specialists in food science/microbiology, engineering, sanitation, quality assurance, production, and procurement. The team leader should be trained in HACCP.

2. **Describe the Product:** A comprehensive product description is drafted, including formulation, ingredients, physical/chemical properties (pH, water activity $a_w$, moisture content), processing, packaging, shelf-life, storage conditions, and distribution method.

3. **Identify the Intended Use:** This defines the consumer (general population, infants, immunocompromised, allergic), how the product will be consumed (RTE or needs cooking), and place of consumption (home, hospital, canteen).

4. **Construct the Process Flow Diagram:** A schematic of every step from raw material reception through processing, packaging, storage, and distribution is drawn. Each step becomes a candidate for hazard analysis and CCP identification.

5. **On-Site Confirmation of the Flow Diagram:** The HACCP team physically walks through the plant during operational hours to verify the flow diagram's accuracy and to identify hidden cross-contamination routes or rework loops.

**(3.5 marks) Seven Principles:**

1. **Hazard Analysis:** Identify all potential biological, chemical, and physical hazards, assess severity and likelihood, calculate risk score (S × L), and determine preventive control measures.

2. **Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs):** Apply the **Codex Decision Tree** to identify steps where hazards can be prevented, eliminated, or reduced to acceptable levels.

3. **Establish Critical Limits:** Define measurable parameters (e.g., temperature ≥ 72°C × 15 sec for pasteurization, $a_w \leq 0.85$) that distinguish acceptable from unacceptable product.

4. **Establish Monitoring Procedures:** Define what is monitored, the method, frequency, responsible person, and recording method.

5. **Establish Corrective Actions:** Pre-define actions for deviation — product disposition, root cause analysis (5-Why), and preventive measures.

6. **Establish Verification Procedures:** Conduct audits, end-product testing, calibration, and reviews to confirm the HACCP system is functioning correctly.

7. **Establish Documentation and Record-Keeping:** Maintain records for at least the product shelf-life plus 1 year (or as per regulation).

| Aspect | Preliminary Steps | Seven Principles |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| **Purpose** | Foundation and preparation | Core food safety control logic |
| **Number** | 5 tasks | 7 principles |
| **Output** | Team, scope, flow diagram, PRPs | CCPs, limits, monitoring, records |
| **When Applied** | Before HACCP implementation | During HACCP implementation |
| **Sequencing** | Always first | Always follow preliminary steps |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the structure of a **HACCP Plan**. List and briefly describe its key components with a suitable example from a food product of your choice. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **CCP Decision Tree** as defined by Codex Alimentarius. Apply it to a real-world food process to identify at least two CCPs, justifying each step. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Q(a) (7 marks)

A **HACCP Plan** is a documented system that defines the procedures to be followed to ensure food safety. Its structure is comprehensive and standardized.

**(5 marks) Key Components of a HACCP Plan** (chosen example: **Pasteurized Milk**):

1. **Title Page and Scope Statement:** Identifies the product, process line, plant, and applicable regulations.

2. **HACCP Team:** List of members, their qualifications, roles, and responsibilities.

3. **Product Description:** For pasteurized milk:
   - Composition: Cow's milk standardized to 3.5% fat, 8.5% SNF
   - pH: 6.6–6.8
   - $a_w$: ~0.99
   - Processing: HTST pasteurization at 72°C × 15 sec
   - Packaging: Tetra Pak aseptic
   - Shelf-life: 6 months at ambient
   - Storage: Ambient (≤ 25°C)
   - Distribution: Cold chain not required (aseptic)

4. **Intended Use:** Direct consumption by general population; ready-to-drink.

5. **Process Flow Diagram:** Raw milk reception → Chilling → Standardization → HTST Pasteurization → Homogenization → Cooling → Aseptic Filling → Storage → Distribution.

6. **Hazard Analysis Worksheet:** Documents each hazard at each step, severity, likelihood, risk score, and preventive measure.

7. **CCP Decision Tree Application:** Applied at every step to identify CCPs.

8. **CCP Summary Table:**

| CCP # | Process Step | Critical Limit | Monitoring | Corrective Action | Verification |
|-------|-------------|----------------|------------|-------------------|--------------|
| CCP-1 | Pasteurization | T ≥ 72°C ; t ≥ 15 sec | Continuous chart recorder + digital probe | Divert unpasteurized milk; rework | Heat penetration studies; phosphatase test |
| CCP-2 | Metal Detection | Fe ≤ 2.0 mm; Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm; SS ≤ 3.0 mm | Every pack, automatic reject | Reject product; inspect upstream equipment | Test with standardized metal samples |
| CCP-3 | Aseptic Filling | Seal integrity ≥ 95% | Visual + seal-strength test every 30 min | Reject defective packs | Microbiological leak test (weekly) |

9. **Verification Schedule:** Internal audits (quarterly), end-product microbiological testing (per batch), calibration (monthly).

10. **Record-Keeping System:** All records retained for shelf-life + 1 year minimum.

11. **Prerequisite Programs (PRPs):** GMP, SSOPs, pest control, allergen management, training, calibration.

**(2 marks) Conclusion:** A HACCP plan is a living document that integrates preventive control logic with operational practice, ensuring food safety from raw material to consumer.

---

### Model Answer — Q(b) (7 marks)

The **CCP Decision Tree** is a sequential questioning tool developed by Codex Alimentarius to determine whether a process step is a CCP. It consists of **four sequential questions (Q1–Q4)**.

**(3 marks) Description of the Decision Tree:**

- **Q1 — Preventive Measure Exists?** If NO, modify the process or accept the hazard. If YES, proceed to Q2.
- **Q2 — Eliminate or Reduce to Acceptable Level?** If YES, the step **IS a CCP**. If NO, proceed to Q3.
- **Q3 — Could Contamination Occur Above Acceptable Level?** If NO, **Not a CCP**. If YES, proceed to Q4.
- **Q4 — Will a Subsequent Step Eliminate or Reduce?** If YES, **Not a CCP**. If NO, the step **IS a CCP**.

**(4 marks) Application — Pasteurized Milk Processing:**

**Step: Pasteurization**
- **Hazard identified:** *Listeria monocytogenes* (biological, severe pathogen)
- **Q1:** Do preventive measures exist? → **YES** (temperature control)
- **Q2:** Does this step eliminate or reduce the hazard to acceptable level? → **YES** (72°C × 15 sec destroys *Listeria*)
- **∴ This step IS a CCP → CCP-1** ✓

**Justification:** Pasteurization is the *only* kill-step in the process. No subsequent step reduces *Listeria* count. Therefore, it is essential for product safety.

**Step: Metal Detection**
- **Hazard identified:** Metal fragments (physical, choking/injury hazard)
- **Q1:** Do preventive measures exist? → **YES** (in-line metal detector + upstream equipment maintenance)
- **Q2:** Does this step eliminate the hazard? → **YES** (metal detector identifies and rejects)
- **∴ This step IS a CCP → CCP-2** ✓

**Justification:** Once a metal fragment enters a finished pack, no subsequent step can remove it. The metal detector is the *last line of defense*.

**Counter-Example: Cooling Section**
- **Hazard:** Cross-contamination from cooling water
- **Q1:** Preventive measure exists? → **YES** (chlorination)
- **Q2:** Does cooling eliminate hazard? → **NO**
- **Q3:** Could contamination exceed acceptable level? → **YES**
- **Q4:** Will a subsequent step eliminate? → **YES** (aseptic filling is enclosed; no further contamination)
- **∴ Not a CCP — but a Control Point (CP)**

This demonstrates how the decision tree provides **objective, consistent logic** for CCP identification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing Validation with Verification (loses 2–3 marks):** Validation is done **once before** implementation (proof that the CCP works). Verification is **continuous, after** implementation (proof that the system works as designed). Many students interchange these terms.
>
> 2. **Confusing CCP with CP (loses 1–2 marks):** A **Control Point (CP)** is a step where a hazard is controlled but is **not the only or essential** step for safety. A **CCP** is the **critical** step where absence of control leads to unacceptable risk.
>
> 3. **Skipping PRPs (loses 2 marks):** HACCP cannot stand alone. Examiners expect explicit mention of **Prerequisite Programs (GMP, SSOPs, Pest Control, etc.)** as the foundation.
>
> 4. **Listing Principles without elaboration:** In 14-mark questions, each principle requires at least 2–3 lines of explanation, not just a one-line listing.
>
> 5. **Wrong Year/Origin of HACCP:** Many students write "1971" instead of "1959" as the year HACCP was developed. **1959 = NASA-Pillsbury-Army origin; 1971 = First public introduction.**
>
> 6. **Missing the role of Codex Alimentarius (1963 establishment, 1993 HACCP guidelines)** — frequently asked and frequently missed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **HACCP** = **H**azard **A**nalysis **C**ritical **C**ontrol **P**oints
- **Year of Origin:** 1959 (NASA + Pillsbury + U.S. Army Natick)
- **Public Introduction:** 1971 (U.S. National Conference on Food Protection)
- **Standardization of 7 Principles:** 1989 (NACMCF)
- **International Adoption:** 1993 (Codex Alimentarius)
- **12 Implementation Steps:** 5 Preliminary + 7 Principles
- **Three Hazard Categories:** Biological (B), Chemical (C), Physical (P)
- **Risk Score Formula:** R = Severity (S) × Likelihood (L)
- **Codex Decision Tree:** Q1 → Q2 → Q3 → Q4 (apply sequentially)
- **Critical Limits:** Must be **measurable** (temperature, time, pH, $a_w$, metal size)
- **Validation** = before implementation (Are we doing the right things?)
- **Verification** = after implementation (Are we doing what we said?)
- **PRPs** (Prerequisite Programs) are the **foundation** of HACCP
- **Records Retention:** Shelf-life + 1 year (or per regulation)
- **Examples of CCPs:** Pasteurization, retort sterilization, metal detection, allergen segregation, pH control for *C. botulinum* prevention (pH ≤ 4.6)
- **HACCP is mandatory** under FDA (1997 seafood), USDA-FSIS (1998 meat/poultry), EU Reg. 852/2004 (all foods)
- **Modern integrations:** ISO 22000:2018, FSSC 22000 v6, BRC v9, SQF Edition 9

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Pre-requisite Programmes (PRP), Operational Pre-requisite Programmes (OPRP), and GMP in Food Industry
Documentation Required for Implementation of HACCP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT868 - HACCP PLANNING AND IMPLEMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Documentation Required for Implementation of HACCP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HACCP Documentation** refers to the systematic, written, and verifiable set of records, procedures, worksheets, and plans that form the auditable evidence base for a Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point (HACCP) system. As per the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)** and the **National Advisory Committee on Microbiological Criteria for Foods (NACMCF)** principles, documentation is the *Third Preliminary Task* and the foundation of the **Seven Principles of HACCP**, ensuring traceability, verification, regulatory compliance, and continuous improvement.

Documentation transforms an *invisible food safety control system* into a **transparent, auditable, and reproducible** management framework that withstands third-party audits (FSSC 22000, BRCGS, SQF, ISO 22000).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pilot's Flight Manual" Intuition

Imagine a commercial airline pilot preparing for a flight:

- The **cockpit checklist** = CCP Monitoring Records
- The **pre-flight inspection log** = Prerequisite Programs (PRPs) and Sanitation Records
- The **flight plan** = The HACCP Plan Document
- The **air traffic control logs** = Verification & Validation Records
- The **incident/emergency response procedure** = Corrective Action Records
- The **black box (flight data recorder)** = All HACCP Records (proof that controls actually worked)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** If a food safety control is not *documented*, it essentially did not happen — both in the eyes of an auditor and in the eyes of the law. This is the cardinal rule of HACCP documentation under the KTU 2024 scheme.

## 1.3 Why Documentation is Non-Negotiable

| Purpose | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Legal Compliance** | Mandatory under FSSAI (Food Safety & Standards Act, 2006, India), US FDA 21 CFR Part 117, EU Regulation 852/2004 |
| **Auditability** | Provides objective evidence to third-party auditors (FSSC 22000, BRC) |
| **Traceability** | One-step-forward and one-step-back product traceability |
| **Verification** | Demonstrates that the HACCP system is functioning as designed |
| **Knowledge Continuity** | Ensures uniformity when staff changes occur |
| **Recall Readiness** | Enables rapid and accurate product recall within mandated timeframes |
| **Continuous Improvement** | Trend analysis from records drives preventive actions |
| **Customer Confidence** | Builds buyer, retailer, and consumer trust |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks "Why is documentation important?", always structure your answer around the *Five Pillars*: **Legal, Audit, Trace, Verify, Improve (LATVI)**. Examiners reward this structured mnemonic.

## 1.4 The HACCP Pyramid of Documentation

A useful mental model places HACCP documentation in a **4-tier pyramid**:

1. **Tier 1 — Foundation (PRPs & SOPs):** Sanitation, personal hygiene, pest control, supplier approval
2. **Tier 2 — The HACCP Plan Document:** Hazard analysis, CCP determination, critical limits
3. **Tier 3 — Operational Records:** Monitoring, corrective actions, calibration logs
4. **Tier 4 — Verification & Validation:** Internal audits, end-product testing, validation studies

Each tier **depends on the tier below it** — if the foundation (PRPs) fails, the entire HACCP structure collapses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Mandatory Categories of HACCP Documentation

According to the **Codex Alimentarius** and the **NACMCF HACCP Principles (1997, updated 2020)**, HACCP documentation is organized into **six interconnected categories**. Each must exist in a written, controlled, and version-managed format.

### Category 1: Prerequisite Programs (PRPs) Documentation

PRPs are the **operational foundation** upon which the entire HACCP system is built. They include Good Manufacturing Practices (GMPs), Good Hygienic Practices (GHPs), and other baseline conditions.

**Required PRP Documents:**
- Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures (SSOPs)
- Personal Hygiene Policy
- Pest Control Program & Logbook
- Allergen Management Program
- Supplier Approval & Incoming Material Control Procedure
- Equipment Calibration Schedule & Records
- Maintenance & Preventive Maintenance Records
- Cleaning & Disinfection Schedules
- Water & Air Quality Testing Records
- Waste Management Procedure
- Recall & Traceability Procedure
- Training Program & Competency Records

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Codex Statement (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020):** "PRPs shall be documented, implemented, and maintained to control the likelihood of introducing food safety hazards through the work environment, personnel, and equipment."

### Category 2: HACCP Plan Document

The **HACCP Plan** is the master document — the "blueprint" — that captures the **Five Preliminary Tasks** and the **Seven Principles** applied to a specific product/process.

**Mandatory Components of a HACCP Plan:**

| Component | Content |
|---|---|
| **Scope** | Product name, intended use, consumer target, process boundaries |
| **Product Description** | Composition, physical/chemical properties, shelf life, packaging |
| **Process Flow Diagram** | Block diagram of all process steps (raw material → dispatch) |
| **Hazard Analysis Worksheet** | Identified biological, chemical, physical hazards for each step |
| **Risk Assessment Matrix** | Severity × Likelihood = Risk Score |
| **CCP Determination Record** | Decision tree output (CCPs marked with the "tree") |
| **Critical Limits** | Measurable parameters (e.g., temperature ≤ **4 °C**, pH ≤ **4.6**) |
| **Monitoring Procedures** | What, How, When, Where, Who |
| **Corrective Actions** | Predefined actions for each CCP deviation |
| **Verification Activities** | Calibration, sampling, audits |
| **Record-Keeping System** | List of all records, retention period, storage location |

### Category 3: Monitoring Records (CCP Operational Logs)

These are the **real-time data capture documents** filled at the shop-floor level. They must be:
- Filled at the **time of monitoring** (no back-dating allowed)
- Signed/initialed by the **designated responsible person**
- Reviewed by a **qualified supervisor** within **24 hours**
- Retained for a **minimum of one product shelf life + one year** (typically 2 years for ambient, longer for frozen/sterile products)

**Common Monitoring Record Templates:**

1. CCP Temperature Monitoring Log (e.g., pasteurization at **72 °C for 15 seconds**)
2. pH Monitoring Log (e.g., final product pH ≤ **4.6** for acidified foods)
3. Metal Detector Check Log (test pieces: Fe **2.5 mm**, Non-Fe **3.0 mm**, SS **3.5 mm**)
4. Visual Inspection Record (foreign body control)
5. Weight/Portion Control Log
6. Cooking Temperature & Time Log
7. Cooling Rate Record (e.g., 70 °C → 4 °C within **4 hours** for cooked foods)
8. Chlorine Concentration Log (e.g., **50–200 ppm** for sanitizer)

### Category 4: Corrective Action Records

Whenever a critical limit is exceeded or a deviation occurs, a **Corrective Action Report (CAR)** must be completed. The classical Codex-compliant format captures:

$$
\text{CAR} = f(\text{Deviation},\ \text{Root Cause},\ \text{Action Taken},\ \text{Disposition},\ \text{Preventive Measure})
$$

**Required fields in a CAR:**
- Date, time, shift, product batch/lot
- Specific deviation observed (e.g., cooker temperature = **68 °C** vs limit **72 °C**)
- Quantity of affected product
- **Immediate corrective action** (e.g., reprocess, rework, hold, destroy)
- Investigation of root cause
- **Preventive action** to prevent recurrence
- Signature of HACCP team leader
- Verification of effectiveness

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Corrective Action" (fixes the *current* deviation) with "Preventive Action" (prevents *future* deviations). Both must be in the CAR. Losing marks here is common.

### Category 5: Verification Records

Verification answers the question: *"Is the HACCP system working as intended?"*

**Verification Documentation Includes:**
- **Calibration Records** for thermometers, pH meters, metal detectors (frequency: typically monthly/quarterly, traceable to national standards)
- **Internal Audit Reports** (at least once per year per Codex)
- **End-Product Microbiological Test Reports** (pathogen + indicator organism results)
- **HACCP Plan Reassessment** records (annual review or when any change occurs)
- **Supplier Verification** (audit reports, certificates of analysis)
- **Consumer Complaint Log** (trends reviewed at HACCP team meetings)
- **HACCP Team Meeting Minutes** (typically quarterly)

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation vs. Verification Distinction** *(Frequently asked in KTU exams)*:
> - **Validation** = "Have we set the **right** controls?" *(done initially, evidence that the control is scientifically effective)*
> - **Verification** = "Are we doing the controls **right**?" *(done routinely, evidence that the system is operating correctly)*

### Category 6: Support Documentation

- **HACCP Team Composition & Training Records** (qualifications, HACCP training certificates)
- **Flow Diagram Accuracy Confirmation** (on-site walk-through by HACCP team)
- **Recall Records** (mock recall traceability test results — should achieve **≥ 95% traceability within 4 hours**)
- **Customer Specifications**
- **Regulatory Permits & Licenses** (FSSAI license, export certifications)
- **Pest Control Service Reports** (monthly or quarterly)
- **Chemical Inventory & Safety Data Sheets (SDS)**
- **Equipment Validation Documents** (IQ, OQ, PQ)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Documentation Master Table

| Document Type | Frequency of Use | Retention Period | Who Maintains | Audit Risk if Missing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| HACCP Plan | Created once, reviewed annually | Permanent (lifecycle of product) | HACCP Team Leader | **Critical Major Non-Conformance** |
| Hazard Analysis Worksheet | Initial + on change | Permanent | Food Safety Team | Critical Major |
| CCP Monitoring Logs | **Real-time / per shift** | 1 year past shelf life + 1 year | Production Supervisor | Critical Major |
| Corrective Action Reports | As needed (on deviation) | 2 years minimum | HACCP Coordinator | Critical Major |
| Calibration Records | Monthly / Quarterly | 3–5 years | QA/Engineering | Major |
| Sanitation Records | Daily | 1 year | Sanitation Supervisor | Major |
| Pest Control Records | Monthly | 2 years | Facility Manager | Major |
| Training Records | Per training event | Duration of employment + 2 years | HR / QA | Minor–Major |
| Mock Recall Records | Annually | 3 years | Food Safety Manager | Major |
| Internal Audit Reports | Annually | 3 years | Lead Auditor | Major |
| Supplier Approval Records | On supplier onboarding + annually | 3 years | Purchasing / QA | Major |
| Verification Testing Reports | Per schedule | 2–3 years | QA Lab | Major |

## 2.3 The Record-Keeping Equation — KTU High-Yield Conceptual Model

Although HACCP is not a numerical science, the **completeness of documentation** can be conceptualized as:

$$
\text{Documentation Effectiveness (DE)} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i \cdot C_i}{T_{total}}
$$

Where:
- $R_i$ = Number of records of type *i* maintained correctly
- $C_i$ = Criticality weight of record type *i* (1 = minor, 2 = major, 3 = critical)
- $T_{total}$ = Total required record types per the HACCP plan
- $n$ = Total categories of HACCP records (typically 6)

A **DE ≥ 0.95** is generally considered audit-ready under FSSC 22000 / BRCGS standards.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering/Industry Utility

In the food processing industry (which is the primary application context of HACCP), the **documentation framework directly supports**:
- **Plant design & commissioning** — equipment specification traceability
- **Process engineering** — flow diagrams become the basis for P&ID alignment
- **Automation & Industry 4.0** — digital HACCP records integrate with SCADA/MES systems
- **Cold chain logistics** — temperature records enable predictive analytics
- **Regulatory submissions** — FSSAI product approval requires HACCP documentation
- **Export compliance** — US FDA, EU EFSA, and Codex require traceable HACCP records
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation: How to Build HACCP Documentation

The **Six-Stage Documentation Architecture** is derived from Codex Principles 1 through 7 plus the Preliminary Tasks. Below is the complete step-by-step build:

### **Stage 1 — Assemble the HACCP Team**

The team must be **multidisciplinary** and include:
- HACCP Coordinator / Food Safety Manager
- Production / Operations Manager
- Quality Assurance / Quality Control
- Maintenance / Engineering
- Sanitation / Housekeeping
- Purchasing / Supplier Interface
- R&D / Product Development
- (Optional) Microbiologist, External Consultant

**Documentation Output:** HACCP Team Charter (names, roles, responsibilities, signatures).

### **Stage 2 — Describe the Product & Its Intended Use**

**Documentation Output:** Product Description Sheet containing:
- Product name and code
- Composition (ingredients with percentages)
- Physical/chemical properties (pH, $a_w$, moisture)
- Intended consumer (general public, infants, immunocompromised)
- Packaging format and material
- Shelf life and storage condition
- Distribution method (ambient, chilled, frozen)
- Labeling instructions (allergen, cooking instructions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marker:** The product description directly determines which hazards are considered — this is why it is part of the *Preliminary Tasks* (not Principle 1).

### **Stage 3 — Construct & Verify the Process Flow Diagram**

The flow diagram must cover **every step** from raw material receipt to consumer use.

**Block Diagram (Generic Food Process):**

$$
\text{Receiving} \rightarrow \text{Storage} \rightarrow \text{Prep} \rightarrow \text{Mixing} \rightarrow \text{Heat Process} \rightarrow \text{Cooling} \rightarrow \text{Packaging} \rightarrow \text{Storage} \rightarrow \text{Distribution}
$$

**Verification Step:** The HACCP team must perform an **on-site walk-through** of the actual process during operating hours and sign a "Flow Diagram Accuracy Confirmation" document.

### **Stage 4 — List All Potential Hazards (Principle 1)**

The **Hazard Analysis Worksheet** is a structured table with the following columns:

| Process Step | Hazard Type | Hazard Description | Source | Severity | Likelihood | Risk Score | Control Measure | Is Step a CCP? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

**Hazard Categories:**
- **Biological (B):** *Salmonella spp.*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *E. coli* O157:H7, *Clostridium botulinum*, *Staphylococcus aureus* enterotoxin, parasites, viruses
- **Chemical (C):** Pesticide residues, antibiotics, allergens (peanut, gluten, milk), heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg), cleaning chemical residues, mycotoxins
- **Physical (P):** Metal fragments, glass, bone, stone, plastic, hair, insect fragments

**Risk Score Calculation:**

$$
\text{Risk Score (R)} = \text{Severity (S)} \times \text{Likelihood (L)}
$$

Where $S \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ (Low, Medium, High) and $L \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ (Low, Medium, High).

**Decision Rule:**
- $R \geq 6$ → **Significant hazard** → Address via PRP or CCP
- $R = 3$ or $4$ → **Moderate hazard** → Address via PRP/SOP
- $R \leq 2$ → **Low hazard** → Document and monitor

### **Stage 5 — Apply the CCP Decision Tree (Principle 2)**

The **Codex CCP Decision Tree** is a logical flow used to determine whether a step is a CCP:

**Step-by-step CCP Tree Logic:**

$$
\text{Q1: Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard?}
$$

- **No** → Modify process, change, or stop → Is the step still a food safety concern? (Answer depends on case)
- **Yes** → Move to **Q2**

$$
\text{Q2: Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the likely occurrence of the hazard to an acceptable level?}
$$

- **Yes** → **This step is a CCP** (stop here)
- **No** → Move to **Q3**

$$
\text{Q3: Could contamination with the identified hazard(s) occur in excess of acceptable level(s), or could it increase to unacceptable level(s)?}
$$

- **No** → **Not a CCP** (proceed to next step)
- **Yes** → Move to **Q4**

$$
\text{Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard to an acceptable level?}
$$

- **No** → **This step is a CCP** (stop here)
- **Yes** → **Not a CCP** (the downstream step is the CCP)

**Documentation Output:** Decision Tree Worksheet with **Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4** answers signed for each potential CCP candidate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Many students incorrectly assume that *every* significant hazard step is a CCP. In reality, the decision tree often reveals that **only 2–5 steps** in a complex flow diagram qualify as CCPs. Documenting the *non-CCP* decisions is equally important to demonstrate the system was properly thought through.

### **Stage 6 — Establish Critical Limits (Principle 3)**

A **Critical Limit (CL)** is a measurable value that separates **acceptable** from **unacceptable** product safety.

**Examples of Critical Limits:**

| Process | Hazard | Critical Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Pasteurization of milk | *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, *Coxiella burnetii* | **72 °C for 15 seconds** |
| Canning (low-acid) | *Clostridium botulinum* | **121 °C for 3 min**, F₀ ≥ 3 |
| Acidified food | Pathogen survival | **pH ≤ 4.6** |
| Hot holding (kitchen) | Pathogen growth | **≥ 63 °C** |
| Cold holding | Pathogen growth | **≤ 4 °C** |
| Metal detection | Physical (metal) | Fe 2.5 mm, Non-Fe 3.0 mm, SS 3.5 mm |
| Refrigerated storage | Listeria growth | **≤ 4 °C throughout** |

**Documentation Output:** CCP Critical Limit Table with measurable parameters, regulatory basis, and scientific justification.

### **Stage 7 — Establish Monitoring Procedures (Principle 4)**

Each CCP must have a monitoring procedure that answers:
- **What** is monitored (parameter)
- **How** it is monitored (instrument/method)
- **When** it is monitored (frequency — continuous preferred)
- **Where** it is monitored (specific location)
- **Who** is responsible (named operator, not "QC department")

### **Stage 8 — Establish Corrective Actions (Principle 5)**

Predefined actions must exist for *every* possible CCP deviation. These must be **specific, actionable, and immediate**.

**Sample Corrective Action — Pasteurization CCP:**

> *If the pasteurization temperature drops below 72 °C for any reason:*
> 1. *Stop the line immediately.*
> 2. *Hold the affected product in a clearly marked quarantine area.*
> 3. *Verify the temperature and time deviation; evaluate against regulatory safe-time/temperature tables.*
> 4. *If product safety can be confirmed by re-pasteurization or downstream kill step → reprocess.*
> 5. *If safety cannot be confirmed → destroy product and document disposal.*
> 6. *Investigate root cause (e.g., equipment failure, sensor error, operator error).*
> 7. *Implement preventive action (e.g., backup sensor, retraining, equipment modification).*
> 8. *Sign-off by HACCP Team Leader.*

### **Stage 9 — Establish Verification Procedures (Principle 6)**

Verification must be **scheduled and documented**. Frequency varies:
- **Calibration:** monthly for thermometers, quarterly for pH meters
- **End-product testing:** weekly for pathogens on high-risk products
- **Internal audits:** annual minimum
- **HACCP plan review:** annual or upon any change (ingredient, process, equipment, supplier, regulatory)

### **Stage 10 — Establish Record-Keeping (Principle 7)**

The records are the *proof* that Principles 1–6 are operational. The retention principle (per Codex):

$$
\text{Retention Period} \geq \text{Shelf Life of Product} + 1 \text{ year}
$$

For products with shelf life > 2 years, retention may be aligned with corporate policy or regulatory minimums (typically 2–5 years).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Documentation by HACCP Principle

| Principle | Documentation Required | Audit Risk Level |
|---|---|---|
| **P1: Hazard Analysis** | Hazard Analysis Worksheet, Risk Assessment Matrix | Critical |
| **P2: CCP Determination** | CCP Decision Tree Worksheet | Critical |
| **P3: Critical Limits** | CL Table with scientific justification | Critical |
| **P4: Monitoring** | Monitoring SOPs + CCP Monitoring Logs | Critical |
| **P5: Corrective Actions** | Corrective Action SOP + CAR forms | Critical |
| **P6: Verification** | Calibration, Audit, Testing records | Major–Critical |
| **P7: Record Keeping** | Record Index, Retention Policy, Storage SOP | Major |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Risk Score Calculation

**Scenario:** A bakery produces bread. At the *post-baking cooling* step, a hazard analysis identifies **growth of *Bacillus cereus*** (a spore-former that survives baking) due to slow cooling.

**Step 1:** Identify hazard type → Biological
**Step 2:** Identify source → Spore survival through baking + germination during slow cooling
**Step 3:** Assign severity → $S = 3$ (High — *B. cereus* emetic toxin causes vomiting; severe in vulnerable populations)
**Step 4:** Assign likelihood → $L = 2$ (Medium — happens only when cooling is delayed > 4 hours)
**Step 5:** Calculate risk score:

$$
R = S \times L = 3 \times 2 = 6
$$

**Step 6:** Decision — $R \geq 6$ → **Significant hazard** → Apply CCP decision tree.

**Step 7:** Apply CCP tree:
- Q1: Are there preventive controls? → **Yes** (rapid cooling to 30 °C within 2 hours)
- Q2: Is this step designed to eliminate the hazard? → **No** (it controls growth, does not eliminate spores)
- Q3: Could contamination reach unacceptable levels? → **Yes** (if cooling delayed)
- Q4: Will a later step eliminate the hazard? → **No**
- **Result: Cooling step = CCP**

**Step 8:** Define Critical Limit → *"Cool product from 60 °C to 30 °C within 2 hours."*

**Step 9:** Monitoring → Continuous temperature probe with chart recorder; hourly manual check.

**Step 10:** Corrective Action → If cooling takes > 2 hours, hold product, investigate airflow, and reject if microbial testing fails.

**Documentation Set Generated:**
- Hazard Analysis Worksheet entry for *B. cereus* at cooling
- CCP Decision Tree for cooling step
- Critical Limit specification
- Monitoring SOP + Log
- Corrective Action SOP
- Verification: probe calibration records, weekly *B. cereus* testing

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Walk through a *real* product (milk, jam, ready-to-eat salad) end-to-end using this 10-step methodology. Examiners reward structured, principle-mapped answers.

## 4.5 CCP Decision Tree Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{Q1 - Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard?}
    Q1 -- No --> MODIFY[Modify process, change, or stop]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Q2 - Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> CCPYES1[STEP IS A CCP]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Q3 - Could contamination with the identified hazard occur in excess of acceptable levels?}
    Q3 -- No --> NOTCCP1[NOT a CCP - proceed to next step]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Q4 - Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard to an acceptable level?}
    Q4 -- No --> CCPYES2[STEP IS A CCP]
    Q4 -- Yes --> NOTCCP2[NOT a CCP - downstream step is the CCP]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Q1.a]**
> **Q1.** Define HACCP documentation. List any **four** types of records maintained in a HACCP system.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** HACCP documentation refers to the written set of records, procedures, worksheets, and plans that provide objective evidence of a functioning Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point (HACCP) system. It transforms the food safety control system into an auditable, traceable, and verifiable framework as per the **Codex Alimentarius (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)**.
>
> **Four types of records (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. CCP Monitoring Logs (real-time operational records)
> 2. Corrective Action Reports (on deviation from critical limit)
> 3. Calibration Records (for measuring instruments at CCPs)
> 4. Hazard Analysis Worksheet (foundation document for the HACCP plan)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Q2.b]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Validation** and **Verification** in the context of HACCP documentation. Give **one example** of each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Validation | Verification |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Question answered** | "Have we set the **right** controls?" | "Are we doing the controls **right**?" |
> | **When performed** | Initially, before implementation; on change | Routinely, ongoing |
> | **Example** | Scientific proof that pasteurization at **72 °C for 15 s** destroys *M. tuberculosis* (literature + challenge study) | Daily temperature chart check; monthly probe calibration; annual internal audit |
> | **Documentation** | Validation report with scientific evidence | Calibration log, audit report, testing report |
>
> *Awarding marks:* 1.5 for clear distinction in a table, 1 for the examples.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q7]**
>
> ### Question A (14 Marks)
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **HACCP Plan Document** and list its **eight essential components** as per Codex Alimentarius. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Describe the **CCP Decision Tree** and apply it to determine whether the **cooking step** in a ready-to-eat meat processing line is a CCP for the hazard of *Salmonella* survival. (7 marks)

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
> **(a)** Discuss the **six categories of HACCP documentation** with examples and explain the **retention period** rule for HACCP records. (7 marks)
> **(b)** A dairy plant pasteurizes milk at **72 °C for 15 seconds**. Construct the **complete documentation set** required for this CCP, including the **monitoring log format**, the **corrective action procedure**, and the **verification records**. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) HACCP Plan Document and its Eight Essential Components (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** The HACCP Plan is the **master written document** that captures the application of the Five Preliminary Tasks and Seven Principles to a specific product-process combination. It is the central evidence required for third-party audits under FSSC 22000, BRCGS, and SQF.

**Eight Essential Components (6 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Scope of the HACCP Plan** — Defines the product, its variants, process boundaries, and intended consumers.
2. **Product Description** — Composition, physical/chemical properties (pH, $a_w$), packaging, shelf life, storage conditions.
3. **Intended Use and Consumer** — General population, vulnerable groups (infants, elderly, immunocompromised, pregnant women).
4. **Process Flow Diagram** — Block diagram showing every step from raw material receipt to distribution; verified by on-site walk-through.
5. **Hazard Analysis Worksheet** — Lists biological, chemical, physical hazards; evaluates severity and likelihood; assigns risk score; identifies control measures.
6. **CCP Determination Record** — Outputs of the CCP Decision Tree for each significant hazard step.
7. **Critical Limits, Monitoring, Corrective Actions, and Verification** — The "operational core" of the plan for each identified CCP.
8. **Record-Keeping System** — List of all records, formats, responsibilities, retention periods, storage location, and review frequency.

#### (b) CCP Decision Tree + Application to Cooking Step for *Salmonella* (7 marks)

**Description of the CCP Decision Tree (3.5 marks):**

The CCP Decision Tree is a **logical questioning tool** developed by the Codex Alimentarius Commission to systematically determine whether a process step qualifies as a Critical Control Point. The tree consists of **four sequential questions (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4)** applied to each potential CCP candidate:

- **Q1:** Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard?
- **Q2:** Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the likely occurrence of the hazard to an acceptable level?
- **Q3:** Could contamination with the identified hazard(s) occur in excess of acceptable level(s), or could it increase to unacceptable level(s)?
- **Q4:** Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard to an acceptable level?

The decision tree is documented for **every significant hazard identified in the hazard analysis**, regardless of outcome, to demonstrate due diligence.

**Application to the Cooking Step — *Salmonella* (3.5 marks):**

**Hazard:** *Salmonella spp.* — biological hazard, high severity (foodborne illness with hospitalization risk in vulnerable groups).

**Applying the decision tree:**

- **Q1:** Do preventive control measures exist? → **Yes** — time/temperature control during cooking.
- **Q2:** Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce *Salmonella* to an acceptable level? → **Yes** — heat treatment is the **primary kill step** for *Salmonella* in meat.
- → **Result: The cooking step IS a CCP** (decision tree stops at Q2 with a "Yes").

**Documented HACCP Control Set:**

- **Critical Limit:** Internal meat temperature ≥ **70 °C held for at least 2 minutes** (or **75 °C instantaneous**), supported by USDA-FSIS and FSSAI thermal lethality tables.
- **Monitoring SOP:** Continuous temperature probe logging; manual reading every 30 minutes; verification with calibrated needle thermometer.
- **Corrective Action:** If temperature drops below 70 °C → hold product, extend cooking, or destroy; investigate equipment and operator compliance.
- **Verification:** Probe calibration monthly; *Salmonella* end-product testing weekly; annual cooking validation study.
- **Records:** Cooking temperature log, probe calibration log, *Salmonella* test report, corrective action reports.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) Six Categories of HACCP Documentation + Retention Rule (7 marks)

**Introduction (0.5 mark):** HACCP documentation, as codified by Codex and the NACMCF, is organized into **six functional categories**, each serving a distinct purpose in the food safety management system.

**The Six Categories (5 marks — 0.85 each):**

1. **Prerequisite Programs (PRPs):** Sanitation SOPs, personal hygiene, pest control, allergen management, supplier approval, calibration/maintenance, training.
2. **HACCP Plan Document:** The master document containing scope, product description, flow diagram, hazard analysis, CCP determination, critical limits, monitoring, corrective actions, verification, and record-keeping.
3. **Monitoring Records:** Real-time operational logs — CCP temperature, pH, metal detector checks, weight checks.
4. **Corrective Action Records:** Reports documenting the deviation, immediate action, root cause, and preventive action.
5. **Verification Records:** Calibration certificates, internal audit reports, end-product test reports, mock recall records, plan review minutes.
6. **Support Documentation:** HACCP team charter, training certificates, flow diagram verification, regulatory permits, customer specifications.

**Retention Period Rule (1.5 marks):** As per the **Codex Alimentarius (CAC/RCP 1-1969)**:

$$
\text{Retention Period} \geq \text{Shelf Life of the Product} + 1 \text{ year}
$$

**Worked Example:** For a pasteurized milk with **7-day refrigerated shelf life**, records must be retained for **7 days + 1 year ≈ 1 year and 1 week minimum**. For long-shelf-life canned products (2 years), records must be retained for **3 years minimum**. Many corporations and FSSC 22000-aligned systems set a **2–5 year corporate minimum** regardless of product shelf life to support export markets and post-market surveillance.

#### (b) Complete Documentation Set for Pasteurization CCP (7 marks)

**CCP Identification:** Pasteurization of milk at **72 °C for 15 s** (HTST — High Temperature Short Time) is a CCP for the biological hazards *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, *Coxiella burnetii*, *Salmonella spp.*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, and *E. coli*.

**1. Monitoring Log Format (2.5 marks):**

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| Date | DD/MM/YYYY |
| Shift | A / B / C |
| Product / Batch | "Full Cream Milk 1L" / Batch No. |
| Start Time | HH:MM |
| End Time | HH:MM |
| Set Temperature | **72 °C** |
| Actual Temperature (continuous) | Chart recorder trace (attach) |
| Actual Holding Time (continuous) | Chart recorder trace (attach) |
| Within Limits? | Yes / No (circle) |
| Operator Signature | Initials |
| Supervisor Verification | Name, signature, time |
| Corrective Action Reference (if any) | CAR No. |

**2. Corrective Action Procedure (2.5 marks):**

If the temperature drops below **72 °C** or the holding time falls below **15 s** at any point during the run:

1. **Stop the line** immediately and divert the affected milk to a **quarantine/holding tank**.
2. **Tag the product** with batch details, deviation time, and product quantity.
3. **Verify the deviation** using the chart recorder and a calibrated secondary thermometer.
4. **Decide disposition** based on FSSAI/FDA safe-time-temperature tables:
   - If total time above 65 °C ≥ the minimum regulatory threshold → product may be re-pasteurized.
   - Otherwise → destroy the product (e.g., divert to non-food use or disposal).
5. **Investigate root cause** (e.g., sensor failure, steam pressure drop, operator error, valve malfunction).
6. **Implement preventive action** (e.g., replace sensor, retrain operator, repair steam line).
7. **Document** in the Corrective Action Report with signatures from the operator, supervisor, and HACCP Team Leader.
8. **Release for sale** only after the preventive action is verified effective.

**3. Verification Records (2 marks):**

- **Daily:** Review of chart recorder trace by QA Officer (signature).
- **Weekly:** End-product microbiological testing for *Coliforms*, *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*.
- **Monthly:** Calibration of the Pasteurization Temperature Recorder (PTR) using a certified reference thermometer traceable to NABL/NPL standards.
- **Quarterly:** Calibration of the flow diversion device (FDD).
- **Annually:** Internal audit of the pasteurization process; review of all CCP records by the HACCP team; validation of HTST lethality using a challenge study (F₀ value ≥ 5-log *Coxiella* reduction).
- **Mock Recall:** Annual traceability exercise — must achieve **≥ 95% traceability within 4 hours** as per FSSC 22000.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating PRPs with HACCP:** PRPs are *not* CCPs. Writing "Pest Control = CCP" is a **major error**. CCPs must be derived through the decision tree.
> 2. **Skipping non-CCP documentation:** Documenting *why* a step is **not** a CCP is equally important to demonstrate a thought-through system.
> 3. **Vague critical limits:** "Cook thoroughly" is **not** a critical limit. Critical limits must be **measurable** (e.g., **72 °C for 15 s**, **pH ≤ 4.6**).
> 4. **Confusing Validation with Verification:** Validation is *once-off scientific proof*; verification is *routine confirmation*. Both are required and must be separately documented.
> 5. **Missing signatures/dates:** Every record must have a **date, time, operator signature, and supervisor verification** within 24 hours. Unsigned or back-dated records are treated as evidence of **falsification** during audits — an **automatic critical non-conformance**.
> 6. **Omitting retention logic:** Just stating "kept for 2 years" is insufficient. The *basis* (shelf life + 1 year minimum) must be stated.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "HACCP DOC-7"**
>
> ✅ **D — Documentation Hierarchy:** PRPs (foundation) → HACCP Plan (master) → Monitoring & Action Records (operational) → Verification Records (assurance).
>
> ✅ **O — Origin of Standards:** Codex Alimentarius (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020) and NACMCF are the international reference standards; FSSAI mandates it in India.
>
> ✅ **C — Codex's Three Preliminary Tasks:** (1) Assemble HACCP team, (2) Describe product, (3) Identify intended use — these precede the 7 Principles.
>
> ✅ **Seven Principles Recap:** P1 Hazard Analysis → P2 CCP Determination (Decision Tree) → P3 Establish Critical Limits → P4 Monitoring Procedures → P5 Corrective Actions → P6 Verification Activities → P7 Record-Keeping.
>
> ✅ **Six Document Categories:** PRPs, HACCP Plan, Monitoring Records, Corrective Action Records, Verification Records, Support Documentation.
>
> ✅ **Retention Period Formula:** Shelf life + 1 year (Codex minimum); corporate practice is 2–5 years.
>
> ✅ **Risk Score Equation:** $R = S \times L$ (both on 1–3 scale); $R \geq 6$ → Significant hazard → Apply CCP tree.
>
> ✅ **Decision Tree Endpoints:** Q2 "Yes" = CCP; Q4 "No" = CCP; otherwise = Not CCP. Always document the **Q1–Q4 answers** for traceability.
>
> ✅ **Critical Limits Must Be:** Measurable, scientifically justified, regulatory-aligned, and operationally achievable (e.g., **72 °C × 15 s**, **pH ≤ 4.6**, **Fe 2.5 mm** metal detectability).
>
> ✅ **Corrective Action vs. Preventive Action:** Corrective = fixes *this* deviation; Preventive = prevents *future* deviations. Both go in the CAR.
>
> ✅ **Validation vs. Verification:** Validation = "**Right** controls?" (initial); Verification = "Controls **right**?" (ongoing).
>
> ✅ **Record Discipline:** Real-time filling, operator signature, supervisor verification within 24 h, no back-dating, no pencil (use indelible ink), no correction fluid (strike through, initial, date).
>
> ✅ **The "No Documentation = No Proof" Rule:** A control that is not documented is considered **not implemented** in the eyes of an auditor.
>
> ✅ **Mock Recall Standard:** Traceability should be demonstrable for **≥ 95% of product** within **4 hours** (FSSC 22000 benchmark).
>
> ✅ **Audit Severity Ladder:** Minor < Major < **Critical** (e.g., missing CCP monitoring = Critical; missing calibration = Major; missing training record = Minor).
>
> ✅ **Mnemonic for Audit Defences:** **LATVI** = Legal, Audit, Trace, Verify, Improve — the five pillars of documentation value.
