This is precisely why **HACCP** is the gold-standard preventive system — it identifies chemical hazards *before* they reach the plate, rather than detecting them after consumption. ## 1.4 Standard Regulatory Metrics - **Maximum Residue Limit (MRL)** = **mg/kg** or **ppm (parts per million)** - **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** = **mg/kg body weight/day** - **Tolerable Weekly Intake (TWI)** = **µg/kg body weight/week** - **Benchmark Dose (BMD)** = **mg/kg/day** > [!IMPORTANT] > The key regulatory body for chemical hazard standards in India is the **Food Safety and Standards (Contaminants, Toxins and Residues) Regulations, 2011**, which specifies MRLs for over 250 chemicals. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Classification Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Naturally Occurring Chemical Hazards These are toxic compounds produced intrinsically by plants, animals, or microorganisms as part of their natural biochemistry. **Key Sub-Categories:** **(a) Mycotoxins** — Secondary metabolites of fungi (mainly *Aspergillus*, *Fusarium*, *Penicillium*): - **Aflatoxin B1** — Most potent natural hepatocarcinogen; regulated at **≤ 20 µg/kg** in food (FSSAI). Produced by *Aspergillus flavus* in improperly stored grains, groundnuts, maize. - **Ochratoxin A** — Nephrotoxic; produced in cereals, coffee, dried fruits. - **Fumonisins** — Carcinogenic; produced in maize by *Fusarium verticillioides*. - **Patulin** — Produced in damaged apples; regulated at **≤ 50 µg/L** in apple juice. **(b) Marine Biotoxins (Shellfish Poisoning Syndromes):** - **Paralytic Shellfish Poisoning (PSP)** — Saxitoxin; **regulatory limit = 0.8 mg/kg (80 µg/100g)** shellfish meat. - **Diarrheic Shellfish Poisoning (DSP)** — Okadaic acid. - **Amnesic Shellfish Poisoning (ASP)** — Domoic acid. - **Neurotoxic Shellfish Poisoning (NSP)** — Brevetoxins. **(c) Plant-Based Natural Toxins:** - **Cyanogenic Glycosides** — In cassava (linamarin), bitter almonds (amygdalin); release **HCN (Hydrogen Cyanide)** upon enzymatic hydrolysis. - **Solanine & Chaconine** — In green potatoes; glycoalkaloid content must be **< 200 mg/kg fresh weight**. - **Phytohaemagglutinin** — In raw red kidney beans; destroyed by boiling for **≥ 10 minutes at 100°C**. - **Goitrogens** — In cabbage, broccoli (thiocyanates); interfere with iodine uptake. - **Erucic Acid** — In mustard oil; **limit = 5%** of total fatty acids. **(d) Scombrotoxin (Histamine Poisoning):** - Formed by bacterial decarboxylation of histidine in scombroid fish (tuna, mackerel, mahi-mahi). - Regulatory limit: **Histamine ≤ 100 mg/kg** (FSSAI/CAC). - Causes allergic-like symptoms within **minutes to hours**. ### 2.1.2 Intentionally Added Chemicals (Food Additives) - **Preservatives** — Sodium benzoate, potassium sorbate. - **Antioxidants** — BHA, BHT. - **Colouring agents** — Tartrazine, Sunset Yellow FCF. - **Sweeteners** — Aspartame, Saccharin. - **Flavour enhancers** — MSG (Monosodium Glutamate). - **Emulsifiers, stabilizers, thickeners.** > [!NOTE] > In HACCP, additives become *hazards* when they are used **above the permitted level**, used in **unpermitted foods**, or when their label declaration is missing (especially for allergens like sulfites, which trigger anaphylaxis in sensitive individuals). ### 2.1.3 Agricultural and Veterinary Residues - **Pesticide Residues** — Organochlorines (DDT, endosulfan — now banned), organophosphates, carbamates, pyrethroids. - **Veterinary Drug Residues** — Antibiotics (penicillin, tetracycline, chloramphenicol — banned), anthelmintics, growth promoters. - **Hormone Residues** — Estradiol, testosterone (banned in India for meat-producing animals). - **FSSAI Standard** — MRLs under the **Insecticides Act, 1968** and **Prevention of Food Adulteration Rules**. ### 2.1.4 Environmental Contaminants - **Lead (Pb)** — From contaminated soil, lead-soldered pipes, ceramic glazes. MRL: **≤ 0.1 mg/kg** in cereals. - **Cadmium (Cd)** — Industrial pollution, phosphate fertilizers. MRL: **≤ 0.1 mg/kg** in cereals. - **Mercury (Hg)** — Bioaccumulation in fish; methylmercury is the toxic form. MRL: **≤ 0.5 mg/kg** in fish. - **Arsenic (As)** — In groundwater (Bengal basin crisis), rice. MRL: **≤ 1.0 mg/kg** in food grains. - **Dioxins & PCBs** — Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) under the **Stockholm Convention, 2001**. ### 2.1.5 Process-Induced Chemical Hazards These are formed during cooking, frying, baking, fermentation, or storage: - **Acrylamide** — Formed in Maillard reaction in starchy foods (potato chips, bread, coffee) above **120°C**. - **Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs)** — Formed during smoking, grilling, charcoal cooking (e.g., **Benzo[a]pyrene** in smoked meat). - **Nitrosamines** — From nitrite-curing of meats; N-nitrosodimethylamine (NDMA). - **Heterocyclic Amines (HCAs)** — In overcooked red meat. - **Trans-Fatty Acids (TFAs)** — From partial hydrogenation of oils; FSSAI limit: **≤ 2%** of total fat in oils. - **3-MCPD (3-Monochloropropane-1,2-diol)** — Formed in acid-hydrolyzed vegetable protein (acid-HVP) and soy sauce. ## 2.2 The "Why and How" of Chemical Hazard Control in HACCP - **Why control chemical hazards?** Unlike microbial hazards that can be destroyed by cooking, many chemical hazards are **heat-stable** (e.g., heavy metals, dioxins, pesticide residues). Once incorporated, they cannot be removed by conventional processing. - **How does HACCP control them?** Through **preventive control points (CCPs)** established during **Principle 1 (Hazard Analysis)**, with **critical limits (Principle 3)** set against regulatory MRLs, and **monitoring procedures (Principle 4)** such as supplier guarantees, certificates of analysis (CoA), and end-product laboratory testing. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet | Parameter / Formula | Definition | Unit / Symbol | Regulatory Standard | |----------------------|------------|---------------|---------------------| | $C_{residue} = \dfrac{m_{contaminant}}{m_{food}} \times 10^6$ | Residue Concentration | ppm = mg/kg | ≤ MRL | | $ADI = \dfrac{NOAEL}{SF}$ | Acceptable Daily Intake | mg/kg b.w./day | Per chemical | | $EDI = C \times \dfrac{F}{BW}$ | Estimated Daily Intake | mg/kg b.w./day | ≤ ADI | | $TMDI = \sum_i (MRL_i \times F_i)$ | Theoretical Max. Daily Intake | mg/day | ≤ ADI × BW | | $HI = \dfrac{EDI}{ADI}$ | Hazard Index | Dimensionless | < 1 = Safe | | $BMD_{10}$ | Benchmark Dose (10% response) | mg/kg/day | Risk assess. | | $MOE = \dfrac{BMDL}{EDI}$ | Margin of Exposure | Dimensionless | ≥ 10,000 (genotoxic) | | $\text{HI}_{combined} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{EDI_i}{ADI_i}$ | Cumulative HI (multiple chemicals) | Dimensionless | < 1 | | $AF = \dfrac{C_{measured}}{MRL}$ | Achievement Factor | Dimensionless | ≤ 1 = Compliant | | $TWI = ADI \times 7$ | Tolerable Weekly Intake | mg/kg b.w./week | Cumulative toxins | > [!NOTE] > **Key abbreviations** — NOAEL = No Observed Adverse Effect Level; SF = Safety Factor (typically 100); BW = Body Weight; F = Food consumption; i = chemical index. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Chemical hazard control in HACCP is critical in: - **Food Processing Industries** — Setting supplier specifications for raw materials. - **Water Treatment Plants** — Heavy metal removal via RO/ion exchange. - **Cold Chain Logistics** — Histamine control in tuna through temperature management (**≤ 4°C** throughout the chain). - **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing** — Cross-contamination of allergens (a *chemical* hazard class). - **Export Industry** — Compliance with **Codex MRLs** for international trade (EU has stricter standards than FSSAI). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Worked Derivations ### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Hazard Index (HI) for a Single Chemical **Scenario:** A 60 kg adult consumes 500 g/day of rice contaminated with lead. Laboratory analysis shows lead concentration = **0.2 mg/kg** in rice. **Step 1:** Compute the Estimated Daily Intake (EDI). $$ EDI = C \times \dfrac{F}{BW} $$ $$ EDI = 0.2 \;\text{mg/kg} \times \dfrac{0.5 \;\text{kg/day}}{60 \;\text{kg}} $$ $$ EDI = 0.2 \times 0.00833 = 0.001667 \;\text{mg/kg b.w./day} $$ **Step 2:** Apply the JECFA-defined ADI for lead (provisional tolerable daily intake, PTWI converted): **ADI = 0.0035 mg/kg b.w./day** (PTWI of 25 µg/kg b.w./week). **Step 3:** Compute the Hazard Index. $$ HI = \dfrac{EDI}{ADI} = \dfrac{0.001667}{0.0035} = 0.476 $$ **Step 4:** Interpretation. Since $HI = 0.476 < 1$, the daily rice consumption is within the safe exposure limit for the average 60 kg adult. However, if multiple foods contain lead (e.g., vegetables, water), the **cumulative HI** must be calculated. **Step 5:** Cumulative HI computation with two additional foods (vegetables 0.1 mg/kg at 300 g/day, water 0.01 mg/L at 2 L/day): $$ EDI_{veg} = 0.1 \times \dfrac{0.3}{60} = 0.0005 \;\text{mg/kg/day} $$ $$ EDI_{water} = 0.01 \times \dfrac{2}{60} = 0.000333 \;\text{mg/kg/day} $$ $$ HI_{cumulative} = \dfrac{0.001667 + 0.0005 + 0.000333}{0.0035} = \dfrac{0.0025}{0.0035} = 0.714 $$ Still below 1, but the margin is reduced — a typical KTU examiner expects you to note that **vulnerable populations (children, pregnant women) have lower thresholds**. ### 3.1.2 Derivation of Histamine Formation in Scombroid Fish **Scenario:** Fresh tuna stored at 25°C for 6 hours. Initial histamine = 0 mg/kg. Bacterial decarboxylation of histidine follows **first-order kinetics**. **Step 1:** First-order rate equation. $$ [H] = [H]_0 \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right) $$ where: - $[H]$ = Histamine concentration (mg/kg) - $[H]_0$ = Maximum potential histamine (mg/kg) from histidine pool - $k$ = Rate constant (h⁻¹) - $t$ = Time (h) **Step 2:** For *Morganella morganii* in tuna at 25°C: $k \approx 0.05$ h⁻¹, and the maximum histamine potential from histidine pools is approximately **2000 mg/kg** in tuna muscle. **Step 3:** Compute $[H]$ at $t = 6$ h. $$ [H] = 2000 \times \left(1 - e^{-0.05 \times 6}\right) $$ $$ [H] = 2000 \times \left(1 - e^{-0.3}\right) $$ $$ [H] = 2000 \times (1 - 0.7408) $$ $$ [H] = 2000 \times 0.2592 = 518.4 \;\text{mg/kg} $$ **Step 4:** Compliance check. Since $518.4 \;\text{mg/kg} \gg 100 \;\text{mg/kg}$ (FSSAI limit), the product is **NON-COMPLIANT** and unfit for human consumption. > [!IMPORTANT] > This is why in HACCP, time-temperature control is a **CCP** for scombroid fish — the critical limit is typically **storage temperature ≤ 4°C** and **time from catch to chilling ≤ 6 hours**. ### 3.1.3 Derivation of Acrylamide Formation (Maillard Reaction Kinetics) Acrylamide is formed from **asparagine + reducing sugars** at temperatures > 120°C: $$ \text{Asparagine} + \text{Reducing Sugar} \xrightarrow{\Delta > 120°C} \text{Acrylamide} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ The reaction follows **pseudo-first-order kinetics**: $$ \frac{d[AA]}{dt} = k_{eff} \cdot [Asn]^a \cdot [RS]^b $$ For a typical French fry at 180°C: $k_{eff} = 0.12$ min⁻¹ (activation energy $E_a \approx 85$ kJ/mol). **Quantification via Arrhenius relation:** $$ k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} $$ where: - $A$ = Pre-exponential factor (min⁻¹) - $E_a$ = Activation energy (J/mol) - $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $T$ = Absolute temperature (K) **Computational Example:** At $T = 180 + 273 = 453$ K, with $A = 2.4 \times 10^9$ min⁻¹: $$ k = 2.4 \times 10^9 \times e^{-85000 / (8.314 \times 453)} $$ $$ k = 2.4 \times 10^9 \times e^{-22.57} $$ $$ k = 2.4 \times 10^9 \times 1.49 \times 10^{-10} = 0.358 \;\text{min}^{-1} $$ A 5-minute fry yields: $$ [AA] = [AA]_{max} \left(1 - e^{-0.358 \times 5}\right) = [AA]_{max} \times (1 - 0.167) = 0.833 \times [AA]_{max} $$ This means **83.3% of maximum acrylamide** is formed in 5 minutes, supporting the HACCP critical limit of **frying time ≤ 4 minutes** at 180°C and a **final product acrylamide ≤ 500 µg/kg** (EU benchmark for French fries). ## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Chemical Hazard Identification in HACCP | Stage of Food Chain | Likely Chemical Hazard | CCP? | Critical Limit | Monitoring Method | |----------------------|------------------------|------|----------------|-------------------| | Primary Production (Farm) | Pesticide residues | Yes (CCP-1) | MRL ≤ value as per Codex | CoA, lab GC-MS | | Harvest | Aflatoxin in groundnut | Yes (CCP-1) | AFB1 ≤ 20 µg/kg | HPLC testing | | Receiving (Raw Milk) | Antibiotic residues | Yes (CCP-1) | MRL = 0 | Charm II test / Delvotest | | Storage (Grain) | Mycotoxin growth | Yes (CCP-2) | Moisture ≤ 14%, Temp ≤ 25°C | Moisture meter, sensor | | Processing (Smoking) | PAHs | Yes (CCP-1) | BaP ≤ 5 µg/kg | Lab HPLC | | Cleaning (CIP) | Cleaning agent residue | Yes (CCP-2) | Rinse water TDS ≤ 10 ppm | Conductivity probe | | Packaging (Allergens) | Undeclared allergens | Yes (CCP-1) | 100% label verification | Visual, metal detector | | Distribution (Tuna) | Histamine | Yes (CCP-1) | T ≤ 4°C, time ≤ 6h | Data logger | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: HACCP Flow for Chemical Hazard Control ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Material Supplier] --> B[Receiving Inspection
CoA Verification] B --> C{Chemical Test Pass?} C -- No --> D[Reject Lot
Quarantine] C -- Yes --> E[Storage
CCCP for Mycotoxins] E --> F[Pre-Processing
Cleaning and Sorting] F --> G[Processing Stage
Heat and pH Control] G --> H[CCP 1 Acrylamide Limit
CC 500 microgram per kg] G --> I[CCP 2 PAHs in Smoking
CC 5 microgram per kg] H --> J[Quality Lab Testing
HPLC and GC-MS] I --> J J --> K{Within MRL?} K -- No --> L[Corrective Action
Reformulation] K -- Yes --> M[Packaging
Allergen Verification] M --> N[Final Product Release
End-Product Testing] N --> O[Distribution
Temperature Data Logger] O --> P[Consumer Safety] D --> Q[Disposal Records] L --> Q ``` > [!NOTE] > In the diagram, **CC** denotes "Critical Limit" (in this diagram's context, please note this abbreviation is local to the diagram and not used elsewhere in standard KTU terminology). All critical limits in KTU assessments should be referred to as **Critical Limits (CLs)**. ## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Chemical Hazards ```mermaid graph TD CH[Chemical Hazards] --> NOH[Naturally Occurring] CH --> IAC[Intentionally Added] CH --> AVR[Agricultural and Veterinary Residues] CH --> ECN[Environmental Contaminants] CH --> PIC[Process Induced Chemicals] NOH --> MYC[Mycotoxins] NOH --> MBT[Marine Biotoxins] NOH --> PBT[Plant Based Toxins] NOH --> HIS[Histamine] IAC --> PRS[Preservatives] IAC --> ANT[Antioxidants] IAC --> CLR[Colouring Agents] IAC --> SWE[Sweeteners] IAC --> ALL[Undeclared Allergens] AVR --> PES[Pesticides] AVR --> VDR[Veterinary Drugs] AVR --> HOR[Hormones] ECN --> HVM[Heavy Metals] ECN --> DXN[Dioxins and PCBs] ECN --> RAD[Radionuclides] PIC --> ACR[Acrylamide] PIC --> PAH[PAHs] PIC --> NTR[Nitrosamines] PIC --> TFA[Trans Fatty Acids] PIC --> HCA[Heterocyclic Amines] PIC --> MCP[3-MCPD] ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow: Aflatoxin Control in Groundnut (HACCP Application) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Farm Selection] --> B[Pre-Harvest Sampling] B --> C{Irrigation and Pest
Management} C --> D[Harvest at Correct
Moisture 10 percent] D --> E[Sorting Stage 1
Visual and Electronic] E --> F[Drying Stage
Moisture below 8 percent] F --> G[Storage Stage
CCP Temp 25C Moisture 8 percent] G --> H[Pre-Despatch Sampling
HPLC Analysis] H --> I{Aflatoxin B1
20 microgram per kg} I -- Pass --> J[Approved for Export] I -- Fail --> K[Re-Sort or Reject] K --> L[Industrial Use Only] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define chemical hazard in food. List any four categories of chemical hazards with one example each.** **Model Answer:** A chemical hazard in food is the presence of any chemical agent at a level that may cause an adverse health effect to the consumer. As per Codex Alimentarius, chemical hazards are non-replicating and dose-dependent. Four categories: 1. **Naturally occurring** — Aflatoxin B1 in groundnut. 2. **Intentionally added** — Sodium benzoate as preservative. 3. **Agricultural/veterinary residues** — Chlorpyrifos pesticide residue. 4. **Process-induced** — Acrylamide in French fries. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI) and Maximum Residue Limit (MRL).** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | ADI | MRL | |-----------|-----|-----| | Full form | Acceptable Daily Intake | Maximum Residue Limit | | Definition | Amount of chemical that can be ingested daily over a lifetime without appreciable health risk | Maximum concentration of residue legally permitted in food | | Unit | mg/kg body weight/day | mg/kg (ppm) of food | | Scope | Toxicological basis | Regulatory/commercial basis | | Set by | JECFA/WHO | FSSAI/Codex/Government | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Classify chemical hazards in foods with a comprehensive diagram and discuss the **naturally occurring chemical hazards** in detail, with specific reference to mycotoxins, marine biotoxins, and cyanogenic glycosides. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of **MRL, ADI, and NOAEL** in the risk assessment of pesticide residues. A 50 kg adult consumes 250 g/day of wheat with pesticide concentration of 0.05 mg/kg. Calculate the **Hazard Index (HI)** if the ADI of the pesticide is **0.005 mg/kg body weight/day**. Interpret the result. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** **Classification of Chemical Hazards** (Already shown in §4.2). Students are expected to draw the classification tree. **Naturally Occurring Chemical Hazards — Detailed Discussion:** 1. **Mycotoxins** — Secondary fungal metabolites, produced mainly by *Aspergillus* (Aflatoxin B1, Ochratoxin), *Fusarium* (Fumonisins, Trichothecenes), and *Penicillium* (Patulin). Aflatoxin B1 is classified as **Group 1 carcinogen** by IARC. It targets the liver, causing hepatocellular carcinoma. Regulatory limit: **≤ 20 µg/kg** (FSSAI) and **≤ 5 µg/kg** for export to EU. Occurs in groundnut, maize, spices, dried figs. Control: pre-harvest field management, post-harvest moisture control (**≤ 14%**), sorting, detoxification (nixtamalization with lime). 2. **Marine Biotoxins** — Produced by dinoflagellates and concentrated in filter-feeding shellfish (mussels, oysters, clams, scallops) through bioaccumulation. Categories: - **PSP** (Saxitoxin) — neurotoxin, blocking sodium channels, limit **0.8 mg/kg**. - **DSP** (Okadaic acid) — inhibits protein phosphatases, causes diarrhea. - **ASP** (Domoic acid) — causes amnesia; neurotoxin. - **NSP** (Brevetoxins) — respiratory and neurotoxic. Control: biotoxin monitoring programs in shellfish harvesting areas, closures during harmful algal blooms (HABs), mouse bioassay, HPLC-FLD, ELISA. 3. **Cyanogenic Glycosides** — Examples: **linamarin** in cassava, **amygdalin** in bitter almonds/apricot kernels, **dhurrin** in sorghum. Upon tissue damage, the enzyme **β-glucosidase** hydrolyzes them to release **HCN**. Acute toxicity symptoms: respiratory failure, cardiac arrest. Lethal dose of HCN: **0.5–3.5 mg/kg b.w.** Control: traditional processing — grating, fermenting, sun-drying, boiling (4–5 days for cassava). #### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** **Definitions:** - **MRL (Maximum Residue Limit):** Highest concentration of pesticide residue legally permitted in food. Unit: mg/kg. - **ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake):** Daily exposure level over a lifetime that is considered safe. Unit: mg/kg body weight/day. - **NOAEL (No Observed Adverse Effect Level):** Highest dose at which no statistically significant adverse effect is observed in toxicology studies. **Calculation:** Given: $C = 0.05$ mg/kg, $F = 0.25$ kg/day, $BW = 50$ kg, $ADI = 0.005$ mg/kg/day. **Step 1:** Compute EDI. $$ EDI = C \times \dfrac{F}{BW} = 0.05 \times \dfrac{0.25}{50} $$ $$ EDI = 0.05 \times 0.005 = 0.00025 \;\text{mg/kg b.w./day} $$ **Step 2:** Compute HI. $$ HI = \dfrac{EDI}{ADI} = \dfrac{0.00025}{0.005} = 0.05 $$ **Step 3:** Interpretation. Since $HI = 0.05 \ll 1$, the consumer's exposure is **50 times below** the ADI. The wheat is considered **safe** for long-term consumption by a 50 kg adult under these conditions. The wheat sample is also compliant with the MRL (assuming MRL ≥ 0.05 mg/kg for this pesticide in wheat). However, cumulative exposure from multiple food sources should also be assessed. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part A(b):** Students commonly lose marks by (1) forgetting to divide food intake (250 g) by 1000 to convert to kg, (2) using wrong body weight, (3) failing to interpret the result qualitatively ("safe" vs "unsafe" with quantitative justification), and (4) omitting cumulative exposure analysis. Always state **units explicitly** and **interpret HI < 1 as safe, HI > 1 as unsafe**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Discuss the **process-induced chemical hazards** in foods. Explain with the chemical equations and conditions for the formation of **acrylamide** in starchy foods. Suggest HACCP-based control measures. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A seafood processing company receives tuna at 18°C. The histamine formation follows first-order kinetics with rate constant k = 0.05 h⁻¹ at 25°C and **activation energy Ea = 70 kJ/mol**. The current storage temperature is 25°C. The maximum histamine potential is 2000 mg/kg. The FSSAI histamine limit is **100 mg/kg**. Calculate the maximum safe time the tuna can be held at: - (i) 25°C - (ii) 4°C (using Arrhenius equation, with the same A) Recommend the HACCP critical limit for storage temperature and time. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** **Process-Induced Chemical Hazards** are formed during food processing (cooking, frying, smoking, baking, fermentation, irradiation). Key examples: - Acrylamide in starchy fried foods - Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs) in smoked/grilled meat - Nitrosamines in cured meats - Heterocyclic Amines (HCAs) in overcooked meat - Trans-fatty acids in hydrogenated oils - 3-MCPD in acid-hydrolyzed vegetable protein **Acrylamide Formation:** Acrylamide (CH₂=CH–CONH₂) is formed in the **Maillard reaction** between the amino acid **asparagine** and **reducing sugars** (glucose, fructose) at temperatures **> 120°C**, especially during frying, baking, and roasting. $$ \text{Asparagine} + \text{Reducing Sugar} \xrightarrow{\Delta > 120°C} \text{Schiff Base} \rightarrow \text{Acrylamide} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Conditions favoring formation:** - Temperature > 120°C (typically 140–180°C in frying) - Low moisture content (water activity < 0.6) - High asparagine content (potatoes, cereals, coffee) - Reducing sugar concentration (e.g., glucose in potatoes > 0.3%) **Health impact:** Acrylamide is a probable human carcinogen (IARC Group 2A) and neurotoxin. EU benchmark levels: French fries 500 µg/kg, biscuits 350 µg/kg, coffee 400 µg/kg. **HACCP Control Measures:** - Control of frying temperature (≤ 175°C) and time (≤ 4 minutes) as a **CCP**. - Use of raw materials with low reducing sugars (low-sugar potato varieties). - Blanching of potatoes to leach sugars. - Use of asparaginase enzyme pre-treatment. - End-product testing by LC-MS/MS. - Color control (avoid dark brown color > Maillard reaction completion). #### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** **Given:** - $k_{25} = 0.05$ h⁻¹ - $E_a = 70,000$ J/mol - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) - $T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298$ K - $T_2 = 4 + 273 = 277$ K - $[H]_{max} = 2000$ mg/kg - $[H]_{limit} = 100$ mg/kg **Part (i):** Time to reach 100 mg/kg at 25°C. Using first-order formation kinetics: $$ [H](t) = [H]_{max} \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right) $$ $$ 100 = 2000 \left(1 - e^{-0.05 t}\right) $$ $$ \dfrac{100}{2000} = 1 - e^{-0.05 t} $$ $$ 0.05 = 1 - e^{-0.05 t} $$ $$ e^{-0.05 t} = 0.95 $$ $$ -0.05 t = \ln(0.95) = -0.0513 $$ $$ t = \dfrac{0.0513}{0.05} = 1.026 \;\text{hours} \approx 61.6 \;\text{minutes} $$ **Result:** At 25°C, tuna must be consumed or chilled within **~1 hour** to stay under the FSSAI limit. **Part (ii):** Time at 4°C — use Arrhenius equation to find $k_4$. $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{k_4}{k_{25}}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right) $$ $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{k_4}{0.05}\right) = -\dfrac{70000}{8.314} \left(\dfrac{1}{277} - \dfrac{1}{298}\right) $$ $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{k_4}{0.05}\right) = -8420.3 \times (0.003610 - 0.003356) $$ $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{k_4}{0.05}\right) = -8420.3 \times 0.000254 = -2.139 $$ $$ \dfrac{k_4}{0.05} = e^{-2.139} = 0.1178 $$ $$ k_4 = 0.05 \times 0.1178 = 0.00589 \;\text{h}^{-1} $$ **Time at 4°C:** $$ t = \dfrac{\ln(0.95)}{-k_4} = \dfrac{-0.0513}{-0.00589} = 8.71 \;\text{hours} $$ **Result:** At 4°C, tuna can be stored for **~8.7 hours** before the histamine limit is breached. **HACCP Critical Limit Recommendation:** - **CL (Storage Temperature):** T ≤ 4°C - **CL (Time from Catch to Chilling):** ≤ 6 hours - **Monitoring:** Continuous data logger, visual temperature strip - **Corrective Action:** If T > 4°C for > 6 h, divert batch to pet food or destroy > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B(b):** Common errors include (1) using 273 instead of 298 in the Arrhenius calculation, (2) forgetting to convert temperatures to Kelvin, (3) mishandling the natural log sign, (4) failing to state HACPP CCPs (Critical Control Points) explicitly, and (5) not providing corrective actions. Always show all 4-5 steps explicitly with units. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Chemical hazards are non-replicating and dose-dependent** — unlike biological hazards. - **Five main categories** — Naturally Occurring, Intentionally Added, Agricultural/Veterinary, Environmental, Process-Induced. - **Key regulatory bodies** — Codex Alimentarius, FSSAI, EU Commission, JECFA, IARC, WHO. - **Critical formulas:** - $EDI = C \times F / BW$ - $HI = EDI / ADI$ (must be < 1) - $[H](t) = [H]_{max}(1 - e^{-kt})$ for histamine - $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ (Arrhenius) - **Most-regulated mycotoxins** — Aflatoxin B1 (≤ 20 µg/kg in food, ≤ 5 µg/kg export). - **Histamine** — FSSAI limit **100 mg/kg**; CCP for tuna/mackerel storage. - **Acrylamide** — Forms in starchy foods above 120°C; reduce by lowering frying T, time, and pre-blanching. - **Allergens** — Considered a *chemical* hazard in HACCP; undeclared allergens are leading cause of food recalls globally. - **Heavy metals** — Lead, Cadmium, Mercury, Arsenic — non-degradable, cumulative in body. - **Process-induced hazards** — Acrylamide, PAHs, Nitrosamines, HCAs, TFAs, 3-MCPD. - **HACCP role** — Identify chemical hazards in **Principle 1**, establish **CCPs** for raw material testing, supplier CoA, and time-temperature control, set **critical limits = MRLs**, and maintain **monitoring records** (Principle 4) and **corrective actions** (Principle 5). - **Always remember:** For KTU numericals, show the unit conversions (g → kg, °C → K) and the sign in Arrhenius equation explicitly to avoid 2-mark deductions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Quick-Fire Trivia (Frequently Asked in Viva):** > - Most potent hepatocarcinogen: **Aflatoxin B1** > - Most common allergen in undeclared food recalls: **Milk** > - Codex MRL for Aflatoxin B1 in peanuts: **15 µg/kg** (international) vs **20 µg/kg** (FSSAI) > - PAH marker: **Benzo[a]pyrene** (≤ 5 µg/kg in smoked meat) > - Acrylamide marker: Asparagine + reducing sugar > - Histamine: Time-temperature CCP at **≤ 4°C**, **≤ 100 mg/kg** limit

topics.title: Physical Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT868 - HACCP PLANNING AND IMPLEMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Physical Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Physical Hazard** in the context of food safety and the Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point (HACCP) system is defined as any extraneous physical foreign object or material not normally found in a food product that may cause illness, injury, or physical trauma (such as choking, cuts, dental damage, or internal perforation) to a consumer upon ingestion. The **Codex Alimentarius Commission** and the **U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA)** classify physical hazards as one of the three primary categories of food safety hazards, alongside **chemical hazards** and **biological hazards**.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEFTT868)** syllabus, physical hazards are formally defined as:

> *"Any physical agent or foreign material of a non-biological and non-chemical nature that is unintentionally introduced into a food product and has the potential to cause adverse health effects to the consumer."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Physical hazards are often the most *immediately detectable* hazard class because the consumer can frequently see, feel, or bite into the foreign object. However, they are not always preventable through heat treatment (unlike many biological hazards), making **prevention at source** and **detection at critical control points (CCPs)** the two primary HACCP control strategies.

### 1.2 Key Terminology in HACCP Context

| Term | KTU Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **Hazard** | A biological, chemical, or physical agent in, or condition of, food with the potential to cause an adverse health effect. |
| **Physical Hazard** | An extraneous solid object or material of non-food origin (or unintended food-origin material) that may cause injury. |
| **Foreign Body / Extraneous Matter** | The physical object itself that constitutes the hazard. |
| **Control Measure (PRP/OPRP/CCP)** | An action or activity that can be used to prevent, eliminate, or reduce a hazard to an acceptable level. |
| **Acceptable Level** | The threshold below which a hazard is considered to be at a tolerable risk — typically zero tolerance for hard/sharp physical hazards in ready-to-eat foods. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are at a beach preparing a fresh fruit salad. You collect ripe mangoes, pineapples, and watermelons. Now, imagine that during the cutting process, a small piece of the **stainless steel knife blade** chips off and falls into the bowl. Alternatively, picture a **small pebble** from the ground being kicked into the fruit basket, or a **strand of hair** from the chef falling in.

These are all **physical hazards** — they are not chemicals (like pesticides) and not biological agents (like bacteria or viruses), but **solid physical matter** that has no business being in the food.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Think of physical hazards as "uninvited guests" at a party. The food is the host, and the foreign object crashes the party. The host (food manufacturer) has two options: (1) **Stop the guest at the door** (prevention — metal detectors at the entrance), or (2) **Catch the guest inside** (detection — X-ray machines scanning the dance floor). HACCP helps you decide which doors (CCPs) to install these security measures at.

### 1.4 Why Physical Hazards Matter in HACCP

Physical hazards are particularly important in HACCP planning because:

1. **Acute vs. Chronic:** Unlike many chemical or biological hazards that manifest over time, physical hazards typically cause **acute, immediate injury**.
2. **Zero Tolerance Philosophy:** Regulatory bodies (FDA, FSSAI, EFSA) generally enforce a **zero-tolerance** policy for hard, sharp physical hazards above a defined size threshold (commonly **> 7 mm** for hard/sharp objects in ready-to-eat foods, per FDA *Health Hazard Evaluation Board*).
3. **High Consumer Complaint Trigger:** Physical hazards are the **leading cause** of consumer complaints to food safety authorities, often resulting in expensive **product recalls**, **litigation**, and **brand reputation damage**.
4. **Detection Reliance:** Many physical hazards cannot be destroyed by cooking, freezing, or chemical treatment — they must be **physically detected and removed**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Physical Hazards

Physical hazards are systematically classified into **three primary categories** based on their origin, nature, and risk profile:

#### 2.1.1 Extraneous Matter (Non-Food Origin)
Foreign materials that originate from sources **outside** the food itself.

**Common Examples:**
- **Glass fragments** — from bottles, jars, light bulbs, glassware, or thermometer breakage.
- **Metal fragments** — from machinery wear (e.g., sieves, blades, conveyor belts, staples, wire, nuts, bolts).
- **Stone, pebbles, and soil** — from harvesting (root crops like potatoes and carrots).
- **Plastic pieces** — from packaging, gloves, conveyor belts, plastic containers.
- **Wood splinters** — from wooden pallets, crates, or construction materials.
- **Personal effects** — jewellery, hair, fingernails, Band-Aids, pen caps.
- **Insect parts** — whole insects, fly fragments (sometimes classified under biological, but often under physical as foreign matter).

#### 2.1.2 Naturally Occurring Physical Hazards (Food-Origin)
Hazards that are **part of the raw food material** itself.

**Common Examples:**
- **Bones** in fish (especially boneless fish fillets) and meat products.
- **Shells and shell fragments** in shellfish (mussels, clams, oysters).
- **Pits/stones** in fruits (cherry, olive, peach, mango).
- **Seeds** in certain berries.
- **Fish spines** in processed seafood.

#### 2.1.3 Process-Introduced Physical Hazards
Hazards introduced **during manufacturing or handling**.

**Common Examples:**
- **Lubricant droplets** from machinery.
- **Cleaning chemical residues** (technically chemical, but if solid, can be physical).
- **Paint chips** from walls or equipment.
- **Rust particles** from corroded equipment.
- **Welding slag** from maintenance work.
- **Glove fragments** from damaged rubber gloves.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How a Physical Hazard Enters the Food Chain

The entry pathway of a physical hazard follows a predictable sequence:

**Step 1: Source Identification**
Every physical hazard has an **origin point**. HACCP requires identifying *where* the hazard could be introduced — this is called the **hazard source analysis**.

**Step 2: Contamination Route**
The hazard travels along the **process flow** — from raw material reception → storage → processing → packaging → distribution → consumer.

**Step 3: Survival Mechanism**
Unlike microbial hazards, physical hazards **do not multiply or die**. They are introduced **once** and remain throughout the product's shelf life unless removed.

**Step 4: Consumer Exposure**
The hazard reaches the consumer via ingestion, causing **acute physical trauma**.

### 2.3 Severity and Probability Matrix (HACCP Risk Assessment)

The HACCP team must assess each physical hazard based on two parameters:

- **Severity (S):** The potential health consequence if the hazard is not controlled.
  - **Catastrophic (S = 4):** Death or permanent disability (e.g., swallowing a sharp metal fragment causing internal perforation).
  - **Critical (S = 3):** Serious injury requiring medical intervention (e.g., broken tooth from a stone).
  - **Moderate (S = 2):** Minor injury, transient discomfort.
  - **Negligible (S = 1):** No injury, customer dissatisfaction only.

- **Probability (P):** The likelihood of occurrence.
  - **Frequent (P = 4):** Happens regularly without intervention.
  - **Occasional (P = 3):** Happens sometimes.
  - **Infrequent (P = 2):** Happens rarely.
  - **Improbable (P = 1):** Very unlikely.

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is calculated as:

$$RPN = Severity \, (S) \times Probability \, (P)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Note:** The RPN formula is **not unique to food safety**; it was originally a Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) concept adapted into HACCP. For exam purposes, remember that an RPN value above a certain threshold (typically **8 or higher** in a 1–4 scale, or **50+ in a 1–10 scale**) requires the hazard to be addressed by a **Critical Control Point (CCP)**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Threshold | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Risk Priority Number | $RPN = S \times P$ | Dimensionless; threshold: CCP required if $RPN \geq 8$ (4-point scale) |
| Sieve Aperture Reference | $d_{sieve} \leq 0.5 \times d_{hazard}$ | Sieve mesh size must be smaller than the hazard to retain it |
| Detection Sensitivity | $D_{min} = 0.5 \, mm$ (metal), $D_{min} = 2.0 \, mm$ (glass), $D_{min} = 1.0 \, mm$ (stone) | Minimum detectable size for X-ray / metal detection systems |
| Metal Detector Frequency Range | $f = 25 \, kHz$ to $1 \, MHz$ | Typical operating range for industrial food metal detectors |
| FDA Hazard Size Threshold | $L_{critical} \geq 7 \, mm$ | Length threshold for hard/sharp physical hazards in RTE foods |
| Sieve Conversion | $\text{Mesh} \# = \frac{25.4 \, mm}{d_{sieve}}$ | Standard ASTM E11 sieve designation |
| Throughput vs. Detection Limit Trade-off | $v_{belt} = \frac{Q_{max}}{A_{detector}}$ | Higher belt speed reduces detection sensitivity |
| Ballistic Trajectory (Gravity) | $v_{y} = v_{0} \sin(\theta) - g \cdot t$ | Used in optical sorting ejection trajectory calculation |
| Air Pressure for Rejection | $P_{rejection} = \frac{F_{drag}}{A_{nozzle}}$ | For pneumatic sorters; P typically 2–6 bar |
| Conveyor Belt Speed | $v_{belt} \leq 0.5 \, m/s$ | Maximum recommended for reliable X-ray detection |

### 2.5 Control Measures (PRPs, OPRPs, and CCPs)

Control measures for physical hazards in HACCP are organized into a **hierarchy of controls**:

**Level 1: Prerequisite Programs (PRPs) — Foundational Controls**
- **Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP)** — Sanitary facility design, equipment maintenance.
- **Glass and Brittle Plastics Policy** — Glass register, replacement of glass with shatter-proof materials.
- **Preventive Maintenance Schedules** — Regular inspection of equipment for wear.
- **Pest Control Programs** — Screens, traps, and inspection.
- **Foreign Body Policy** — Prohibition of jewellery, personal items in production areas.
- **Supplier Approval and Inspection of Incoming Materials.**

**Level 2: Operational Prerequisite Programs (OPRPs) — Process-Specific Controls**
- **Sieve and Filter Installation** — Inline sieves to trap larger objects.
- **Magnets and Magnetic Separators** — For ferrous metal removal.
- **Optical Sorting** — Camera-based color/shape sorting.
- **Dedetection Equipment Maintenance** — Calibration schedule.

**Level 3: Critical Control Points (CCPs) — The Final Defence**
- **Metal Detectors** (ferrous, non-ferrous, stainless steel).
- **X-Ray Inspection Systems.**
- **Visual Inspection Stations** (manual or automated).
- **Filtration / Sieving at Critical Process Steps.**

### 2.6 Detection Technology Comparison

| Technology | Detects | Misses | Sensitivity | Cost | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Metal Detector** | Ferrous, non-ferrous, stainless steel | Glass, stone, plastic, bone | 0.5–2.0 mm metal | Medium | Most packaged foods |
| **X-Ray Inspection** | Metal, glass, stone, bone, dense plastic | Low-density plastics, wood, hair | 0.3–1.0 mm | High | Glass jars, metalized films |
| **Optical / Camera Sorter** | Color defects, surface contamination, some plastics | Hidden internal objects, transparent glass | 0.5 mm surface | Medium-High | Fruits, vegetables, grains |
| **Ultrasonic** | Foreign bodies in liquids, soft products | Air pockets, small dense objects | 1.0 mm | High | Liquids, pastes |
| **Magnetic Separator** | Ferrous metals only | Non-ferrous metals, other materials | Very high for ferrous | Low | Dry powders, grains |
| **Sieve / Filter** | Anything larger than aperture | Anything smaller than aperture | Depends on mesh | Low | Granular products |
| **Gravity Separator / Destoner** | Stones, glass, metal in granular products | Light plastic, organic matter | 1–2 mm | Medium | Grains, legumes, seeds |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a real food processing plant, the HACCP team for physical hazards performs the following:

1. **Constructs a Process Flow Diagram** for every product line.
2. **Identifies all potential physical hazard sources** at each step.
3. **Applies a Decision Tree** (Codex Decision Tree) to determine if the step is a **CCP**.
4. **Sets Critical Limits** (e.g., "no metal fragment ≥ 1.5 mm and ≥ 0.5 mm diameter shall pass the metal detector").
5. **Establishes Monitoring Procedures** (e.g., metal detector test with a ferrous, non-ferrous, and stainless steel test piece every hour).
6. **Defines Corrective Actions** (e.g., "reject last 30 minutes of production, hold and re-inspect").
7. **Implements Verification Activities** (e.g., "quarterly validation of metal detector sensitivity by third-party lab").
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Risk Priority Number (RPN) Threshold Justification

The RPN is the cornerstone of HACCP risk assessment. The mathematical derivation of its application threshold follows:

**Given:**
- A physical hazard (e.g., a metal fragment from a hammer mill).
- Severity classification: **S = 4 (Catastrophic)** — can cause internal perforation.
- Probability classification: **P = 3 (Occasional)** — happens 2–3 times per year without controls.

**Step 1: Compute the Risk Priority Number.**

$$RPN = S \times P = 4 \times 3 = 12$$

**Step 2: Compare to the decision threshold.**

The KTU / Codex threshold for classifying a hazard as requiring a **CCP** is typically $RPN \geq 8$ (on a 1–4 scale).

**Step 3: Make the decision.**

$$RPN_{calculated} = 12 \geq 8 = RPN_{threshold}$$

Since the calculated RPN exceeds the threshold, the metal fragment hazard **must be controlled by a CCP** — typically a metal detector at the end of the production line.

**Step 4: Document the decision.**

The HACCP team records: *"Metal fragment hazard from hammer mill identified as CCP-3 (Metal Detection) with critical limit: rejection of product containing Fe ≥ 1.5 mm, Non-Fe ≥ 2.0 mm, SS ≥ 2.5 mm."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always show the **S × P table** before computing RPN. Examiners award partial marks for correct identification of severity and probability, even if the final multiplication is incorrect.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sieve Aperture Selection Logic

**Scenario:** A spice manufacturer processes turmeric powder. The smallest hard physical hazard of concern is a **0.8 mm stone fragment** (above which is considered "critical size" by the company's hazard policy). The plant manager wants to install a sieve.

**Given:**
- Critical hazard size: $d_{hazard} = 0.8 \, mm$
- Safety factor recommendation: sieve aperture should be **at most 50%** of the hazard size to ensure retention.

**Step 1: Apply the safety factor formula.**

$$d_{sieve} \leq 0.5 \times d_{hazard}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the value.**

$$d_{sieve} \leq 0.5 \times 0.8 \, mm$$

$$d_{sieve} \leq 0.4 \, mm$$

**Step 3: Convert to ASTM mesh number.**

$$\text{Mesh} \# = \frac{25.4 \, mm}{d_{sieve}} = \frac{25.4 \, mm}{0.4 \, mm}$$

$$\text{Mesh} \# = 63.5$$

**Step 4: Select the nearest standard sieve.**

Since ASTM E11 standard sieves come in fixed grades, the manufacturer would select a **Mesh #60** (aperture ≈ 0.250 mm) or **Mesh #70** (aperture ≈ 0.212 mm) for additional safety margin. A **Mesh #40** (aperture = 0.425 mm) would be the closest match for the calculated 0.4 mm requirement.

**Step 5: Final selection.** 

The plant selects a **Mesh #60 sieve** because:
- It is finer than required (0.250 mm < 0.400 mm), giving an extra safety margin.
- It is a standard, commercially available sieve.
- It balances retention efficiency with acceptable pressure drop across the sieve.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Metal Detector Sensitivity Validation

**Scenario:** A snack food plant (extruded corn puffs) installs a metal detector rated to detect **1.0 mm ferrous, 1.5 mm non-ferrous, and 2.0 mm stainless steel** test pieces. The HACCP plan requires validation that the detector meets this rating.

**Step 1: Prepare the test pieces.**

Three standard test spheres are obtained from a certified supplier:
- Ferrous (Fe) test sphere: $d_{Fe} = 1.0 \, mm$
- Non-ferrous (Non-Fe, e.g., aluminum) test sphere: $d_{Non-Fe} = 1.5 \, mm$
- Stainless steel (SS 316) test sphere: $d_{SS} = 2.0 \, mm$

**Step 2: Run the test pieces through the detector.**

Each test sphere is passed through the detector **20 times** in different positions (centre, left edge, right edge of the conveyor belt).

**Step 3: Compute detection efficiency.**

$$E_{detection} = \frac{N_{detected}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$

For ferrous:
$$E_{Fe} = \frac{20}{20} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

For non-ferrous:
$$E_{Non-Fe} = \frac{19}{20} \times 100\% = 95\%$$

For stainless steel:
$$E_{SS} = \frac{18}{20} \times 100\% = 90\%$$

**Step 4: Accept/Reject decision.**

The HACCP plan requires $E_{detection} \geq 95\%$. The SS result (90%) **fails** validation. The plant must:
- Adjust the detector sensitivity.
- Reduce the conveyor belt speed to allow better detection.
- Re-validate until $E_{SS} \geq 95\%$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Physical Hazards and Their HACCP CCPs

| Product Type | Primary Physical Hazard | Source | Control Measure | Critical Limit | Monitoring Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bottled beverages** | Glass fragments | Glass bottle breakage | Visual inspection, X-ray | Zero glass > 2 mm | Continuous (X-ray inline) |
| **Canned vegetables** | Metal fragments (can lid) | Can seamer malfunction | Visual inspection, double seaming | No metal > 0.5 mm | Hourly visual check |
| **Baked goods** | Stone, plastic, metal | Flour, ingredient contamination | Sieving, metal detection | Sieve: Mesh #60; MD: 1.5 mm Fe/2.0 mm Non-Fe/2.5 mm SS | Continuous |
| **Fish fillets** | Bones | Natural occurrence | X-ray, manual pin-boning | Zero bones > 5 mm | Continuous (X-ray) + spot manual |
| **Ground spices** | Stones, soil, metal | Raw agricultural material | Gravity separator, destoner, metal detector | Stones > 2 mm rejected; MD as above | Continuous |
| **Pickled vegetables** | Glass (jar), brine solids | Jar breakage | X-ray, visual | Zero glass | Continuous |
| **Pasta** | Metal fragments from dies | Extruder die wear | Metal detector, sieve | MD: 1.0 mm Fe/2.0 mm Non-Fe/2.5 mm SS | Continuous + hourly test |
| **Dairy products** | Metal, glass | Equipment wear, glass thermometers | Metal detector, glass policy | As above | Continuous |
| **Frozen vegetables** | Stones, wood, metal | Field contamination | Optical sorter, gravity separator, X-ray | Stones > 3 mm rejected | Continuous |
| **Honey** | Bee parts, wax, metal | Extraction process | Filtration, settling, metal detector | Wax < 1 mm, zero metal | Batch filtration |

### 3.5 Decision Tree Application: Is This Step a CCP?

The **Codex Alimentarius Decision Tree** is applied at each process step where a physical hazard is identified:

**Question 1:** Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard?
- If **YES** → go to Question 2.
- If **NO** → the process step must be redesigned, or the product must be modified.

**Question 2:** Does this step specifically eliminate or reduce the likely occurrence of the hazard to an acceptable level?
- If **YES** → **this step is a CCP** (go to monitoring section).
- If **NO** → go to Question 3.

**Question 3:** Could contamination with the identified hazard(s) occur in excess of acceptable level(s), or could it increase to unacceptable level(s)?
- If **YES** → go to Question 4.
- If **NO** → **not a CCP** (control is sufficient).

**Question 4:** Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard to an acceptable level?
- If **YES** → **not a CCP** (downstream control sufficient).
- If **NO** → **this step is a CCP**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The Decision Tree has **two CCP endpoints**: Q2 (for hazard elimination steps) and Q4 (for the last effective control step). Many students confuse these — remember that **Q2 CCPs are "kill" steps** (e.g., a metal detector that physically removes contamination) and **Q4 CCPs are "last-line" defences**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Physical Hazard Entry Points in a Food Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Material Reception] --> A2[Storage and Warehousing]
    A2 --> A3[Sorting and Cleaning]
    A3 --> A4[Processing and Cutting]
    A4 --> A5[Heat Treatment / Cooking]
    A5 --> A6[Packaging Line]
    A6 --> A7[Metal Detection / X-Ray]
    A7 --> A8[Distribution and Retail]
    A8 --> A9[Consumer]

    H1[Hazard Stones, Soil] -.-> A1
    H2[Hazard Bone, Shell, Pit] -.-> A3
    H3[Hazard Metal from Equipment] -.-> A4
    H4[Hazard Glass from Lamps] -.-> A5
    H5[Hazard Metal from Seamer] -.-> A6
    H6[Hazard Plastic, Hair, Jewellery] -.-> A6
    H7[Hazard Detection Limit Breach] -.-> A7

    A1 --> CCP1[CCP1 Visual Inspection of Incoming Materials]
    A4 --> CCP2[CCP2 Sieve and Magnetic Trap]
    A6 --> CCP3[CCP3 Metal Detector Calibration]
    A7 --> CCP4[CCP4 X-Ray Inspection Final Check]

    CCP1 -->|Reject| REJ1[Reject Nonconforming Batch]
    CCP2 -->|Reject| REJ1
    CCP3 -->|Reject| REJ1
    CCP4 -->|Reject| REJ1
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Multi-Layer Physical Hazard Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[Layer 1 Prerequisite Programs PRP]
        P1[Glass and Brittle Plastic Policy]
        P2[Preventive Equipment Maintenance]
        P3[Personal Hygiene and Foreign Body Policy]
        P4[Supplier Approval Program]
    end

    subgraph L2[Layer 2 Operational Prerequisite Programs OPRP]
        O1[Inline Sieves and Filters]
        O2[Magnetic Separators]
        O3[Gravity Separators and Destoners]
        O4[Optical Sorting]
    end

    subgraph L3[Layer 3 Critical Control Points CCP]
        C1[Metal Detector]
        C2[X-Ray Inspection]
        C3[Final Visual Inspection]
    end

    subgraph L4[Layer 4 Verification and Validation]
        V1[Daily Test Piece Validation]
        V2[Quarterly Third Party Calibration]
        V3[Annual HACCP Audit]
    end

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 -->|Feedback Loop| L1

    L3 --> PROD[Safe Finished Product]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow with Physical Hazard Decision Tree (Codex)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Process Step with Physical Hazard] --> Q1{Q1 Do preventive control measures exist}
    Q1 -->|No| ACT1[Redesign Process or Modify Product]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Q2 Does this step eliminate or reduce hazard to acceptable level}
    Q2 -->|Yes| CCP1[This Step is a CCP Kill Step]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Q3 Could contamination occur above acceptable level}
    Q3 -->|No| NOCP[Not a CCP]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Q4 Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce hazard}
    Q4 -->|Yes| NOCP2[Not a CCP Downstream Control Exists]
    Q4 -->|No| CCP2[This Step is a CCP Last Line Defence]
    CCP1 --> END[Document CCP Critical Limits and Monitoring]
    CCP2 --> END
    NOCP --> END2[Document as OPRP or PRP]
    NOCP2 --> END2
```

### 4.4 Severity Probability Risk Matrix (2D Visualisation)

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Risk Priority Number RPN Matrix for Physical Hazards
    x-axis "Low Probability" --> "High Probability"
    y-axis "Low Severity" --> "High Severity"
    quadrant-1 "CCPs Required"
    quadrant-2 "OPRPs and PRPs"
    quadrant-3 "Monitor and Review"
    quadrant-4 "CCPs and High Vigilance"
    "Metal Fragment from Hammer Mill": [0.75, 0.95]
    "Glass from Light Bulb": [0.65, 0.90]
    "Stone in Spinach": [0.55, 0.70]
    "Bone in Fish Fillet": [0.45, 0.60]
    "Hair in Pasta": [0.40, 0.25]
    "Plastic Glove Fragment": [0.35, 0.55]
    "Jewellery Piece": [0.20, 0.80]
    "Paint Chip": [0.30, 0.50]
    "Wood Splinter": [0.25, 0.45]
    "Insect Part": [0.60, 0.35]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Quadrant Chart:** The top-right quadrant (high severity × high probability) requires **CCPs**. The top-left and bottom-right are **OPRPs/PRPs**. The bottom-left is **monitored but lower priority**.

### 4.5 Schematic: Metal Detector Operating Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROD[Food Product on Conveyor] --> COIL1[Transmitter Coil Generates Magnetic Field]
    COIL1 --> COIL2[Receiver Coils Balanced Bridge]
    COIL2 -->|Product Clean| SIG1[Balanced Signal No Detection]
    COIL2 -->|Metal Present| SIG2[Disturbed Signal Alarm Triggered]
    SIG2 --> REJ[Pneumatic Rejector Activated]
    REJ --> BIN[Reject Bin Quarantine Area]
    SIG1 --> OK[Product Continues to Packaging]
    OK --> NEXT[Next Process Step]
    BIN -->|Investigation| ROOT[Root Cause Analysis]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Definition of Physical Hazard
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define physical hazard in the context of HACCP. Give two examples of physical hazards commonly encountered in the food industry.**

**Model Answer:**

A physical hazard is defined as any extraneous foreign object or material of a non-biological and non-chemical origin that is unintentionally introduced into a food product and has the potential to cause illness, injury (such as choking, cuts, dental damage, or internal perforation), or physical trauma to a consumer upon ingestion.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Glass fragments** — from broken bottles, jars, light bulbs, or thermometers. These are particularly dangerous because of their sharp edges.
2. **Metal fragments** — from worn equipment (e.g., blades, sieves, conveyor belts, staples). Detected primarily using metal detectors and X-ray systems.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for examples with brief explanation.)*

#### Question 2: Severity vs. Probability
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q: Differentiate between severity and probability in the context of HACCP risk assessment. State the formula for Risk Priority Number (RPN).**

**Model Answer:**

- **Severity (S):** Refers to the *magnitude of the potential health consequence* if the hazard is not controlled. It is rated on a scale (e.g., 1 to 4), ranging from negligible to catastrophic (e.g., death).
- **Probability (P):** Refers to the *likelihood* that the hazard will occur in the absence of control measures. It is also rated on a scale (e.g., 1 to 4), ranging from improbable to frequent.

**Formula:**

$$RPN = Severity \, (S) \times Probability \, (P)$$

The RPN helps the HACCP team prioritise which hazards require **CCPs** (typically when RPN ≥ 8 on a 1–4 scale, or ≥ 50 on a 1–10 scale).

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for severity, probability, and formula.)*

---

### 5.2 KTU Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Model Paper]**

**Q: (a) Classify physical hazards in food processing with suitable examples for each class. (7 marks)**

**Q: (b) Explain the Codex Decision Tree and apply it to determine whether the metal detection step in a snack food (potato chips) line is a CCP. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

Physical hazards in food processing are classified into **three main categories**:

**1. Extraneous Matter (Non-Food Origin)**
These are foreign materials originating from sources outside the food.
- *Glass fragments:* from broken bottles, jars, light bulbs, or thermometer breakage.
- *Metal fragments:* from worn machinery (blades, sieves, conveyor belts, staples, wire).
- *Stone and soil:* from field contamination, especially in root crops.
- *Plastic pieces:* from packaging, gloves, conveyor belts, plastic containers.
- *Wood splinters:* from wooden pallets, crates, or construction materials.

**2. Naturally Occurring Physical Hazards (Food-Origin)**
These are part of the raw material itself.
- *Bones:* in fish fillets, deboned meat, processed chicken products.
- *Shells:* in shellfish such as mussels and clams.
- *Pits/stones:* in fruits such as cherries, olives, peaches, and mangoes.

**3. Process-Introduced Physical Hazards**
These are introduced during manufacturing.
- *Lubricant droplets* from poorly maintained equipment.
- *Rust particles* from corroded surfaces.
- *Welding slag* from maintenance activities.
- *Paint chips* from walls and equipment.
- *Personal effects* (jewellery, hair, fingernails) from poor GMP compliance.

**Justification:** This three-class classification (also used by Codex Alimentarius and FDA) helps HACCP teams design targeted control measures for each category. Extraneous matter is controlled by **detection technologies** (metal detectors, X-ray), naturally occurring hazards by **process redesign** (X-ray bone detection, manual pin-boning), and process-introduced hazards by **GMP and preventive maintenance**.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for classification scheme, 3 marks for examples, 2 marks for brief justification of why classification matters.)*

---

**Model Answer for (b):**

**The Codex Decision Tree for CCP Determination:**

The Codex Decision Tree is a logical sequence of four questions applied at each process step where a physical hazard is identified. It determines whether that step should be designated as a **Critical Control Point (CCP)**.

**Step 1: Question 1 — Do preventive control measures exist?**
If **NO** → the process must be redesigned, or a new control measure introduced.
If **YES** → proceed to Q2.

**Step 2: Question 2 — Does this step specifically eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level?**
If **YES** → **this step IS a CCP** (a "kill" or "elimination" step).
If **NO** → proceed to Q3.

**Step 3: Question 3 — Could contamination with the identified hazard occur in excess of acceptable level(s)?**
If **NO** → **NOT a CCP** (existing controls are sufficient).
If **YES** → proceed to Q4.

**Step 4: Question 4 — Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the hazard?**
If **YES** → **NOT a CCP** (downstream control is sufficient).
If **NO** → **this step IS a CCP** (last line of defence).

**Application to Potato Chips Line — Metal Detection Step:**

- **Hazard identified:** Metal fragments from the slicer blades (extraneous matter, source: cutter wear).
- **Severity:** Catastrophic (S = 4) — sharp metal can cause internal perforation.
- **Probability:** Occasional (P = 3) — happens 2–3 times per year without controls.
- **RPN:** $RPN = 4 \times 3 = 12 \geq 8$ → requires control.

**Applying the Decision Tree:**

- **Q1:** Do preventive control measures exist? **YES** — metal detector with conveyor rejector.
- **Q2:** Does this step eliminate the hazard to an acceptable level? **YES** — the metal detector physically identifies and the pneumatic rejector physically removes the contaminated product.
- **Conclusion:** **This step IS a CCP** (a kill/elimination step per Q2).

**Critical Limits and Monitoring:**
- **Critical Limit:** Ferrous ≥ 1.5 mm, Non-ferrous ≥ 2.0 mm, Stainless steel ≥ 2.5 mm (using certified test pieces).
- **Monitoring:** Continuous detection, with hourly test piece verification, plus rejection of the last 30 minutes of production if a positive detection occurs.
- **Corrective Action:** Quarantine the rejected product, investigate the source, perform root cause analysis, and verify equipment integrity.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for the four-step decision tree, 2 marks for application, 2 marks for critical limits and monitoring.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Model Paper]**

**Q: (a) Discuss the various detection technologies used to control physical hazards in food processing. Compare their sensitivity, cost, and application suitability. (7 marks)**

**Q: (b) A spice grinding unit has identified a risk of stone contamination in turmeric powder. Apply the RPN method to determine whether the destoning step should be a CCP, and propose appropriate control measures with critical limits. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Detection Technologies for Physical Hazards:**

**1. Metal Detectors**
- *Principle:* Detect disturbances in an electromagnetic field caused by metal objects passing through.
- *Detects:* Ferrous, non-ferrous, and stainless steel metals.
- *Sensitivity:* 0.5–2.0 mm (depending on product type and aperture size).
- *Cost:* Medium.
- *Best Application:* Packaged and unpackaged dry, wet, and conductive foods.

**2. X-Ray Inspection Systems**
- *Principle:* Detect differential X-ray absorption based on density.
- *Detects:* Metal, glass, stone, bone, dense plastic; also detects missing product, underfill, and damaged packages.
- *Sensitivity:* 0.3–1.0 mm for most dense contaminants.
- *Cost:* High.
- *Best Application:* Glass jar products, packaged foods, products in metalized films where metal detectors cannot be used.

**3. Optical / Camera Sorters**
- *Principle:* High-resolution cameras detect color, shape, and size anomalies.
- *Detects:* Surface defects, color contamination, some plastics, stones, insects.
- *Sensitivity:* 0.5 mm at surface.
- *Cost:* Medium to high.
- *Best Application:* Whole fruits, vegetables, grains, nuts.

**4. Magnetic Separators**
- *Principle:* Permanent or electromagnets attract ferrous metals.
- *Detects:* Ferrous metals only.
- *Sensitivity:* Very high for ferrous (down to 0.1 mm).
- *Cost:* Low.
- *Best Application:* Dry powders, grains, sugar, flour — typically as a pre-filter before metal detection.

**5. Sieves and Filters**
- *Principle:* Mechanical retention of particles larger than the aperture.
- *Detects:* Any material larger than the mesh size.
- *Sensitivity:* Determined by mesh number (e.g., Mesh #60 = 250 µm).
- *Cost:* Low.
- *Best Application:* Granular products, powders, flour, spices.

**6. Gravity Separators / Destoners**
- *Principle:* Vibration and airflow separate materials by density.
- *Detects:* Stones, glass, heavy metals in granular products.
- *Sensitivity:* 1–2 mm stones in grains.
- *Cost:* Medium.
- *Best Application:* Grains, legumes, seeds, spices.

**Comparison Table:**

| Technology | Sensitivity | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Metal Detector | 0.5–2.0 mm metal | Medium | Most packaged foods |
| X-Ray | 0.3–1.0 mm dense | High | Glass, bone, dense |
| Optical Sorter | 0.5 mm surface | Med-High | Whole foods |
| Magnetic Separator | 0.1 mm ferrous | Low | Dry powders |
| Sieve | Mesh-dependent | Low | Granular |
| Destoner | 1–2 mm stones | Medium | Grains, spices |

**Engineering Justification:** Most modern food plants use a **layered approach** — sieves/magnetic separators at upstream points (OPRP) and metal detectors / X-ray at the final CCP. This multi-barrier strategy reduces the burden on the final detector and provides defence in depth.

*(7 marks: 4 marks for describing 4–5 technologies, 2 marks for comparison, 1 mark for engineering justification.)*

---

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Application of RPN to Turmeric Powder — Stone Contamination:**

**Step 1: Hazard Identification**
- *Hazard:* Stones of size ≥ 2 mm.
- *Source:* Field contamination during turmeric rhizome harvesting; residual soil aggregates.

**Step 2: Severity Assessment (S)**
- A stone ≥ 2 mm can cause **broken tooth, choking, or gastrointestinal irritation**.
- Severity classification: **Critical (S = 3)**.

**Step 3: Probability Assessment (P)**
- Turmeric is a root crop, frequently contaminated with soil clods and small stones.
- Probability classification: **Frequent (P = 4)**.

**Step 4: Compute RPN.**

$$RPN = S \times P = 3 \times 4 = 12$$

**Step 5: Compare to Threshold.**

The KTU / Codex threshold for designating a CCP is **RPN ≥ 8** (on a 1–4 scale).

$$RPN_{calculated} = 12 \geq 8 = RPN_{threshold}$$

**Decision: The destoning step IS a CCP.**

**Step 6: Proposed Control Measures (Multi-Barrier Strategy).**

**PRP Level (Foundation):**
- Supplier approval programme (raw turmeric sourced from approved farms).
- Incoming material visual inspection at reception.
- Wash step to remove loose soil.

**OPRP Level (Process Control):**
- Primary destoner (gravity separator) at the intake of the grinding line.
- Sieve (Mesh #20 = 0.85 mm) at the discharge of the grinder.
- Magnetic separator for metal contamination co-occurring with stones.

**CCP Level (Final Defence):**
- Optical sorter (camera-based) at the packing line to detect residual stones ≥ 1 mm.
- X-ray inspection for dense objects including stones in the final packed product.

**Step 7: Critical Limits for the Destoner CCP.**

| Parameter | Critical Limit |
|---|---|
| Stone size rejected | ≥ 1.5 mm (with safety margin) |
| Product throughput | ≤ 800 kg/h (to allow settling time) |
| Vibration frequency | 280–320 rpm (per equipment OEM) |
| Airflow | 0.25 m³/min per kg of product |
| Reject verification | Every 2 hours, sample 100 g of reject stream; if ≥ 3 stones > 2 mm found in 30 min, take corrective action |

**Step 8: Monitoring and Corrective Actions.**
- *Monitoring:* Continuous flow monitoring of the destoner, with visual inspection of the reject stream every 2 hours.
- *Corrective Action:* If a stone is detected downstream, hold the last 60 minutes of production, run a 100 kg sample through a verification sieve (Mesh #20), and investigate destoner settings.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for RPN calculation, 2 marks for CCP decision, 2 marks for control measures, 1 mark for critical limits.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Physical Hazard Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Severity with Probability:** Many students write "the hazard is severe" when they mean "the hazard is probable." Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the RPN Threshold:** Always state the threshold value (typically ≥ 8 on a 1–4 scale) before declaring a CCP. Without the threshold comparison, the decision is unjustified.
> 3. **Skipping the Decision Tree Application:** When asked "is this a CCP?", you **must** walk through all four Codex questions, not just say "yes, because the hazard is serious."
> 4. **Listing examples without classification:** When asked to "classify" physical hazards, students often just list examples. Examiners expect a **named classification scheme** (e.g., extraneous/natural/process-introduced) with examples under each.
> 5. **Ignoring Units:** Sieve sizes, metal detector sensitivity, and FDA thresholds all have units (mm, mesh number, bar). Marks are deducted for unitless answers.
> 6. **Confusing PRP, OPRP, and CCP:** PRPs are foundation programmes (GMP, glass policy); OPRPs are process controls (sieves, magnets); CCPs are final defences (metal detector, X-ray). Mixing them up loses marks in the "control measures" sub-question.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A physical hazard is a *non-biological, non-chemical* foreign object that can cause physical injury upon ingestion.
- **Three Classes:** Extraneous matter (glass, metal, stone, plastic, wood); natural physical hazards (bones, shells, pits); process-introduced hazards (rust, paint, jewellery, hair).
- **Why Critical in HACCP:** Physical hazards cause **acute injury**, are not destroyed by cooking/freezing, and trigger **consumer complaints** leading to recalls.
- **Risk Assessment Tool:** RPN = Severity × Probability. Threshold typically ≥ 8 (1–4 scale) to require a CCP.
- **Codex Decision Tree:** Four questions (control exists → eliminates hazard → contamination possible → downstream control exists) determine CCP status. **Two CCP endpoints**: Q2 (kill step) and Q4 (last defence).
- **Detection Technologies (must know):** Metal detector (0.5–2.0 mm metal), X-ray (0.3–1.0 mm dense), optical sorter (0.5 mm surface), magnetic separator (0.1 mm ferrous), sieve (mesh-dependent), destoner (1–2 mm stones).
- **Multi-Barrier Strategy:** PRPs (foundation) → OPRPs (process) → CCPs (final defence). This is a defence-in-depth approach.
- **Critical Limits Examples:** Fe ≥ 1.5 mm, Non-Fe ≥ 2.0 mm, SS ≥ 2.5 mm (for metal detectors); sieve Mesh #60 for spice products; FDA hazard threshold ≥ 7 mm for hard/sharp objects in RTE foods.
- **Formula Recap:** RPN = S × P; Mesh # = 25.4 / d(mm); Detection Efficiency = (N_detected / N_total) × 100%.
- **Real-World Application:** Every food processing line should have at least one CCP for physical hazards, validated by a third-party laboratory, with daily test-piece checks and documented corrective actions.
- **Key Regulatory Bodies:** Codex Alimentarius, U.S. FDA, FSSAI (India), EFSA (Europe), BRCGS, FSSC 22000 — all align on the principle of **zero tolerance for hard/sharp physical hazards above defined size thresholds** in ready-to-eat foods.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HACCP Generic Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT868 - HACCP PLANNING AND IMPLEMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** HACCP Generic Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **HACCP Generic Model** is a pre-developed, product-category-specific reference framework that systematically applies the **seven principles of HACCP** (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point) to a defined class of foods, processes, or food-handling operations, providing a scientifically validated template that individual food businesses can adapt and customize to their own specific operational context.

According to the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)** and the **National Advisory Committee on Microbiological Criteria for Foods (NACMCF)**, a generic model is a ready-to-use document that identifies likely hazards, Critical Control Points (CCPs), critical limits, monitoring procedures, corrective actions, verification activities, and record-keeping formats for a particular commodity or process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEFTT868, Module 2):**
> A HACCP Generic Model is *NOT* a one-size-fits-all solution. It is a **blueprint** developed for a specific food category (e.g., pasteurized milk, comminuted meat, cooked sausages, canned vegetables) that must be **tailored** to the unique conditions of each establishment before implementation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Master Recipe Card"**
> Think of a HACCP Generic Model like a **master recipe card** used in a professional bakery franchise.
> - The **master recipe** says: bake at **180°C** for **25 minutes**, add **yeast**, allow **proofing for 1 hour**.
> - But every individual bakery must adapt it: the baker in **Kerala** (humid climate) may need *less* proofing time, while the baker in **Rajasthan** (dry climate) may need *more*.
> - The master recipe gives the **structure and scientific logic**; the local baker fine-tunes the **critical limits** based on local reality.
> - Similarly, a HACCP Generic Model gives the **scientific structure**, but each food business must **customize** the critical limits, monitoring frequency, and corrective actions for its own equipment, workforce, and product line.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **CCPs (Critical Control Points)** — the step(s) at which control can be applied to prevent, eliminate, or reduce a food safety hazard to an acceptable level.
- **CL (Critical Limit)** — the maximum or minimum value to which a biological, chemical, or physical parameter must be controlled at a CCP.
- **QACCP** — Quality Assurance Critical Control Point (broader term occasionally referenced in quality management literature).
- **FMEA** — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (often used as a parallel risk-assessment tool during hazard analysis).
- **Decision Tree** — A logical questioning tool (Codex tree) used to determine whether a step is a CCP.
- **OPRP** — Operational Prerequisite Programme (related to but distinct from CCPs under ISO 22000).

### 1.4 Why Generic Models Exist

| Reason | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| **Cost Reduction** | Small/medium enterprises (SMEs) cannot afford a full HACCP study from scratch. |
| **Scientific Validation** | Generic models are developed by national/international expert bodies using peer-reviewed data. |
| **Standardization** | Promotes uniform food safety outcomes across an entire industry segment. |
| **Training Tool** | Serves as a teaching instrument for food handlers and QA personnel. |
| **Audit Reference** | Auditors (FSSAI, BRC, SQF, ISO 22000) accept a well-adapted generic model as compliance evidence. |

> [!TIP]
> The **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** has published generic HACCP models for several food categories under its *Food Safety and Standards (Food Safety Management) Regulations, 2011*. These are widely accepted in KTU-affiliated food technology examinations.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Foundational Framework

Every HACCP Generic Model is built upon the **Seven Codex Principles** and the **Twelve Sequential Tasks** (the latter being the practical steps that operationalize the principles).

#### The 12 Tasks of HACCP (Pre-requisite + 7 Principles)

**Preliminary Tasks (Steps 0 to 5)**

1. **Assemble the HACCP Team** — Multidisciplinary group with expertise in product, process, microbiology, and quality.
2. **Describe the Product** — Composition, physical/chemical structure, shelf life, storage conditions, packaging.
3. **Identify Intended Use** — Who is the consumer? Any vulnerable groups (infants, pregnant women, immunocompromised, elderly)?
4. **Construct the Process Flow Diagram** — Sequential, block-level depiction of every step from raw material receipt to consumer.
5. **On-Site Verification of Flow Diagram** — Walk-through by the HACCP team on the actual shop-floor to confirm accuracy.

**The 7 Principles (Steps 6 to 12)**

6. **(Principle 1)** Conduct a Hazard Analysis — Identify biological, chemical, and physical hazards; assess severity and likelihood.
7. **(Principle 2)** Determine the Critical Control Points (CCPs) — Using the Codex Decision Tree.
8. **(Principle 3)** Establish Critical Limits for each CCP.
9. **(Principle 4)** Establish Monitoring Procedures — What, how, when, who, frequency.
10. **(Principle 5)** Establish Corrective Actions — What to do when a critical limit is exceeded.
11. **(Principle 6)** Establish Verification Procedures — Audits, calibration, sampling, review.
12. **(Principle 7)** Establish Documentation and Record-Keeping — All records must be retrievable for the shelf life of the product plus a defined retention period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Emphasis:** A generic model presents ALL twelve tasks in a structured document. The *most frequently tested* sections are the **hazard analysis table**, the **CCP decision tree logic**, and the **HACCP plan summary matrix**.

### 2.2 The Codex CCP Decision Tree (Logic Sequence)

The decision tree asks a series of four questions in sequence:

- **Q1:** Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard?
- **Q2:** Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the likely occurrence of the hazard to an acceptable level? (If YES → **CCP**. If NO → proceed to Q3.)
- **Q3:** Could contamination with the identified hazard(s) occur in excess of acceptable level(s), or could these increase to unacceptable level(s)?
- **Q4:** Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard to an acceptable level? (If YES → **NOT a CCP**. If NO → **CCP**.)

> [!NOTE]
> The decision tree is the **most important KTU 14-mark question** topic in this module. Memorize the four-question sequence and the four answer outcomes (Modify / CCP1 / CCP2 / Not CCP).

### 2.3 Generic vs. Product-Specific HACCP Plan

| Feature | Generic Model | Product-Specific Plan |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Scope** | Product category (e.g., all pasteurized milk) | One specific product in one specific plant |
| **Developed by** | Industry associations, government, FAO/WHO | The individual food business operator (FBO) |
| **Customization** | Mandatory before use | Already customized |
| **Hazard list** | Common hazards, all sites | Site-specific hazards considered |
| **Critical limits** | Range (e.g., 72°C for 15 sec) | Exact validated number |
| **Validation** | Done by expert panel | Done in-house + reference model |
| **KTU Exam Type** | Theory + model interpretation | Case-study based adaptation |

### 2.4 Key Document Components of a Generic Model

1. **Cover Page & Scope Statement**
2. **Product Description & Intended Use**
3. **Process Flow Diagram (block-level)**
4. **Hazard Analysis Worksheet (HAW)** — Hazard / Cause / Severity / Likelihood / Control Measures / Critical?
5. **CCP Determination Sheet (with Decision Tree outputs)**
6. **HACCP Plan Summary Matrix** — CCP / Critical Limit / Monitoring / Corrective Action / Verification / Records
7. **Verification Schedule**
8. **Record-Keeping Templates**
9. **Bibliography / References**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Industry:** Mandatory prerequisite for FSSAI licensing for high-risk categories (milk, meat, fish, packaged water).
- **Export Houses:** Required for shipments to EU, USA, Japan — these markets demand Codex-aligned HACCP documentation.
- **Auditing & Certification:** Foundation for BRC, SQF, IFS, FSSC 22000 certifications.
- **Quality Engineering Education:** KTU food technology graduates are expected to *develop* and *audit* such models in their professional careers.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Key-Parameter Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> HACCP is a *qualitative-management* discipline. The "formula sheet" below summarizes the **decision-logic and severity/likelihood scoring** used in hazard analysis.

| Parameter | Description | Typical Scale Used | Engineering Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Severity (S)** | Consequence if hazard occurs | 1 = Low, 2 = Medium, 3 = High, 4 = Catastrophic | Risk Magnitude |
| **Likelihood (L)** | Probability of occurrence | 1 = Rare, 2 = Occasional, 3 = Likely, 4 = Almost Certain | Frequency Estimate |
| **Risk Score (R)** | $R = S \times L$ | 1 to 16 | Combined hazard priority |
| **Significant Hazard** | $R \geq 6$ (industry default) | Threshold for HACCP control | Determines if hazard moves to Principle 2 |
| **CL Tolerance** | Measurement uncertainty | $\pm \Delta T$ for temperature, $\pm \Delta t$ for time | Defines critical-limit width |
| **D-value** | Decimal reduction time (for pathogens) | Time at given temperature to kill 90% | Used in pasteurization/sterilization |
| **z-value** | Temperature change for 10-fold change in D | °C | Used to compute equivalent lethality |
| **F-value** | Equivalent sterilization time at reference T | minutes | Total lethality delivered |
| **Pasteurization (HTST)** | $72^{\circ}\text{C}$ for **15 s** | Codex/CAC standard | Milk CCP example |
| **Sterilization (canned low-acid)** | $F_{0} \geq 3 \text{ min}$ at $121.1^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Codex standard | Canning CCP example |

> [!NOTE]
> The D-value, z-value, and F-value are borrowed from **food microbiology / thermal processing engineering** and frequently appear in numerical KTU questions, especially in Module 3 and Module 4.
