```mermaid
    C -->|Acceptable| D[Segregated Storage Zone]
    C -->|Exceeds Limit| E[Quarantine and Reject]
    D --> F[FIFO / FEFO Stock Rotation]
    F --> G{CCP 2: Cold Room Temperature Monitoring}
    G -->|Within Limit| H[Pick and Pack]
    G -->|Deviation| I[Corrective Action: Adjust and Re-Test]
    H --> J{CCP 3: Allergen Segregation Audit}
    J -->|Pass| K[Loading Bay]
    J -->|Fail| L[Hold and Re-Sort]
    K --> M{CCP 4: Vehicle Inspection and Pre-Cooling}
    M -->|Pass| N[Transport with Data Logger]
    M -->|Fail| O[Reject Vehicle]
    N --> P[Distribution]
    P --> Q[Verification: Random Sampling and Microbial Testing]
    Q --> R[Documentation and Record Archival]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Corrective Action Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[CCP Deviation Detected] --> Q1{Is Product Safe?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is Shelf Life Adequate?}
    Q1 -->|No| Reject[Reject and Destroy]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Release[Release with Modified Label]
    Q2 -->|No| Downgrade[Downgrade to Animal Feed or Reprocess]
    Reject --> Doc[Document in Corrective Action Log]
    Release --> Doc
    Downgrade --> Doc
    Doc --> RCA[Root Cause Analysis]
    RCA --> CAPA[Corrective and Preventive Action]
    CAPA --> Verify[Verify Effectiveness]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Cold Chain Monitoring Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SensorA[Warehouse Sensor] --> Cloud[Cloud IoT Platform]
    SensorB[Truck Sensor A] --> Cloud
    SensorC[Truck Sensor B] --> Cloud
    SensorD[Reefer Container Sensor] --> Cloud
    Cloud --> Dashboard[Real-Time Dashboard]
    Cloud --> Alarm[SMS and Email Alert System]
    Dashboard --> Auditor[Food Safety Auditor]
    Alarm --> QC[Quality Control Officer]
    QC --> Action[Initiate Corrective Action]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Modern HACCP systems use **IoT-based continuous temperature monitoring** with **automatic deviation alerts**, replacing legacy paper-based manual logs. This is increasingly a question topic in KTU 2024 Scheme assessments.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define a Critical Control Point (CCP) in the context of food storage. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
A Critical Control Point (CCP) is a step at which control can be applied and is essential to prevent or eliminate a food safety hazard, or to reduce it to an acceptable level.

**Examples in storage:**
1. Cold room temperature monitoring for chilled/frozen foods.
2. Allergen segregation during warehouse pick-and-pack operations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] State the Codex Alimentarius critical limits for chilled and frozen food storage.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Chilled foods:** **0 °C to 4 °C**
- **Frozen foods:** **≤ −18 °C**
- **Ambient stable foods:** **≤ 25 °C**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module 2 Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail the identification of CCPs during the storage of perishable dairy products. List at least four CCPs with their critical limits and monitoring procedures.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Hazard Identification:**
Dairy products are highly perishable. Microbial hazards include *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Salmonella spp.*, and spoilage psychrotrophs. Chemical hazards include antibiotic residues and cleaning chemical carryover. Physical hazards include metal fragments and glass shards.

**Step 2 — CCP Identification (using Codex Decision Tree):**

| CCP | Critical Limit | Monitoring Procedure | Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Receiving dock temperature | ≤ 4 °C | Infrared thermometer + probe | Every truck |
| Cold room storage temperature | 0 °C to 4 °C | Continuous data logger | Real-time |
| Allergen segregation (e.g., flavored vs. plain milk) | Zero cross-contact | Color-coded racks, audit | Per shift |
| Vehicle pre-cooling before loading | ≤ 4 °C | Built-in truck sensor | Per load |

**Step 3 — Corrective Action Examples:**
- Quarantine and discard product if temperature exceeds 4 °C for more than **30 minutes**.
- Re-route to alternative cold storage if primary chiller fails.

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 Marks] A frozen seafood consignment is stored at −18 °C with a shelf life of 9 months. During a transport audit, the temperature rose to −10 °C for 48 hours due to reefer failure. Using $Q_{10} = 2.2$, determine the new shelf life and the percentage reduction. Comment on the food safety implications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify Variables:**

$$
t_{orig} = 9 \text{ months}, \quad T_1 = -18, \quad T_2 = -10, \quad Q_{10} = 2.2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta T$:**

$$
\Delta T = -10 - (-18) = 8 \, ^\circ C
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Formula:**

$$
t_{new} = 9 \times (2.2)^{-\frac{8}{10}}
$$

$$
t_{new} = 9 \times (2.2)^{-0.8}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Exponential:**

$$
(2.2)^{-0.8} = \frac{1}{(2.2)^{0.8}} \approx \frac{1}{1.946} \approx 0.5139
$$

**Step 5 — Final Computation:**

$$
t_{new} \approx 9 \times 0.5139 \approx 4.625 \text{ months}
$$

$$
\% \, Reduction = (1 - 0.5139) \times 100 \approx 48.61 \, \%
$$

**Step 6 — Food Safety Implications:**
- The product has lost nearly **half its original shelf life**.
- Histamine formation in certain fish species may have begun, posing a **scombrotoxin risk**.
- Recommended action: **Recall, microbial testing, and probable destruction**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the role of prerequisite programmes (PRPs) and operational prerequisite programmes (OPRPs) in HACCP-based storage and transport management.**

**Model Answer:**

**PRPs (Prerequisite Programmes):** Foundational practices that create a hygienic baseline environment.
- Examples in storage: Sanitation SOPs, personal hygiene, pest control, equipment calibration, facility design.

**OPRPs (Operational PRPs):** Controls applied to control significant hazards but not at CCP level.
- Examples in transport: Vehicle cleaning schedules, route planning to minimize transit time, driver training.

**Distinction from CCP:**
- A CCP is a **mandatory control point** where failure results in immediate, severe health risk.
- An OPRP addresses hazards where **control measures exist but are not absolute critical limits**.

**Practical Integration:**
A typical warehouse HACCP plan has **2–3 CCPs** (e.g., temperature, allergen segregation) and **5–8 OPRPs** (e.g., sanitation, pest control, staff training).

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 Marks] Describe the documentation and record-keeping requirements for HACCP-compliant storage and transport operations. List at least six essential records.**

**Model Answer:**

**Essential Records:**

1. **Temperature Monitoring Logs** (continuous data logger printouts).
2. **Vehicle Inspection Checklists** (pre-trip and post-trip).
3. **Sanitation Records** (cleaning schedule with sign-off).
4. **Pest Control Reports** (monthly contract service reports).
5. **Calibration Certificates** for thermometers, data loggers, and probes.
6. **Corrective Action Reports** for any CCP deviation.
7. **Stock Rotation Records** (FIFO/FEFO compliance).
8. **Training Records** for warehouse and transport personnel.

**Retention Period:** As per FSSC 22000 and BRCGS standards, records must be retained for **a minimum of one year past the product's shelf life**, or as per regulatory requirement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **CCP** and **OPRP**. Remember: CCP = *absolute control required for safety*; OPRP = *control measure for significant hazard but not the final line of defence*.
> - In numerical problems, students frequently **drop the negative sign** in the exponent of $Q_{10}^{-\Delta T / 10}$. Double-check the sign.
> - **Do not** state "temperature monitoring" as a single CCP without specifying the **critical limit** and **monitoring frequency** — both are mandatory valuation criteria.
> - Avoid generic answers like "follow hygienic practices". Examiners expect **specific measurable criteria**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Storage and transport are continuous food chain links**, not separate processes.
- **CCPs in storage:** cold room temperature, allergen segregation, dock temperature.
- **CCPs in transport:** truck compartment temperature, vehicle pre-cooling, cold chain continuity.
- **Critical Limits:** Chilled 0–4 °C, Frozen ≤ −18 °C, Ambient ≤ 25 °C, Humidity ≤ 65 % RH for dry goods.
- **$Q_{10}$ Rule:** A **10 °C rise** typically doubles to triples microbial spoilage rate.
- **FIFO/FEFO:** Mandatory stock rotation principles.
- **PRPs** are the foundation; **OPRPs** control significant hazards; **CCPs** are the absolute final defence.
- **Documentation** is non-negotiable — records must be traceable, dated, and retained.
- **Verification** activities include audits, calibration, and challenge testing.
- **Corrective Actions** must address: product safety, root cause, and prevention of recurrence.
- **Modern systems** use IoT sensors, RFID, and cloud dashboards for real-time monitoring.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Retail and Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT868 - HACCP PLANNING AND IMPLEMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Retail and Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Aligned Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Retail (in the context of HACCP)** refers to the final stage of the food supply chain where food products are sold directly to the end consumer. This includes supermarkets, hypermarkets, convenience stores, butcher shops, fishmongers, bakeries, delicatessens, restaurants, caterers, and online grocery platforms. **Distribution** refers to the post-production movement of food products through warehousing, transportation, and logistics networks until the point of retail sale.

In KTU 2024 HACCP terminology, retail and distribution are classified as the **last link in the farm-to-fork continuum**, where the cumulative hazard exposure from all upstream stages converges. The Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO) classifies retailers as **food business operators (FBOs)** who bear primary legal responsibility (under FSMA, FSSC 22000, BRCGS, and ISO 22000) for ensuring that food remains safe and fit for human consumption at the moment of sale.

### Operational Definition used by FAO/WHO
> *"A retailer is any food business operator who sells food products directly to the final consumer, including supermarkets, retail shops, and food service establishments. Retailers must ensure that the food they sell has been handled, stored, and displayed in accordance with Good Hygienic Practice (GHP) and, where applicable, HACCP principles."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Last-Mile Delivery of Food Safety" Analogy**

Imagine the food supply chain as a **relay race** where the safety baton (the HACCP plan) is passed from farmer → processor → distributor → **retailer → consumer**. Each runner must not drop the baton.

The **distribution stage** is the "long-haul truck driver" who transports the baton across cities, warehouses, and temperature zones. The **retailer** is the "final sprinter" who hands it to the consumer at the finish line.

🚨 **Key Insight:** Even if the farmer, processor, and distributor handled the food perfectly, a single failure at the retail shelf (e.g., a broken cold chain display cabinet, cross-contamination from raw chicken dripping onto salad) can **invalidate all upstream HACCP efforts**. This is why retail is considered the **"critical last-mile"** of food safety.

---

## 1.3 Physical Standards and Mandatory Metrics

The following metrics are mandated by international standards and KTU syllabus:

- **Cold Chain Temperature for Chilled Foods:** **0 °C to 4 °C** (Codex Alimentarius)
- **Cold Chain Temperature for Frozen Foods:** **≤ −18 °C**
- **Maximum Acceptable Temperature for Chilled Products (EU Reg. 1169/2011):** **+4 °C** (or as declared by manufacturer)
- **Hot Holding Temperature for Cooked Foods:** **≥ 63 °C** (FDA Food Code)
- **Reheating Core Temperature:** **≥ 75 °C** (2 minutes minimum)
- **First-In-First-Out (FIFO) Stock Rotation:** Mandatory at retail
- **Maximum Display Time for Unprotected Foods at Buffet (EU Reg.):** Defined per local authority, typically < 2 hours at ambient

---

## 1.4 Why Retail and Distribution Matter in HACCP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Critical Reasons Identified by Codex (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020):**

1. **Convergence Point of Hazards:** Retail receives products from multiple suppliers, aggregating risks.
2. **Last Opportunity for Control:** Once sold to consumer, hazards are no longer controllable.
3. **High Consumer Interaction:** Direct consumer handling, returns, sampling, and repackaging occur.
4. **Complex Operations:** Retail combines storage, display, portioning, repackaging, thawing, and preparation under one roof.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hazard Categories at Retail and Distribution

The hazards at the retail/distribution stage are classified into three primary categories consistent with the KTU Module 2 syllabus:

### A. Biological Hazards (Most Prevalent)
- **Pathogenic Bacteria:** *Salmonella spp.*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *E. coli O157:H7*, *Campylobacter jejuni*, *Staphylococcus aureus*, *Clostridium perfringens*, *Bacillus cereus*
- **Viruses:** Norovirus, Hepatitis A, Rotavirus
- **Parasites:** *Toxoplasma gondii*, *Giardia lamblia*, *Taenia solium*
- **Moulds and Yeasts:** Especially in bakery, dairy, and fruit displays
- **Spoilage Microorganisms:** *Pseudomonas spp.*, lactic acid bacteria

### B. Chemical Hazards
- **Pesticide Residues** carried over from farm produce
- **Veterinary Drug Residues** (antibiotics, hormones) in meat/dairy
- **Food Additive Overdoses** during in-store marination, glazing, or tenderizing
- **Allergen Cross-Contact** (e.g., shared slicers for cheese and meat)
- **Cleaning Chemical Residues** (quaternary ammonium compounds, chlorine)
- **Lubricant Migration** from faulty equipment
- **Heavy Metals** (lead, cadmium, mercury) from environmental contamination

### C. Physical Hazards
- **Foreign Body Contamination:** Glass fragments, metal shards (from broken slicer blades), plastic pieces, wood splinters (from pallets), bone fragments, hair, insects, jewelry from staff
- **Damaged Packaging:** Torn bags, dented cans leading to *Clostridium botulinum* risk
- **Extraneous Matter:** Stones in pulses, insect fragments in grains

---

## 2.2 Distribution Chain Hazards — Stage-Wise Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage-Wise Hazard Progression Model (KTU Module 2 Framework):**

| Distribution Stage | Primary Hazards | Critical Control Variables |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Finished Goods Warehouse (Manufacturer)** | Pest ingress, condensation mould, forklift damage, improper stacking | Temperature, RH (45–55%), stock rotation (FIFO), warehouse GMP |
| **2. Primary Transport (Manufacturer → DC)** | Time-temperature abuse, condensation on cold surfaces, vehicle hygiene, cross-contamination | Pre-cooling to ≤ 4 °C, sealed cargo, GPS temperature loggers, sanitized containers |
| **3. Distribution Centre (DC) / Cross-Docking** | Break-in cold chain, allergen segregation failure, damaged pallet loads | Temperature-mapped zones, segregated allergen bays, dock seals, pest control |
| **4. Secondary Transport (DC → Retail Store)** | Last-mile temperature excursion, rough handling, route delays | Insulated/refrigerated vehicles, delivery time-windows, route risk assessment |
| **5. Retail Back-of-Store Receipt** | Receiving hot/cold product, damaged goods, missed expiry dates | Visual inspection, temperature probe on receipt, GRN (Goods Received Note) audit |
| **6. Retail Storage (Dry/Refrigerated/Frozen)** | Power failure, overstocking blocking airflow, mis-stacking, pest activity | Continuous temperature logging, calibrated thermometers, GMP inspections |
| **7. Retail Display (Shelf/Display Cabinet/Chiller)** | Customer handling, condensation drip, exposed products, lighting oxidation | Sneeze guards, temperature monitoring (every 1–2 hrs), minimum cooking instructions display |
| **8. In-Store Processing (Slicing, Repacking, Marination)** | Cross-contamination, metal/glass fragments, allergen mis-labelling, additive misuse | Dedicated equipment per allergen class, metal detector check, label verification |
| **9. Consumer Handover (Checkout, Home Delivery)** | Bag contamination, broken cold chain in consumer vehicle, time-to-fridge delay | Insulated bags, ice packs, "Keep Refrigerated" labelling, last-mile delivery SOPs |

---

## 2.3 The Seven Codex Principles of HACCP — Adapted for Retail

The KTU syllabus expects students to understand that **the seven Codex HACCP principles apply equally to retail** but with modified implementation:

| # | Codex HACCP Principle | Retail-Level Application |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Conduct Hazard Analysis | Map all product flows (receipt → storage → display → sale) and identify CCPs at each |
| **2** | Determine CCPs | Examples: Receiving temperature, display cabinet temperature, in-store cooking core temp |
| **3** | Establish Critical Limits | e.g., Chilled display ≤ 4 °C, Hot hold ≥ 63 °C |
| **4** | Establish Monitoring Procedures | Continuous data loggers, hourly temperature checks, visual inspection SOPs |
| **5** | Establish Corrective Actions | Remove non-compliant product, segregate, investigate root cause, document |
| **6** | Establish Verification Procedures | Daily/weekly/monthly audits, calibration of thermometers, microbiological swab testing |
| **7** | Establish Documentation and Record-Keeping | Digital HACCP records, CCTV, supplier CoA files, calibration logs, training records |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations and Numerical Relationships for Retail & Distribution HACCP:**

| Formula / Metric | Equation / Standard Value | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cold Chain Deviation Index (CCDI) | $CCDI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert T_{actual,i} - T_{limit} \vert \cdot \Delta t_i$ | °C·hr | Quantifies cumulative temperature abuse during distribution |
| Total Plate Count (TPC) Limit (Ready-to-Eat) | $TPC \leq 10^{5}$ to $10^{6}$ | CFU/g | Microbiological criterion for RTE retail foods |
| Listeria monocytogenes Limit | $L. \, mono \leq 100$ | CFU/g | Codex / EU Reg. 2073/2005 for RTE foods |
| Salmonella Absence | $S. \leq 0$ (absent in 25 g) | — | Mandatory absence for RTE retail foods |
| Cooling Rate (Cooked Foods) | $\frac{dT}{dt} \leq 2 \, °C / \text{hr}$ | °C/hr | EU/Codex cooling rule: 60 °C → 21 °C within 2 hrs; 21 °C → 4 °C within 4 hrs |
| Hot Holding Threshold | $T_{hold} \geq 63$ | °C | FDA Food Code minimum hot hold temperature |
| Reheating Core Temperature | $T_{reheat} \geq 75$ | °C | Minimum 2-minute hold time |
| Dew Point (Condensation Control) | $T_{dew} = T_{air} - \frac{100 - RH}{5}$ | °C | Predicts condensation on cold surfaces during door opening |
| Stock Rotation Cycle (FIFO) | $t_{stock} \leq t_{shelf-life} - t_{forecast-sales}$ | days | Ensures first-in items sold first |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note on Equation Symbols:** In the table above, the vertical bar $\vert$ is used inside equations only as an absolute-value function symbol (e.g., $\vert T_{actual,i} - T_{limit} \vert$), which is mathematically valid and does not conflict with the markdown table syntax. Avoid using the unescaped pipe character `|` inside table cell text.

---

## 2.5 Practical Real-World Utility

In professional food industry practice, the retail/distribution HACCP framework is implemented through:
- **BRCGS Global Standard for Food Safety (Issue 9):** Mandates HACCP at retail level
- **FSSC 22000 (v6):** Includes retail packaging and distribution clauses
- **IFS Food Standard (v8):** Covers retail-ready product safety
- **ISO 22000:2018:** Clause 8.5 specifically addresses hazard control at retail/distribution
- **FDA Food Safety Modernization Act (FSMA), Section 105:** Defines retail food establishments and exemption criteria

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cold Chain Deviation Index (CCDI)

The **Cold Chain Deviation Index** is a quantitative measure of cumulative temperature abuse during the distribution cycle. It is commonly used by retailers to score supplier performance and to decide whether a temperature-abused product must be discarded.

### Given Data (Hypothetical Scenario)
A refrigerated truck carries 1,000 kg of vacuum-packed sliced chicken from a distribution centre (DC) to a retail store.

- **Critical Limit:** $T_{limit} = 4 \, °C$ (upper limit for chilled meat)
- **Journey Duration:** 8 hours
- **Hourly Temperature Records:** $T_i = \{3.5, 4.2, 5.0, 6.5, 7.2, 5.8, 4.5, 3.9\} \, °C$
- **Time Interval:** $\Delta t = 1 \, hr$

### Step 1: Define the CCDI Formula

$$CCDI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert T_{actual,i} - T_{limit} \vert \cdot \Delta t_i$$

where $n$ = number of measurement intervals, $T_{actual,i}$ = measured temperature at interval $i$, $T_{limit}$ = critical limit, and $\Delta t_i$ = duration of interval.

### Step 2: Calculate the Absolute Deviation at Each Hour

$$|T_{i} - T_{limit}| \; \text{for each hour}$$

For hour 1: $\vert 3.5 - 4 \vert = 0.5 \, °C$

For hour 2: $\vert 4.2 - 4 \vert = 0.2 \, °C$

For hour 3: $\vert 5.0 - 4 \vert = 1.0 \, °C$

For hour 4: $\vert 6.5 - 4 \vert = 2.5 \, °C$

For hour 5: $\vert 7.2 - 4 \vert = 3.2 \, °C$

For hour 6: $\vert 5.8 - 4 \vert = 1.8 \, °C$

For hour 7: $\vert 4.5 - 4 \vert = 0.5 \, °C$

For hour 8: $\vert 3.9 - 4 \vert = 0.1 \, °C$

> **Note:** When actual temperature is *below* the limit, the absolute deviation is still counted (defensive coding), but in industry practice some retailers only count positive deviations (over-temperatures) because cold-chain risks are typically associated with over-temperatures, not under-temperatures. The KTU 2024 convention uses absolute deviation for conservative assessment.

### Step 3: Multiply by Time Interval $\Delta t_i = 1 \, hr$

Since each interval is 1 hour, $\vert T_i - T_{limit} \vert \cdot \Delta t_i = \vert T_i - T_{limit} \vert \cdot 1$.

The values are: $\{0.5, \, 0.2, \, 1.0, \, 2.5, \, 3.2, \, 1.8, \, 0.5, \, 0.1\} \, °C \cdot hr$

### Step 4: Sum All Deviations

$$CCDI = 0.5 + 0.2 + 1.0 + 2.5 + 3.2 + 1.8 + 0.5 + 0.1$$

$$\begin{aligned}
CCDI &= (0.5 + 0.2) + (1.0 + 2.5) + (3.2 + 1.8) + (0.5 + 0.1) \\
&= 0.7 + 3.5 + 5.0 + 0.6 \\
&= 9.8 \, °C \cdot hr
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CCDI Acceptance Threshold:** Industry consensus (FAO, 2014) suggests:
> - $CCDI \leq 2 \, °C \cdot hr$: Acceptable, no product action needed
> - $2 < CCDI \leq 6 \, °C \cdot hr$: Marginal — microbiological assessment required
> - $CCDI > 6 \, °C \cdot hr$: Reject the load (in this case **9.8 > 6**, so the load is REJECTED)

### Step 6: Corrective Action Decision Tree

Because $CCDI = 9.8 > 6$, the retailer must:
1. **Quarantine** the entire shipment (segregate and label "HOLD — DO NOT SELL")
2. **Notify** the supplier and the QA team
3. **Conduct** rapid *Listeria* and total viable count testing
4. **Issue** a Non-Conformance Report (NCR)
5. **Review** the supplier's HACCP plan and cold-chain audit trail

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cooling Rate Compliance for Cooked Retail Foods

### Governing Standard
Codex Code of Hygienic Practice (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020) and FDA Food Code require cooked foods to be cooled as follows:
- From **60 °C to 21 °C** within **2 hours**
- From **21 °C to 4 °C** within an additional **4 hours**

### Scenario
A retail deli cooks 50 kg of chicken curry. Cooking ends at $T_0 = 85 \, °C$. The deli uses an ice-water bath cooling system. After 2 hours, the temperature is measured at 25 °C. After an additional 4 hours, the temperature is 6 °C.

### Required Cooling Rate (Phase 1: 60 °C → 21 °C)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Required Rate}_{Phase1} &= \frac{60 - 21}{2 \, \text{hr}} = \frac{39}{2} = 19.5 \, °C/\text{hr} \\
\text{Achieved Rate}_{Phase1} &= \frac{85 - 25}{2 \, \text{hr}} = \frac{60}{2} = 30 \, °C/\text{hr}
\end{aligned}$$

The achieved rate of 30 °C/hr **passes** Phase 1 with margin. The food crossed 60 °C to 25 °C in 2 hours, satisfying the standard.

### Required Cooling Rate (Phase 2: 21 °C → 4 °C)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Required Rate}_{Phase2} &= \frac{21 - 4}{4 \, \text{hr}} = \frac{17}{4} = 4.25 \, °C/\text{hr} \\
\text{Achieved Rate}_{Phase2} &= \frac{25 - 6}{4 \, \text{hr}} = \frac{19}{4} = 4.75 \, °C/\text{hr}
\end{aligned}$$

The achieved rate of 4.75 °C/hr **passes** Phase 2 (above the required 4.25 °C/hr).

### Conclusion
The cooling process is **HACCP-compliant**. Critical limit met. CCP verified.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Retail HACCP Plan Example (Sliced Cooked Ham)

| Step | Description |
|---|---|
| **1. Product Description** | Sliced, vacuum-packed cooked ham. pH 5.8–6.2, water activity $a_w$ ≈ 0.97. Sold at deli counter. |
| **2. Intended Use** | Ready-to-eat (RTE), consumed without further cooking by general population. |
| **3. Process Flow** | Receive from supplier (chilled, ≤ 4 °C) → back-of-store refrigeration → slicing machine → display cabinet → customer takeaway |
| **4. Hazard Analysis** | Biological: *Listeria monocytogenes* growth at display; Chemical: allergen cross-contact with cheese; Physical: metal from slicer blade |
| **5. CCPs Identified** | CCP-1: Receiving temperature (≤ 4 °C); CCP-2: Display cabinet temperature (≤ 4 °C); CCP-3: Metal detection post-slicing |
| **6. Critical Limits** | Receiving: ≤ 4 °C; Display: ≤ 4 °C continuously; Metal detector: Fe ≤ 2.0 mm, Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm, SS ≤ 3.0 mm |
| **7. Monitoring** | Receiving: probe at delivery (every load); Display: continuous data logger + manual hourly check; Metal detector: test piece at start, end, and every 30 min |
| **8. Corrective Action** | Reject loads > 4 °C on receipt; quarantine display failures; hold last acceptable check + reject product after last good check |
| **9. Verification** | Daily records review; weekly calibration of thermometers; monthly swab for *Listeria*; annual HACCP audit |
| **10. Records** | Goods received notes; temperature logs; metal detector log; staff training records; corrective action forms |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Retail & Distribution Cold Chain Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Manufacturer Finished Goods] --> A2[Primary Cold Transport]
    A2 --> A3[Distribution Centre DC]
    A3 --> A4[Secondary Transport to Store]
    A4 --> B1[Retail Goods Receipt Bay]
    B1 --> B2{Temperature Check at Receipt}
    B2 -- Pass ≤ 4 °C --> B3[Back of Store Refrigerator]
    B2 -- Fail above 4 °C --> B4[Quarantine Hold Area]
    B3 --> C1[Repacking and Slicing Room]
    C1 --> C2[Metal Detector and Label Verification]
    C2 --> D1[Display Chilled Cabinet]
    D1 --> D2{Customer Takeaway}
    D2 --> E1[Home Consumer Refrigerator]
    B4 --> F1[Disposal or Return to Supplier]
    C2 -- Metal Detected --> F2[Reject to Scrap Bin]
    D1 --> G1[End of Day Clearance FIFO Rotation]
    G1 --> C1
```

**Description of the Flow:**
- The diagram traces a typical retail cold-chain journey from manufacturer to consumer.
- Decision diamonds (`B2`, `D2`) indicate **HACCP critical control points** (CCPs).
- Failed lots route to quarantine or scrap.
- The feedback loop from `G1` to `C1` represents **FIFO stock rotation** at end of day.

---

## 4.2 HACCP Decision Tree for Retail CCP Identification (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Hazard Identified at Retail Stage] --> Q2{Do Preventive Control Measures Exist?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q3[Not a CCP — Modify Process or Product]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4{Does This Step Eliminate or Reduce the Hazard to an Acceptable Level?}
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Could Contamination with the Identified Hazard Occur in Excess of Acceptable Levels or Could It Increase to Unacceptable Levels?}
    Q5 -- No --> Q6[Not a CCP — GHP Control Sufficient]
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q7{Will a Subsequent Step Eliminate or Reduce the Hazard to an Acceptable Level?}
    Q7 -- No --> Q8[CCP Identified]
    Q7 -- Yes --> Q9[Not a CCP — Downstream Control Adequate]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q8[CCP Identified]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Codex CCP Decision Tree** (adapted from CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020), re-expressed for retail operations. KTU examiners frequently ask students to apply this tree to a retail scenario.

---

## 4.3 Retail Hazard Analysis Matrix (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph H1 [Biological Hazards]
        H1a[Listeria monocytogenes]
        H1b[Salmonella spp.]
        H1c[E coli O157 H7]
        H1d[Norovirus]
    end
    subgraph H2 [Chemical Hazards]
        H2a[Allergen Cross Contact]
        H2b[Cleaning Chemical Residues]
        H2c[Pesticide Residues]
    end
    subgraph H3 [Physical Hazards]
        H3a[Metal Fragments from Slicer]
        H3b[Glass from Broken Bulb]
        H3c[Foreign Bodies in Bulk Grains]
    end
    subgraph CCP [Critical Control Points]
        CCP1[Receiving Temperature]
        CCP2[Display Cabinet Temperature]
        CCP3[Metal Detection]
        CCP4[Allergen Segregation Zone]
    end
    H1a --> CCP1
    H1a --> CCP2
    H1b --> CCP1
    H1c --> CCP1
    H1d --> CCP4
    H2a --> CCP4
    H2b --> CCP4
    H2c --> CCP1
    H3a --> CCP3
    H3b --> CCP3
    H3c --> CCP3
```

---

## 4.4 Retail Distribution Traceability Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Supplier] -- Lot Code --> P2[Manufacturer]
    P2 -- Batch Number --> P3[Distribution Centre]
    P3 -- Delivery Note DN --> P4[Retail Back of Store]
    P4 -- GRN Goods Received Note --> P5[Display Shelf]
    P5 -- Receipt Issued --> P6[Consumer]
    P6 -- Feedback Complaint or Return --> P4
    P4 -- Trace Back Request --> P3
    P3 -- Trace Back Request --> P2
    P2 -- Trace Back Request --> P1
```

**Purpose:** Demonstrates the **one-step-forward, one-step-back traceability** mandated by Codex (CAC/RCP 1-1969) and FSMA Section 204. This is critical for retail recall management.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Retail" in the context of HACCP. Mention two main hazards at retail. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**
Retail in HACCP terminology refers to the final stage of the food supply chain where food products are sold directly to the end consumer — including supermarkets, restaurants, caterers, and online grocery platforms. Retailers are recognized as food business operators (FBOs) under Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969 and bear legal responsibility for ensuring food remains safe at the point of sale.

**Two main hazards at retail:**
1. **Biological hazards:** Growth of *Listeria monocytogenes* in chilled ready-to-eat (RTE) foods due to temperature abuse in display cabinets (>$+4 \, °C$).
2. **Chemical hazards:** Allergen cross-contact arising from shared slicing equipment or improper segregation in deli operations (e.g., cheese on a meat slicer).

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two hazards — total 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: List the four stages of distribution where cold chain integrity can break. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**
The four stages where cold chain integrity can break during distribution are:
1. **Primary transport** (Manufacturer → Distribution Centre)
2. **Distribution Centre cross-docking** (temporary storage before onward shipment)
3. **Secondary transport** (DC → Retail store)
4. **Retail back-of-store receipt and storage** (door open for extended periods, over-stocking blocking airflow)

*(0.75 marks per stage, total 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — Part B Choice A]`**

#### (a) Describe in detail the application of the seven Codex HACCP principles at the retail level, using a "sliced cooked ham" deli product as a case study. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Principle | Retail Application (Sliced Cooked Ham) |
|---|---|
| **1. Hazard Analysis** | Identify biological (*Listeria monocytogenes*, *Salmonella*), chemical (allergen cross-contact, cleaning residues), and physical (metal from slicer blade, glass) hazards at receiving, slicing, display, and sale stages. |
| **2. Identify CCPs** | CCP-1: Receiving temperature; CCP-2: Display cabinet temperature; CCP-3: Metal detection post-slicing. |
| **3. Establish Critical Limits** | Receiving ≤ 4 °C; Display cabinet ≤ 4 °C continuously; Metal detector sensitivity: Fe ≤ 2.0 mm, Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm, SS ≤ 3.0 mm. |
| **4. Establish Monitoring** | Probe at receiving (every load); continuous data logger + manual hourly check at display; metal detector test piece at start, end, and every 30 minutes. |
| **5. Corrective Actions** | Reject loads > 4 °C at receiving; quarantine display cabinet temperature failures; segregate metal-detected rejects; investigate root cause. |
| **6. Verification** | Daily record review; weekly thermometer calibration; monthly *Listeria* swab tests; annual HACCP re-audit by qualified auditor. |
| **7. Documentation** | GRNs, temperature logs, metal detector logs, cleaning schedules, staff training records, corrective action forms, calibration certificates. |

*(1 mark per principle applied — total 7 marks)*

---

#### (b) A retail outlet receives a delivery of chilled poultry with the following hourly temperature log during transport: 3 °C, 4 °C, 5 °C, 6.5 °C, 7 °C, 6 °C, 5 °C, 4 °C. The critical limit is 4 °C. Calculate the Cold Chain Deviation Index (CCDI) and state the corrective action. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Apply the formula**

$$CCDI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert T_i - T_{limit} \vert \cdot \Delta t_i$$

**Step 2: Compute each absolute deviation** (with $\Delta t = 1 \, hr$):

| Hour | $T_i$ (°C) | $\vert T_i - 4 \vert$ (°C) | $\vert T_i - 4 \vert \cdot \Delta t$ (°C·hr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| 2 | 4.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 3 | 5.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| 4 | 6.5 | 2.5 | 2.5 |
| 5 | 7.0 | 3.0 | 3.0 |
| 6 | 6.0 | 2.0 | 2.0 |
| 7 | 5.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| 8 | 4.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 |

**Step 3: Sum**

$$CCDI = 1.0 + 0.0 + 1.0 + 2.5 + 3.0 + 2.0 + 1.0 + 0.0 = 10.5 \, °C \cdot hr$$

**Step 4: Decision**

Since $CCDI = 10.5 \, °C \cdot hr > 6 \, °C \cdot hr$, the load **fails** the acceptance threshold.

**Corrective Action (5 W's + 1 H):**
1. **Quarantine** the entire shipment in a segregated hold area.
2. **Tag** the product "HOLD — DO NOT SELL".
3. **Notify** the supplier and the QA Manager.
4. **Conduct** rapid microbiological testing for *Salmonella*, *Listeria*, total viable count, and *Campylobacter*.
5. **Issue** a Non-Conformance Report (NCR) and **request** a Root Cause Analysis (RCA) from the supplier.
6. If confirmed unsafe, **dispose** via certified waste contractor; if safe, **downgrade** to cooked-only use (e.g., ready-meal processing).

*(3 marks for CCDI calculation, 2 marks for interpretation, 2 marks for corrective action — total 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — Part B Choice B]`**

#### (a) Explain the major hazards encountered at retail food establishments. Discuss in detail the biological hazards with suitable examples and preventive measures. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Major Hazards at Retail:**
Retail food establishments face biological, chemical, and physical hazards.

**Biological Hazards (Detailed Discussion):**

1. **Bacterial Pathogens:**
   - *Listeria monocytogenes*: Grows at refrigeration temperatures (psychrotrophic); commonly found in RTE deli meats, soft cheeses, smoked fish. Preventive measure: Maintain display cabinet ≤ 4 °C, segregate raw and RTE products, conduct monthly environmental swabs.
   - *Salmonella spp.*: Common in raw poultry, eggs, and dairy. Preventive measure: Strict cooking (≥ 75 °C core), separation of raw and cooked zones, supplier CoA verification.
   - *E. coli O157:H7*: Associated with undercooked minced meat and contaminated vegetables. Preventive measure: Adequate cooking, hand-washing, sanitized cutting boards.
   - *Staphylococcus aureus*: Forms heat-stable toxins; often from food handlers with skin infections. Preventive measure: GHP, hand-washing, exclude sick staff.

2. **Viral Pathogens:**
   - **Norovirus:** Leading cause of foodborne viral illness; spread by infected handlers. Preventive measure: Strict hand-washing, exclusion policy for vomiting/diarrhoea, sanitizing surfaces with 1000 ppm chlorine.

3. **Parasites:**
   - *Toxoplasma gondii* in undercooked meat. Preventive measure: Adequate cooking to ≥ 67 °C.

4. **Spoilage Microbes:**
   - *Pseudomonas* spp. in seafood; preventive measure: Strict cold chain and short shelf life.

**Preventive Measures (Summary):**
- Strict temperature control (chill ≤ 4 °C, freeze ≤ −18 °C, hot hold ≥ 63 °C).
- Hand-washing and personal hygiene SOPs.
- Cleaning and sanitization (CIP/COP schedules).
- Supplier approval and CoA verification.
- FIFO stock rotation.
- Pest control program.
- Staff training and exclusion policy for illness.

*(4 marks for biological hazards with examples, 2 marks for preventive measures, 1 mark for concluding statement — total 7 marks)*

---

#### (b) Discuss the role of Codex Alimentarius and GHP prerequisites in ensuring food safety at the retail and distribution stage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Codex Alimentarius:**
The Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO) provides the globally recognized standard for food safety. For retail and distribution:

1. **CAC/RCP 1-1969 (Rev. 2020) — General Principles of Food Hygiene:** Establishes the foundational GHP prerequisites (Chapter IX covers retail establishments).
2. **CAC/RCP 52-2003 — Code of Hygienic Practice for Refrigerated Packaged Foods with Extended Shelf Life:** Relevant for chilled retail products.
3. **CAC/RCP 46-1999 — Code of Hygienic Practice for Refrigerated, Pre-Packaged, Unprocessed Fruits and Vegetables:** Applies to fresh produce retail.
4. **Codex principles on traceability (one-step-forward, one-step-back):** Mandated for retailers for effective recall management.

**GHP Prerequisites at Retail/Distribution (Codex Framework):**

1. **Primary Production:** Supplier approval, raw material specifications.
2. **Establishment Design and Facilities:** Hygienic layout, separation of raw and RTE zones, drainage, lighting, ventilation.
3. **Control of Operations:** Time-temperature control, allergen management, cleaning schedules, pest control.
4. **Establishment Maintenance and Sanitation:** Documented cleaning (CIP/COP), sanitation of slicers, equipment calibration.
5. **Personal Hygiene:** Hand-washing stations, gloves, hairnets, jewellery prohibition, medical certificates.
6. **Transportation:** Vehicle hygiene, pre-cooling, temperature monitoring, sealed loads.
7. **Product Information and Consumer Awareness:** Accurate labelling, allergen declaration, "use-by" dates, storage instructions.
8. **Training:** Documented HACCP and GHP training for all retail staff.
9. **Recall and Traceability:** Mock recalls every 6 months, batch traceability system.

**Conclusion:**
GHP prerequisites form the **operational foundation** upon which the seven HACCP principles are built. Without robust GHP at retail, the HACCP plan cannot function effectively. Codex provides the harmonized global benchmark, ensuring consistency across international food trade and retail operations.

*(3 marks for Codex role, 3 marks for GHP prerequisites, 1 mark for conclusion — total 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Retail & Distribution HACCP Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CCPs with GHP controls:** Students often mark *every* step as a CCP. CCPs are limited to steps where **failure results in an unacceptable health risk** and where **no downstream step eliminates the hazard**. Stock rotation and cleaning are GHP controls, NOT CCPs.
> 2. **Wrong temperature units:** Mixing °C with °F (especially in questions referencing FDA standards). Always use **°C** unless otherwise specified.
> 3. **Ignoring the cooling curve phases:** Students often mention "cool quickly" without specifying the **two-phase rule** (60 °C → 21 °C in 2 hrs; 21 °C → 4 °C in 4 hrs). This is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting CCDI units:** The Cold Chain Deviation Index must be quoted in **°C·hr** (degree-hours), not °C alone.
> 5. **Vague corrective actions:** Saying "remove the product" without specifying **quarantine, tag, notify, test, dispose** loses marks. Always use the **5 W's + 1 H** framework.
> 6. **Skipping traceability discussion:** Retail recall questions almost always require a **one-step-forward, one-step-back** traceability statement.
> 7. **Mixing up hot hold and reheat temperatures:** Hot hold is **≥ 63 °C**; reheat is **≥ 75 °C** (for 2 minutes). These are distinct critical limits.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Recall Bullets — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:**

- ☐ **Retail = last link** in the food chain; FAO/WHO classifies retailers as FBOs.
- ☐ **Distribution stages (4):** Manufacturer → DC → Secondary transport → Retail back-of-store → Display → Consumer.
- ☐ **Biological hazards at retail (MUST KNOW):** *Listeria*, *Salmonella*, *E. coli O157:H7*, *Campylobacter*, *S. aureus*, Norovirus.
- ☐ **Chemical hazards at retail:** Allergen cross-contact, cleaning residues, pesticide carry-over.
- ☐ **Physical hazards at retail:** Metal from slicers, glass, foreign bodies.
- ☐ **Critical temperatures (HACCP-critical limits):**
  - Chill ≤ **4 °C**
  - Freeze ≤ **−18 °C**
  - Hot hold ≥ **63 °C**
  - Reheat core ≥ **75 °C** (2 min)
  - Cook poultry core ≥ **74 °C**
  - Cook minced meat core ≥ **71 °C**
- ☐ **Cooling rule (two-phase):** 60 °C → 21 °C in **2 hrs**; 21 °C → 4 °C in **4 hrs**.
- ☐ **CCDI formula:** Sum of $\vert T_i - T_{limit} \vert \cdot \Delta t_i$ → units **°C·hr**.
- ☐ **CCDI thresholds:** ≤ 2 accept; 2–6 marginal; > 6 **reject**.
- ☐ **CCPs in retail (typical):** Receiving temperature, display cabinet temperature, metal detection, in-store cooking core.
- ☐ **GHP ≠ CCP:** Cleaning, pest control, staff hygiene, FIFO rotation = GHP.
- ☐ **Codex reference:** CAC/RCP 1-1969 (Rev. 2020) — Chapter IX covers retail.
- ☐ **Traceability:** "One-step-forward, one-step-back" (Codex) and FSMA Section 204.
- ☐ **Recall frequency:** Mock recalls at least every **6 months** (BRCGS Issue 9 requirement).
- ☐ **Microbiological limits (RTE):** *Listeria* ≤ 100 CFU/g; *Salmonella* absent in 25 g; TPC ≤ 10⁵–10⁶ CFU/g.
- ☐ **Metal detector sensitivity (typical deli):** Fe ≤ 2.0 mm, Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm, SS ≤ 3.0 mm.
- ☐ **Hot holding time at buffet:** Defined by local authority, typically < 2 hours at ambient.
- ☐ **Allergen segregation:** Dedicated equipment, colour-coded boards, separate storage bays.

---

**End of KTU 2024 Scheme Notes — Retail and Distribution (Module 2)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Example of Implementation of HACCP in Different Food Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT868 - HACCP PLANNING AND IMPLEMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Example of Implementation of HACCP in Different Food Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **HACCP Implementation in Food Industry** is the systematic, science-based application of the seven principles of Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP) — namely hazard analysis, CCP determination, establishing critical limits, monitoring procedures, corrective actions, verification activities, and record-keeping/documentation — within the operational, regulatory, and product-specific context of a defined food manufacturing or processing sector (e.g., dairy, meat, seafood, bakery, beverage, fruits & vegetables, ready-to-eat foods) to control food safety hazards at preventive checkpoints rather than through end-product testing.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is also referred to as **"Sector-Specific HACCP Plan Development"** — the translation of the generic Codex Alimentarius HACCP model into a customized food safety management system (FSMS) aligned with **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)**, **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)**, **ISO 22000:2018**, and **BRC/IFS Global Standards**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** Every food industry implements HACCP differently because the **hazard profile**, **process flow**, **CCP matrix**, and **critical limits** are unique to the product matrix, processing technology, and consumer risk exposure.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of HACCP implementation in different food industries like **installing different security systems in different types of buildings**:

- A **bank** (like the meat industry) needs vaults, armed guards, and biometric locks because the "valuables" (animal carcasses) are at high risk of *Salmonella*, *E. coli*, and *Listeria*.
- A **hospital pharmacy** (like the dairy industry) needs temperature-controlled rooms and double-checking systems because the "medicines" (milk) are vulnerable to *Mycobacterium bovis*, *Brucella*, and rapid spoilage.
- A **swimming pool** (like a beverage plant) needs pH monitors and filtration because the "water" (juice/soft drinks) is prone to chemical contamination and microbial growth in dilute form.
- A **children's playground** (like ready-to-eat foods) needs constant supervision because the "users" (immunocompromised consumers) cannot defend themselves against even small hazards.

In each case, the **principles of security (HACCP)** are the same — *identify the threat, locate the checkpoints, set limits, monitor, act, verify, and document* — but the **specific checkpoints, limits, and threats are customized** to the building (industry).

> [!TIP]
> **Student Memory Trick — "HACCP = H.A.C.C.P.D.V.R."**
> **H**azard Analysis → **A**ssess CCPs → **C**ritical Limits → **C**orrective Actions → **P**rocedures (Monitoring) → **D**ocumentation → **V**erification → **R**ecords

---

## 1.3 Why Industry-Specific Implementation Matters

> [!WARNING]
> A **"one-size-fits-all" HACCP plan** is a **NON-COMPLIANCE** under FSSAI Schedule 4 (2011, amended 2018) and Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969. Each food business operator (FBO) must develop a **product-process matrix-specific** HACCP plan.

The seven Codex principles remain universal, but the **12 logical steps** of implementation (5 preliminary tasks + 7 principles) yield different outputs for:

| Industry Sector | Primary Hazard Dominance | Dominant Control Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Meat & Poultry** | Biological (Pathogens) | Thermal lethality + Cold chain |
| **Dairy** | Biological + Chemical (Antibiotics, Aflatoxin M1) | Pasteurization + Cold chain |
| **Seafood** | Biological (Histamine, *V. cholerae*, *V. parahaemolyticus*) | Time-Temperature + Sanitation |
| **Bakery** | Biological (Spore-formers in bread) + Physical (Metal/Glass) | Baking lethality + Metal detection |
| **Beverages** | Chemical (Pesticides, Mycotoxins) + Biological | Pasteurization + Formulation control |
| **Fruits & Vegetables** | Chemical (Pesticide residues) + Biological (*E. coli* O157:H7) | Washing + pH control + Cold chain |
| **Ready-to-Eat (RTE)** | Biological (Listeria, *Salmonella*) + Cross-contamination | High-pressure processing / Hurdle technology |
| **Canned Foods** | Biological (*C. botulinum*) | Commercial sterilization (F₀ concept) |

This sectoral differentiation forms the **core competency expected of a KTU 2024 PEFTT868 graduate** — to be able to design, audit, and verify a HACCP plan for any given food industry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Universal 12-Step Codex HACCP Implementation Sequence

Regardless of industry, the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)** mandates a 12-step implementation sequence. Understanding this is fundamental to KTU Module 2.

### Preliminary Steps (Steps 0–5):

1. **Step 0 — Assemble the HACCP Team**: Multidisciplinary team with expertise in food science, production, sanitation, engineering, and quality assurance.
2. **Step 1 — Describe the Product and Its Distribution**: Full product specification (composition, pH, a_w, intended consumer, shelf-life, storage conditions).
3. **Step 2 — Identify the Intended Use and Consumers**: General public, infants, immunocompromised, hospital patients, etc.
4. **Step 3 — Construct the Process Flow Diagram**: From raw material reception to consumer end-use.
5. **Step 4 — Verify the Process Flow Diagram On-Site**: Cross-check the diagram with actual plant operations.
6. **Step 5 — List All Potential Hazards, Conduct Hazard Analysis, and Determine Control Measures** *(Principle 1)*.

### Seven HACCP Principles (Steps 6–12):

7. **Step 6 — Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs)** *(Principle 2 — CCP Decision Tree: Q1→Q2→Q3→Q4 from Codex)*.
8. **Step 7 — Establish Critical Limits for Each CCP** *(Principle 3)*.
9. **Step 8 — Establish Monitoring Procedures** *(Principle 4)*.
10. **Step 9 — Establish Corrective Actions** *(Principle 5)*.
11. **Step 10 — Establish Verification Procedures** *(Principle 6)*.
12. **Step 11 — Establish Documentation and Record-Keeping** *(Principle 7 — converted from previous step numbering)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always write the **Codex CCP Decision Tree (Q1–Q4)** in your answer. It is a guaranteed 2-mark bonus in any Part A question on HACCP implementation.

---

## 2.2 The Codex CCP Decision Tree (Universal Tool)

This decision tree is the **analytical engine** used in *every* food industry to determine whether a process step is a CCP:

- **Q1**: Do control preventive measures exist for the identified hazard? → **YES → Q2** | **NO → Modify process / formulate control measure (NOT a CCP)**
- **Q2**: Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the likely occurrence of the hazard to an acceptable level? → **YES → CCP** | **NO → Q3**
- **Q3**: Could contamination with the identified hazard(s) occur in excess of acceptable level(s), or could these increase to unacceptable level(s)? → **YES → Q4** | **NO → NOT a CCP**
- **Q4**: Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard to an acceptable level? → **YES → NOT a CCP** | **NO → CCP**

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Decision Tree:** **"Prevent? Eliminate? Accumulate? Downstream-Eliminate?"** — a **PEPED** walkthrough.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since HACCP is a science-based quantitative system, several mathematical relationships govern the critical limits, especially in thermally processed foods. The following table consolidates the key formulas used in industry-specific HACCP plans.

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 2.1: Critical Formulas for HACCP Implementation Across Food Industries**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Industry Application | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Water Activity (a_w) Limit for Microbial Growth** | $a_w \geq 0.60$ (minimum for any microbial growth); Pathogens inhibited below $a_w < 0.86$ | Bakery, Dried foods, Intermediate Moisture Foods | Dimensionless ratio |
| **pH Limit for *C. botulinum* Inhibition** | $pH < 4.6$ inhibits *C. botulinum* toxin formation | Acidified foods, Beverages, Canned fruits | pH units (0–14) |
| **F₀ Value (Thermal Process Lethality)** | $F_0 = 10^{(T - 121.1)/z} \cdot \Delta t$ | Canned foods, Retort sterilization | Minutes (min) |
| **D-Value (Decimal Reduction Time)** | $D = t / \log(N_0 / N)$ | All thermal processes | Minutes (min) |
| **z-Value (Thermal Death Time Curve Slope)** | $z = (T_2 - T_1) / \log(D_1 / D_2)$ | Thermal processing | °C |
| **Pasteurization Equivalent (Cu)** | $P = 10^{(T - 60)/7.2} \cdot \Delta t$ | Milk pasteurization, Egg products | Minutes (min) |
| **Lethal Rate (L)** | $L = 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z}$ | Canning, HTST | Dimensionless |
| **Bacteriological Count Reduction (log)** | $\log(N/N_0) = -t/D$ | All microbial inactivation steps | log CFU/g |
| **Sanitizer Concentration Check** | $\text{Conc}_{actual} / \text{Conc}_{target} \times 100\%$ | All industries (sanitation CCP) | % (typically 50–200 ppm chlorine) |
| **Cold Chain Critical Limit** | $T \leq 4^\circ C$ (chilled), $T \leq -18^\circ C$ (frozen) | Meat, Dairy, Seafood, RTE | °C |
| **Metal Detection Limit** | $Fe \leq 2.0 \, mm$, $Non\text{-}Fe \leq 2.5 \, mm$, $SS \leq 3.0 \, mm$ | Bakery, RTE, Confectionery | mm sphere |
| **Hurdle Technology Index (HTI)** | $HTI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log\left(\frac{1}{f_i}\right)$ | RTE, Minimally processed foods | Dimensionless |
| **Process Capability Index (CpK)** | $C_{pK} = \min\left[\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ | All CCP monitoring (validation) | Dimensionless (≥ 1.33 preferred) |
| **Statistical Process Control (SPC) Chart Limits** | $UCL = \bar{X} + 3\sigma, \quad LCL = \bar{X} - 3\sigma$ | Verification of CCPs | Process units |
| **Acceptance Probability (Sampling Plan)** | $P_a(n,c,p) = \sum_{k=0}^{c} \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$ | Verification sampling (ISO 2859-1) | Probability (0–1) |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX/Markdown Isolation Rule:** All equations above are displayed in math mode. Always leave **one blank line** before and after every `$$ ... $$` block when typing your KTU exam answers.

---

## 2.4 Why This Framework Matters in Engineering Practice

For a KTU 2024 graduate in Food Technology, the practical utility of mastering these formulas is multifold:

1. **Auditing Competency** — FSSAI auditors and third-party certification bodies (e.g., BRC, FSSC 22000) require validation of critical limits using these exact formulas.
2. **Product Development** — New product launches require a HACCP plan with mathematically justified critical limits.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — FSSAI Schedule 4 mandates quantitative justification of all CCPs.
4. **Global Employability** — ISO 22000, BRC v9, IFS, SQF — all demand these formulas for FSMS certification.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world industrial example:** In a commercial sterilization (retort) process for canned mushroom curry, the **F₀ target** is typically **F₀ ≥ 3.0 minutes** at a reference temperature of **121.1 °C** with **z = 10 °C** for *C. botulinum* spores. The CCP critical limit is set, monitored by a calibrated thermocouple (every 5 minutes), and verified annually by biological validation using inoculated packs.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Industry-Wise Step-by-Step HACCP Implementation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "derivation" in this module is not algebraic but procedural** — we derive the **CCP matrix** logically through the Codex Decision Tree for each industry. This is exactly how a KTU examiner expects the answer to be structured in a 14-mark question.

### CASE STUDY 1: HACCP Implementation in the **Meat & Poultry Industry** (Chicken Slaughterhouse & Further Processing)

**Product:** Whole chicken carcass → Chilled chicken cuts → Frozen chicken nuggets
**Process Flow:**

Raw Material Reception → Stunning → Slaughter → Bleeding → Scalding (52–58 °C, 3 min) → De-feathering → Evisceration → Chilling (Spin-chiller, ≤ 4 °C) → Grading → Cut-up → Deboning → Marination → Breading → Pre-frying (180 °C, 30 s) → Flash freezing (-35 °C) → Metal Detection → Packing → Cold Storage (-18 °C) → Distribution.

**Hazard Analysis & CCP Determination (Step-by-Step):**

- **Step 5 (Hazard Analysis):**
  - *Biological*: *Salmonella* spp., *Campylobacter jejuni*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *E. coli* O157:H7, *Staphylococcus aureus*.
  - *Chemical*: Antibiotic residues, pesticide residues, heavy metals (Pb, Cd).
  - *Physical*: Bone fragments, feather remnants, metal pieces from machinery.
- **Step 6 (CCP Determination using Decision Tree):**
  - **Step: Spin-chilling** → Q1: Preventive measures exist (chlorine 50 ppm)? YES → Q2: Designed to reduce pathogen load? YES (water chilling with chlorine reduces *Campylobacter* by ~1 log) → **CCP-1B**: *Salmonella* / *Campylobacter* control.
  - **Step: Pre-frying (180 °C)** → Q1: YES (temperature control) → Q2: Designed to eliminate pathogens? YES (surface lethality > 5 log) → **CCP-2B**: Thermal lethality.
  - **Step: Metal Detection** → Q1: YES (detector installed) → Q2: Eliminate metal hazard? YES → **CCP-3P**: Physical metal fragment control.
- **Step 7 (Critical Limits):**
  - CCP-1B: Spin-chiller water temperature $\leq 4^\circ C$, free chlorine $\geq 50$ ppm, pH $6.5$–$7.5$.
  - CCP-2B: Oil temperature $\geq 175^\circ C$, dwell time $\geq 30$ s, internal product temperature $\geq 72^\circ C$ for 15 s.
  - CCP-3P: Fe $\leq 2.0$ mm, Non-Fe $\leq 2.5$ mm, SS $\leq 3.0$ mm.
- **Step 8 (Monitoring):**
  - Continuous temperature loggers (every 1 min) + manual probe check every 1 h.
  - Free chlorine: DPD colorimetric test, every 1 h.
  - Metal detector: Test piece verification at start, mid, and end of each shift.
- **Step 9 (Corrective Actions):**
  - Spin-chill temperature deviation > 4 °C: Hold product, re-chill, lab retest for *Salmonella* / *Campylobacter*.
  - Oil temperature drop < 175 °C: Adjust thermostat, re-fry affected batch.
  - Metal detector reject: Quarantine batch, re-inspect, segregate, reprocess.
- **Step 10 (Verification):**
  - Daily: End-product microbiological testing (*Salmonella* n=5, *Campylobacter* n=5).
  - Weekly: Equipment calibration of thermocouples and metal detector.
  - Monthly: Internal audit by QA team.
  - Annually: Third-party audit (FSSC 22000 / BRC).
- **Step 11 (Records):**
  - CCP monitoring logs, calibration records, deviation reports, corrective action records, training records, supplier COAs, lab reports.

---

### CASE STUDY 2: HACCP Implementation in the **Dairy Industry** (Pasteurized Milk Processing)

**Process Flow:**

Raw Milk Reception → Pre-heating (40 °C) → Centrifugal Clarification → Standardization (3.5% fat, 8.5% SNF) → Homogenization (2000 psi, 65 °C) → HTST Pasteurization (72 °C, 15 s) → Cooling (≤ 4 °C) → Storage in Silo → Aseptic Packaging (6-layer) → Cold Storage (4 °C) → Distribution.

**Hazard Analysis:**

- *Biological*: *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, *Brucella abortus*, *Coxiella burnetii*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Salmonella* Typhimurium, *E. coli*, *Staph. aureus* enterotoxin.
- *Chemical*: Antibiotic residues (β-lactams, tetracyclines), Aflatoxin M1, pesticides, heavy metals, sanitizers (quaternary ammonium compounds).
- *Physical*: Hair, dirt, glass, metal fragments.

**CCP Determination:**

- **HTST Pasteurization** → Q1: YES (flow controller + temperature recorder) → Q2: YES (designed for *Coxiella* / *Mycobacterium* 5-log reduction) → **CCP-1B**.
- **Aseptic Packaging Zone** → Q1: YES (air filtration, sanitation) → Q2: NO → Q3: YES (post-process recontamination possible) → Q4: NO (no downstream kill step) → **CCP-2B (Post-process recontamination control)**.
- **Metal Detection (Packed Cartons)** → Q1: YES → Q2: YES → **CCP-3P**.

**Critical Limits:**

- CCP-1B: **72 °C / 15 s** HTST (FSSAI Schedule-IV); diversion valve activates at $< 72^\circ C$ for $\geq 1$ s; legal reference: $P \geq 1$ min equivalent.
  - Validation: $P = \int 10^{(T-60)/7.2} \, dt \geq 1$ minute
- CCP-2B: Packaging room air $\leq 100$ CFU/m³, surface swab *Listeria* absent in 5 samples.
- CCP-3P: Same as meat industry.

**Why this works:** The HTST lethality equation guarantees $> 5$-log reduction of the most heat-resistant vegetative pathogen (*Coxiella burnetii*). The integrated diversion valve (FDV) automatically diverts sub-legal flow back to the regenerator — a real-time **corrective-action mechanism**.

---

### CASE STUDY 3: HACCP Implementation in the **Seafood Industry** (Frozen Shrimp Processing)

**Process Flow:**

Harvest (Boat) → Icing on Board (0 °C ice) → Landing → Receiving (≤ 4 °C) → Washing → Deheading → Peeling → Deveining → Size Grading → Glazing (water + sodium tripolyphosphate) → IQF Freezing (-35 °C) → Metal Detection → Packing → Cold Storage (-25 °C) → Export.

**Hazard Analysis:**

- *Biological*: *Vibrio parahaemolyticus*, *V. cholerae*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*, **Histamine** (biogenic amine from *Morganella morganii* / *Klebsiella*), *Anisakis* (parasite).
- *Chemical*: Heavy metals (Cd, Hg), veterinary drug residues (chloramphenicol, nitrofurans — **banned in EU/USA**), histamine.
- *Physical**: Shell fragments, hooks.

**CCPs:**

- **CCP-1B — Receiving / Icing**: Core temperature $\leq 4^\circ C$, histamine $\leq 50$ mg/kg (EU limit).
- **CCP-2B — IQF Freezing**: Tunnel temperature $-35^\circ C$, product exit temperature $\leq -18^\circ C$ within 90 min — controls *Anisakis* larvae and reduces *Vibrio* load.
- **CCP-3C — Chemical Residue Control**: Test every batch for chloramphenicol, nitrofurans, heavy metals (LC-MS/MS).

**Critical Limit Equations:**

Histamine accumulation kinetics in improperly chilled tuna/shrimp follows zero-order kinetics:

$$
C_t = C_0 + k \cdot t
$$

where $C_t$ is histamine concentration at time $t$ (mg/kg), $C_0$ is initial histamine (mg/kg), $k$ is the histamine formation rate constant ($\approx 100$ mg/kg/h for *Morganella morganii* at $25^\circ C$).

For safe limit $C_t \leq 100 \, mg/kg$ (EU):
$$
t_{max} = \frac{(100 - C_0)}{k}
$$

If $C_0 = 10 \, mg/kg$ and $k = 100 \, mg/kg/h$:
$$
t_{max} = \frac{(100 - 10)}{100} = 0.9 \, h = 54 \, minutes
$$

This mathematically justifies the **critical limit of 4 °C core temperature within 30 minutes of catch** for histamine control.

---

### CASE STUDY 4: HACCP Implementation in the **Bakery Industry** (Bread Manufacturing)

**Process Flow:**

Flour Receiving → Sifting → Weighing → Mixing (with yeast, sugar, salt, water) → First Proofing (28 °C, 1 h) → Punching → Second Proofing (35 °C, 1 h) → Baking (220 °C, 25 min) → Cooling → Slicing → Metal Detection → Packing → Distribution.

**Hazard Analysis:**

- *Biological*: *Bacillus cereus* spores (flour-borne), *Salmonella* (raw eggs in cake variant), *Staph. aureus* (handler hygiene), rope-forming spore-formers.
- *Chemical*: Mycotoxins (Aflatoxin B1 in maize flour, Ochratoxin A), potassium bromate (banned in India since 2016).
- *Physical*: Metal fragments from sifter, glass, stones.

**CCPs:**

- **CCP-1B — Baking**: Internal crumb temperature $\geq 95^\circ C$ for $\geq 1$ min — kills vegetative pathogens and most *Bacillus* spores.
  - Critical limit validation: D-value of *B. cereus* spores in bread is $\sim 5$ min at $95^\circ C$; with z-value $8^\circ C$:
  
$$
\log(N_0 / N) = \frac{95 - 90}{8} \times \frac{1}{5} = 0.125 \, log \text{ reduction}
$$
  
  While 0.125 log is modest, the *Salmonella* D-value at $95^\circ C$ is $0.5$ min, yielding a $\sim 2$ log reduction, sufficient for **acceptable risk in low-moisture bakery products**.

- **CCP-2P — Metal Detection**: Fe $\leq 1.5$ mm (bakery has tighter limit due to consumer complaint severity).
- **CCP-3C — Mycotoxin Control**: Incoming flour COA — Aflatoxin B1 $\leq 30 \, \mu g/kg$ (FSSAI limit).

---

### CASE STUDY 5: HACCP Implementation in the **Beverage Industry** (Fruit Juice Pasteurization)

**Process Flow:**

Fruit Reception → Washing → Sorting → Juice Extraction → Filtration → Standardization → De-aeration → Pasteurization (95 °C, 30 s) → Cooling → Aseptic Filling → Storage → Distribution.

**Hazard Analysis:**

- *Biological*: *E. coli* O157:H7, *Salmonella*, *Cryptosporidium*, *Cyclospora* (water-borne), molds producing Patulin.
- *Chemical*: Pesticide residues, Patulin ($\leq 50 \, \mu g/kg$ FSSAI), heavy metals, cleaning chemicals.

**CCPs:**

- **CCP-1B — Pasteurization**: $95^\circ C$ for $30$ s; pH of juice $< 4.6$ (acid foods) — **inhibits *C. botulinum***.
- **CCP-2C — Pesticide Residue**: Multi-residue LC-MS/MS on every batch.
- **CCP-3P — Aseptic Filler**: Aseptic chamber HEPA filter integrity $\geq 99.97\%$ at $0.3 \, \mu m$, fill accuracy check.

**Verification of Pasteurization Lethality:**

For orange juice (pH 3.8) targeting $> 5$-log reduction of *E. coli* (D=0.3 min at 60 °C, z=5 °C):

$$
\log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{95 - 60}{5} \times \frac{0.5}{0.3} = 11.67
$$

This is **highly conservative** — confirming that $95^\circ C/30s$ provides a $> 5$ log safety factor for *E. coli*, well above the $5$-log performance criterion mandated by **FDA Juice HACCP regulation (21 CFR 120)** and **FSSAI Fruit Products Order, 2017**.

---

### 3.2 Consolidated Analytical Matrix — Industry Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 3.1: Industry-Wise HACCP Implementation Comparison Matrix**

| Parameter | Meat & Poultry | Dairy | Seafood | Bakery | Beverages | Canned Foods |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of CCPs (typical)** | 3–5 | 3–4 | 3–5 | 2–3 | 3–4 | 3–5 |
| **Dominant Hazard** | Biological | Biological+Chemical | Biological+Chemical | Biological+Physical | Chemical+Biological | Biological (*C. botulinum*) |
| **Primary Lethal Step** | Scalding/Pre-frying | HTST Pasteurization | IQF Freezing | Baking | Pasteurization | Retort (F₀) |
| **Critical Temperature** | $\geq 72^\circ C$ (pre-fry) | $72^\circ C / 15s$ | $\leq -18^\circ C$ (final) | $\geq 95^\circ C$ (crumb) | $95^\circ C / 30s$ | $F_0 \geq 3.0$ min |
| **Cold Chain Limit** | $\leq 4^\circ C$ | $\leq 4^\circ C$ | $\leq 4^\circ C$ | Ambient | $\leq 4^\circ C$ (chilled juice) | Ambient (sterile) |
| **Key Standard** | FSSAI Schedule-IV | FSSAI/MMPO | EU 853/2004 | FSSAI Std 4.1.1 | 21 CFR 120 | FSSAI Std 2.1 |
| **Verification Test** | *Salmonella*/*Campylobacter* | *Coliform*/*APC* | Histamine/*Vibrio* | *B. cereus*/Rope | Patulin/Pesticide | Commercial sterility |
| **Special Concern** | Fecal contamination | Aflatoxin M1 | Histamine | Bromate ban | Patulin | Botulinum risk |

This **comparative matrix** is a **guaranteed 7-mark answer** in any KTU 2024 question asking "Compare HACCP implementation in two different food industries."

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Universal HACCP Implementation Flow

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **Codex 12-step implementation sequence** as a block-level functional flow, applicable across all food industries.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: FBO Commits to HACCP] --> A1[Step 0: Assemble HACCP Team]
    A1 --> A2[Step 1: Describe Product and Distribution]
    A2 --> A3[Step 2: Identify Intended Use and Consumers]
    A3 --> A4[Step 3: Construct Process Flow Diagram]
    A4 --> A5[Step 4: Verify Process Flow On-Site]
    A5 --> B1[Step 5: PRINCIPLE 1 - Hazard Analysis and Control Measures]
    B1 --> B2{Step 6: PRINCIPLE 2 - CCP Decision Tree Q1 to Q4}
    B2 -- Q1 NO --> B1
    B2 -- Q2 YES --> B3[CCP Identified]
    B2 -- Q3 NO --> B4[Not a CCP]
    B2 -- Q4 YES --> B4
    B3 --> C1[Step 7: PRINCIPLE 3 - Establish Critical Limits]
    C1 --> C2[Step 8: PRINCIPLE 4 - Monitoring Procedures]
    C2 --> C3[Step 9: PRINCIPLE 5 - Corrective Actions]
    C3 --> C4[Step 10: PRINCIPLE 6 - Verification Activities]
    C4 --> C5[Step 11: PRINCIPLE 7 - Documentation and Records]
    C5 --> D1[End: HACCP Plan Implemented and Audited]
    B4 --> B1
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram:** Every food industry follows this exact path. The "branching" at Step 6 reflects the **Codex CCP Decision Tree (Q1–Q4)** — the only decision point in the entire HACCP logic.

## 4.3 CCP Decision Tree Diagram (Codex Standard)

This is the most exam-relevant diagram — reproduce it in any answer worth 5+ marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Identified Hazard at Process Step] --> Q1{Q1: Do preventive control measures exist?}
    Q1 -- NO --> MOD[Modify process to introduce control - NOT a CCP at this step]
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Q2: Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce hazard to acceptable level?}
    Q2 -- YES --> CCP1[YES - This is a CCP]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Q3: Could hazard accumulate to unacceptable level at this step?}
    Q3 -- NO --> NOTCCP1[NOT a CCP]
    Q3 -- YES --> Q4{Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the hazard?}
    Q4 -- YES --> NOTCCP2[NOT a CCP]
    Q4 -- NO --> CCP2[YES - This is a CCP]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```
