## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the seven principles of HACCP as per Codex Alimentarius Commission.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The seven principles of HACCP according to **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)** are:

1. **Principle 1:** Conduct a hazard analysis and determine control measures.
2. **Principle 2:** Determine the Critical Control Points (CCPs).
3. **Principle 3:** Establish critical limit(s) for each CCP.
4. **Principle 4:** Establish a system to monitor control of the CCP.
5. **Principle 5:** Establish the corrective action to be taken when monitoring indicates that a particular CCP is not under control.
6. **Principle 6:** Establish procedures for verification to confirm that the HACCP system is working effectively.
7. **Principle 7:** Establish documentation concerning all procedures and records appropriate to these principles and their application.

*(Full marks for listing all seven principles in correct sequence.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Why is the HACCP plan for a seafood industry different from that of a bakery industry? Give two key reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The HACCP plan differs between seafood and bakery industries due to the following reasons:

1. **Hazard Profile Difference:** Seafood is dominated by **biological hazards (histamine, *Vibrio*, *Salmonella*) and chemical residues (chloramphenicol, nitrofurans)**, while bakery is dominated by **biological spore-formers (*B. cereus*) and physical hazards (metal fragments)**. Consequently, the **CCP matrices are different**.

2. **Critical Control Limits Difference:** Seafood requires a **chemical residue CCP (LC-MS/MS test)** for export compliance with EU/USA markets, while bakery requires a **physical metal-detection CCP with tighter limits (Fe $\leq 1.5$ mm)** because of higher consumer complaint sensitivity and lower moisture content allowing pathogen survival.

*(Each correctly explained reason = 1.5 marks. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Part B questions offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both are provided below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Likely Pattern]`

**Q: Develop a complete HACCP plan for a Pasteurized Milk Processing Plant. List any 5 CCPs, their critical limits, monitoring procedures, corrective actions, and verification activities. (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Product Description, Process Flow & Hazard Analysis (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Product Description:** Pasteurized, homogenized, standardized cow milk (3.5% fat, 8.5% SNF), packed in 1 L tetra pak / 500 mL HDPE pouches, shelf-life 7 days at $\leq 4^\circ C$.

**Intended Consumer:** General public including children and elderly.

**Process Flow (11 steps):**
Raw Milk Reception → Pre-heating (40 °C) → Clarification → Standardization → Homogenization (2000 psi, 65 °C) → HTST Pasteurization (72 °C / 15 s) → Cooling (≤ 4 °C) → Storage in insulated silo → Aseptic Packing → Cold Storage (4 °C) → Distribution.

**Hazard Analysis Matrix:**

| Process Step | Hazard Type | Specific Hazard | Control Measure | CCP? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Receiving | Biological | *Salmonella*, *E. coli* | Reject if TTC > 5 × 10⁵ CFU/mL | No (screening) |
| Receiving | Chemical | Antibiotic residue | Delvo test on every tanker | Yes — CCP-1C |
| HTST Pasteurization | Biological | *Coxiella burnetii*, *M. tuberculosis* | Time-Temp control | Yes — CCP-2B |
| Aseptic Packing | Biological | *Listeria* recontamination | Air filtration, sanitization | Yes — CCP-3B |
| Packing | Physical | Metal fragment | Metal detector | Yes — CCP-4P |
| Cold Storage | Biological | *Psychrotrophs* growth | Temperature ≤ 4 °C | Yes — CCP-5B |

*(7 marks distributed as: Product description — 1, Process flow — 1.5, Hazard analysis table — 3, Decision tree logic — 1.5.)*

#### **Part (b) — Critical Limits, Monitoring, Corrective Actions, Verification, Records (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| CCP | Critical Limit | Monitoring | Frequency | Corrective Action | Verification | Records |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CCP-1C** Antibiotic residue | **Negative** Delvo test | Test kit | Every tanker (100%) | Reject tanker, supplier claim | Daily: positive control test | Delvo log, supplier COA |
| **CCP-2B** HTST | **72 °C / 15 s** | Temp recorder + flow diversion | Continuous + 1 h manual | Divert to raw silo, retest, reprocess | Calibration weekly; phosphatase test daily | Chart, calibration, lab log |
| **CCP-3B** Aseptic zone | Air ≤ 100 CFU/m³; surface *Listeria* absent in 5 swabs | Air sampler, swab test | Daily / Weekly | Stop packing, re-sanitize, retest | Monthly: full sanitation audit | Air-sample log, swab results |
| **CCP-4P** Metal detection | Fe ≤ 2.0 mm, Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm, SS ≤ 3.0 mm | Detector with auto-reject | Every pouch | Quarantine, re-inspect | Test pieces start/mid/end shift | Detector log, reject log |
| **CCP-5B** Cold storage | **Temperature ≤ 4 °C** | Data logger | Continuous | Move stock, assess shelf-life | Calibration monthly; product T check 4 h | Temperature chart, calibration |

**Verification Activities:** Daily phosphatase test, weekly *Coliform*/APC testing, monthly environmental swabs, annual third-party FSSC 22000 audit, annual equipment re-calibration.

**Records:** All CCP charts, lab reports, calibration certificates, training records, supplier COAs, internal audit reports, management review minutes.

*(7 marks distributed as: Critical limits — 2, Monitoring — 1.5, Corrective actions — 1.5, Verification — 1, Records — 1.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Compare and contrast the implementation of HACCP in the Meat Industry and the Seafood Industry with specific reference to (a) hazard profile and (b) CCP determination. (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Comparison of Hazard Profile (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Meat & Poultry Industry | Seafood Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **Dominant Biological Hazards** | *Salmonella* spp., *Campylobacter jejuni*, *L. monocytogenes*, *E. coli* O157:H7, *Clostridium perfringens* | *Vibrio parahaemolyticus*, *V. cholerae*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*, *Anisakis simplex* (parasite) |
| **Dominant Chemical Hazards** | Antibiotic residues, pesticides, heavy metals (Pb, Cd), beta-agonists | Histamine (biogenic amine), chloramphenicol (banned), nitrofurans (banned), heavy metals (Hg, Cd) |
| **Dominant Physical Hazards** | Bone fragments, feather remnants, metal pieces from knives/machinery | Shell fragments, fish hooks, metal from processing |
| **Cross-contamination Risk** | High (fecal → carcass) — requires strict process separation | Moderate-high (gut → flesh) — requires careful handling |
| **Source of Raw Material** | Terrestrial farm animals (controlled environment) | Wild catch + aquaculture — variable quality |
| **Regulatory Specificity** | FSSAI Schedule-IV, BIS 2535 | EU Regulation 853/2004, FDA Seafood HACCP, FSSAI Schedule-IV |

**Key Difference:** The seafood industry has a **unique chemical hazard — histamine** — which is **not a concern in the meat industry**. Conversely, the meat industry faces a **parasitic/zoonotic concern (Toxoplasma, Trichinella)** in pork, which is **less common in seafood** (though *Anisakis* is a similar concern).

#### **Part (b) — Comparison of CCP Determination (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Process Step | Meat Industry CCP | Seafood Industry CCP | Reason for Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Receiving** | Antemortem inspection (CCP-1B for fecal contamination) | Histamine test (CCP-1C, ≤ 50 mg/kg) | Histamine is unique to scombroid fish (tuna, mackerel) |
| **Chilling / Cold Chain** | Spin-chiller ≤ 4 °C (CCP-2B) | Icing on board + receiving ≤ 4 °C (CCP-2B) | Both require cold chain, but seafood requires faster chilling due to histamine kinetics |
| **Thermal / Lethal Step** | Scalding (52–58 °C) + Pre-frying (≥ 72 °C core) — CCP-3B | Cooking (for value-added products) ≥ 63 °C — CCP-3B | Meat requires surface lethality for *E. coli*; seafood for *Vibrio* |
| **Chemical Residue** | Antibiotic test (CCP-4C) | LC-MS/MS for chloramphenicol + nitrofurans + heavy metals (CCP-4C) | Seafood is a major export commodity with strict EU/USA chemical residue regulations |
| **Metal Detection** | Fe ≤ 2.0 mm, Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm, SS ≤ 3.0 mm (CCP-5P) | Fe ≤ 2.0 mm, Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm, SS ≤ 3.0 mm (CCP-5P) | Similar limits; both industries use similar equipment |
| **Freezing** | Optional for fresh meat | IQF ≤ −35 °C → product ≤ −18 °C (CCP-6B for parasite control) | Freezing is **mandatory** in seafood to control *Anisakis* in EU markets |

**Conclusion:** While both industries follow the **same 7 HACCP principles and 12 implementation steps**, the **number, type, and critical limits of CCPs differ markedly** due to differences in raw material origin, biological/chemical hazard profile, regulatory requirements (especially export), and end-use risk.

*(7 marks distributed as: Table for CCP comparison — 3, Explanation of differences — 2, Justification of decisions using Codex tree — 1.5, Conclusion — 0.5.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Skipping the Codex Decision Tree (Q1–Q4)** — Always show the decision tree logic when determining a CCP. Without it, the examiner cannot award full marks for CCP identification.
> 2. **Conflating CCPs with Quality Control Points (QCPs)** — A CCP is a step where **food safety** is controlled. A QCP is for quality (e.g., color, taste). The examiner will deduct 1–2 marks if you mark a QCP as a CCP.
> 3. **Generic Critical Limits** — Writing "Temperature" without specifying **°C and time** is a half-answer. Always give both **value and tolerance** (e.g., $72 \pm 0.5^\circ C$ for $\geq 15$ s).
> 4. **No Monitoring Frequency** — "Monitored continuously" is vague. Specify **"Datalogger every 1 minute + manual probe every 1 hour"**.
> 5. **Missing the F₀ Concept in Canning** — If asked about canned food HACCP, **always derive F₀ ≥ 3.0 min at z = 10 °C** for *C. botulinum*.
> 6. **No Legal/Standard Reference** — FSSAI Schedule-IV, Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969, ISO 22000:2018 — citing at least one standard adds professional credibility.
> 7. **No Validation/Verification** — Distinguish: **Validation** (initial proof of adequacy) vs. **Verification** (ongoing confirmation). Examiners love this distinction.
> 8. **Ignoring Records** — "Record-keeping" is **Principle 7**. Always list at least 5 specific records (CCP charts, lab reports, calibration certificates, training records, supplier COAs).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **HACCP = 7 Principles + 12 Implementation Steps** (5 preliminary + 7 principles).
- **CCPs are determined using the Codex Decision Tree:** Q1 (Preventive measure exists?) → Q2 (Designed to eliminate hazard?) → Q3 (Hazard accumulates?) → Q4 (Subsequent step eliminates?).
- **Three types of CCPs:** CCP-1 (eliminates hazard, e.g., retort), CCP-2 (reduces hazard, e.g., pasteurization), CCP-3 (prevents recontamination, e.g., metal detection / aseptic zone).
- **Industry-specific differences** arise from **hazard profile, raw material, regulatory environment, and end-use risk**.
- **Meat industry:** Scalding (52–58 °C) + Pre-frying (≥ 72 °C) + Spin-chilling (≤ 4 °C) + Metal detection.
- **Dairy industry:** HTST (72 °C / 15 s) + Antibiotic test (Delvo) + Aseptic packaging + Cold chain.
- **Seafood industry:** Histamine limit (≤ 50 mg/kg EU) + IQF freezing (≤ −18 °C) + LC-MS/MS for banned antibiotics.
- **Bakery industry:** Baking (≥ 95 °C crumb) + Mycotoxin control (Aflatoxin B1 ≤ 30 μg/kg) + Metal detection (Fe ≤ 1.5 mm).
- **Beverage industry:** Pasteurization (95 °C / 30 s for juice) + Patulin limit (≤ 50 μg/kg) + Pesticide residue + Aseptic fill.
- **Canned foods:** F₀ ≥ 3.0 min at z = 10 °C for *C. botulinum* (12D reduction).
- **Common equations to remember:**
  - $F_0 = 10^{(T-121.1)/z} \cdot \Delta t$
  - $\log(N_0 / N) = t / D$
  - $C_t = C_0 + k \cdot t$ (histamine kinetics)
  - $P = \int 10^{(T-60)/7.2} \, dt$ (pasteurization equivalent)
  - $HTI = \sum \log(1/f_i)$ (hurdle technology index)
- **Critical limits must be measurable, validated, and legally defensible** (FSSAI / Codex / ISO 22000).
- **Verification ≠ Validation:** Validation is **once-only** (initial); Verification is **ongoing**.
- **Records** are a **legal requirement** (FSSAI Act 2006, Section 26).
- **Always include a CCP decision tree in the answer** — it is the examiner's favorite visual aid.
- **The "Big 3" process lethality equations** to remember: F₀ (canning), P (pasteurization), and D-value (decimal reduction).
- **Industry-specific Standards to cite:** FSSAI Schedule-IV, Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969, ISO 22000:2018, BRC v9, FSSC 22000 v6.1, EU 853/2004, FDA 21 CFR 120 (Juice HACCP), FDA Seafood HACCP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:** *"HACCP is not a one-time project — it is a living, breathing, documented, audited, verified, continuously improved Food Safety Management System customized to your industry, your product, and your consumer."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation of HACCP in a Food Industry/Retail Food Establishment/Catering Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT868 - HACCP PLANNING AND IMPLEMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation of HACCP in a Food Industry/Retail Food Establishment/Catering Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: IMPLEMENTATION OF HACCP IN A FOOD INDUSTRY / RETAIL FOOD ESTABLISHMENT / CATERING INDUSTRY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point)** is a systematic, science-based, and preventive food safety management system that identifies, evaluates, and controls hazards which are significant for food safety. Implementation of HACCP in a food industry, retail food establishment, or catering industry refers to the **practical operationalization of the seven Codex Alimentarius principles** within the specific context of a defined food business operator (FBO), translating regulatory and statutory requirements into documented Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), Critical Control Points (CCPs), monitoring mechanisms, corrective actions, verification activities, and record-keeping systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Codex Alimentarius, 2020 + FSSAI Schedule 4, 2011):**
> "HACCP is a structured, risk-based, preventive quality assurance system that systematically addresses biological, chemical, physical, and radiological hazards through a documented plan, ensuring food safety from raw material reception to consumer consumption."

### 1.2 Scope of Implementation Across Three Sectors

| Sector Type | KTU 2024 Definition | Primary Objective |
|---|---|---|
| **Food Industry** | Large-scale manufacturing, processing, or packaging units producing finished food products for wholesale/retail distribution | Ensure microbiological, chemical, and physical safety of mass-produced food across the supply chain |
| **Retail Food Establishment** | Supermarkets, butcher shops, fish markets, bakeries, and grocery stores selling directly to the end consumer | Maintain cold-chain integrity, prevent cross-contamination, and ensure traceability of pre-packed and loose food items |
| **Catering Industry** | Hotels, restaurants, canteens, cloud kitchens, airline catering, and institutional feeding operations | Ensure safe preparation, cooking, holding, and service of food to multiple consumers in a time-bound manner |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Airplane Pre-Flight Checklist"**
> Imagine you are a pilot preparing for takeoff. Before every flight, you do not just "hope" the plane is safe — you follow a **rigorous, documented, step-by-step pre-flight checklist** (HACCP Plan). You identify the **critical safety checkpoints** (CCPs) — engine, fuel, control surfaces, weather. You set **critical limits** (e.g., minimum fuel level, maximum crosswind speed). You **monitor continuously** during the flight. If something goes wrong, you have **corrective actions** (divert to nearest airport). The **co-pilot verifies** the captain's work, and **records** are kept in the black box.
>
> Similarly, HACCP implementation in a food industry is a "pre-flight checklist" for food safety. Instead of hoping the food is safe, we **scientifically identify hazards** (microbes, chemicals, bones, glass), set **critical limits** (e.g., cooking temperature ≥ 75°C), **monitor** (check the thermometer every hour), and **act** (re-cook if temperature drops). Verification and record-keeping act as the "black box" for regulatory and customer audits.

### 1.4 Why Implementation Differs Across Sectors

- **Food Industry** has high-volume, automated, continuous processes — implementation focuses on **process flow, CCPs in production lines, and statistical process control**.
- **Retail Food Establishment** deals with high SKU variety and consumer-facing operations — implementation emphasizes **cold-chain, display, segregation, and traceability**.
- **Catering Industry** is time-sensitive, batch-based, and labor-intensive — implementation focuses on **temperature control during holding, cross-contamination prevention, and personal hygiene**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Implementation is **NOT** a one-time activity. It is a **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** that must be **continuously reviewed, verified, and updated** whenever there is a change in raw material, process, equipment, or regulation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Implementation Matrix

## 2.1 The Twelve-Stage Sequence of HACCP Implementation (Codex Alimentarius + FSSAI)

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates that students must understand the **complete implementation lifecycle**. The Codex framework prescribes a logical, sequential **12-stage implementation path** (which combines the 5 preliminary tasks + 7 principles).

### Stage 1: Assemble the HACCP Team
- A **multi-disciplinary team** must be formed comprising personnel from production, quality, engineering, sanitation, procurement, and storage.
- **Team Leader** must have formal HACCP training and authority.
- For SMEs, external consultants may supplement the team.

### Stage 2: Describe the Product
- A complete product description must be drafted covering: **product name, composition, intended use, target consumer (general public, infants, immunocompromised, elderly), shelf-life, packaging, storage conditions, and distribution method**.

### Stage 3: Identify Intended Use
- The **end-user** of the product must be clearly identified.
- Vulnerable groups (pregnant women, elderly, allergic individuals) must be specifically addressed.

### Stage 4: Construct the Process Flow Diagram
- A **block-level schematic** of the entire process from raw material reception to consumer must be drawn.
- All process steps, inputs, outputs, rework loops, and storage stages must be included.

### Stage 5: On-Site Verification of the Flow Diagram
- The HACCP team must physically walk through the plant and **verify the diagram against actual operations**.
- Any deviation must be corrected.

### Stage 6: Conduct Hazard Analysis (Principle 1)
- List all **potential biological, chemical, physical, and radiological hazards** at every step.
- Assess the **severity** and **likelihood (probability)** of each hazard.
- Identify **control measures** for significant hazards.

### Stage 7: Determine Critical Control Points — CCPs (Principle 2)
- Apply the **CCPs Decision Tree (Codex)** to identify CCPs.
- A CCP is a step where control can be applied to **prevent, eliminate, or reduce a hazard to an acceptable level**.

### Stage 8: Establish Critical Limits (Principle 3)
- For each CCP, define the **maximum and/or minimum value** to which a biological, chemical, or physical parameter must be controlled.
- Example: Cooking temperature ≥ **75°C**, pH ≤ **4.5**, moisture ≤ **15%**, salt ≥ **10% w/w**.

### Stage 9: Establish Monitoring Procedures (Principle 4)
- Define **what to monitor, how to monitor, frequency of monitoring, and who is responsible**.
- Monitoring must provide **real-time, documented evidence** of CCP control.

### Stage 10: Establish Corrective Actions (Principle 5)
- For each CCP, pre-define the **action to be taken when monitoring shows a deviation** from the critical limit.
- Corrective actions must include: **isolate the product, identify the cause, restore control, and prevent recurrence**.

### Stage 11: Establish Verification Procedures (Principle 6)
- Verification confirms that the HACCP system is **working effectively**.
- Activities include: **internal audits, sampling and testing, calibration of equipment, review of records, and re-validation**.

### Stage 12: Establish Documentation and Record-Keeping (Principle 7)
- Maintain: **HACCP plan, CCP monitoring records, corrective action records, verification records, calibration records, training records, and pest control records**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Implementation Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a **distilled cheat sheet** for the 14-mark KTU exam. Memorize the structure: each sector follows the same 12 stages, but the **specific hazards, CCPs, and critical limits differ**.

| Stage | Food Industry (e.g., Dairy Plant) | Retail Food Establishment (e.g., Supermarket) | Catering Industry (e.g., Hotel Kitchen) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Team** | Production, QA, Engineering, Lab, Procurement | Store Manager, Butchers, Cold-chain staff, Cleaning staff | Chef, Sous-chef, Steward, Hygiene officer, Procurement |
| **2. Product** | Pasteurized toned milk (4.5% fat, 8.5% SNF) | Pre-packed raw chicken cuts (500 g, chilled) | Biryani buffet (chicken, basmati rice, served hot) |
| **3. Intended Use** | General population, refrigerated by consumer | Consumer to cook at home, store at ≤ 4°C | Direct consumption at lunch buffet, vulnerable groups included |
| **4. Process Steps** | Reception → Clarification → Pasteurization → Homogenization → Packaging → Cold storage | Receiving → Cold storage → Display → Re-pack → Sale → Home | Procurement → Storage → Thawing → Marination → Cooking → Holding → Service |
| **5. Hazards** | Bio: *Salmonella*, *Listeria*; Chem: Antibiotics; Phys: Dirt | Bio: *Campylobacter*; Chem: None; Phys: Bone fragments | Bio: *S. aureus* toxins; Chem: Pesticides; Phys: Glass, metal |
| **6. CCPs** | Pasteurization (72°C × 15 s); Metal detection | Cold-chain display (≤ 4°C); Expiry date check | Cooking (≥ 75°C core); Hot holding (≥ 63°C) |
| **7. Critical Limits** | Temp ≥ 72°C, Time ≥ 15 s | ≤ 4°C, Expiry > 3 days | Core ≥ 75°C, Holding ≥ 63°C |
| **8. Monitoring** | Automatic chart recorder every 30 s | Thermometer check every 2 hours | Probe thermometer every batch and every 30 min holding |
| **9. Corrective Action** | Divert to re-pasteurize, check chart recorder | Pull product, dispose, refill from cold storage | Re-cook if < 75°C; discard if held < 63°C > 2 hours |
| **10. Verification** | Lab phosphatase test weekly; thermometer calibration monthly | Audit temperature log daily; calibration quarterly | Plate count testing weekly; probe calibration monthly |
| **11. Records** | Pasteurization chart, lab reports, calibration logs | Daily temperature log, FIFO rotation log | Cooking log, holding log, cleaning log, training log |

## 2.3 The Seven Principles of HACCP — Quick Reference

| Principle | KTU 2024 Definition | Engineering / Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **P1: Hazard Analysis** | Identify hazards and assess severity × likelihood | Risk assessment matrix in a chemical plant |
| **P2: CCP Determination** | Identify steps essential for hazard control | Pressure-relief valve in a boiler |
| **P3: Critical Limits** | Set measurable thresholds for each CCP | Maximum allowable stress in a beam design |
| **P4: Monitoring** | Continuous/periodic observation of CCPs | SCADA system monitoring in a power plant |
| **P5: Corrective Actions** | Pre-planned response to deviations | Emergency shutdown procedure |
| **P6: Verification** | Confirm the HACCP system is effective | Third-party ISO 9001 audit |
| **P7: Record-Keeping** | Documented evidence of HACCP operation | Maintenance logbook in a manufacturing plant |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Statutory Compliance:** Mandatory under **FSSAI Schedule 4 (2011)** in India for all FBOs; **EU Regulation 852/2004** in Europe; **FDA 21 CFR Part 117** in the USA; **Codex Alimentarius** globally.
- **Market Access:** Mandatory for export to EU, USA, Japan, and Gulf countries.
- **Brand Protection:** Reduces product recalls, lawsuits, and brand damage.
- **Operational Efficiency:** Reduces rework, wastage, and downtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When asked "Discuss the implementation of HACCP in a catering industry," the answer **must** follow the **12-stage sequence**. Marks are awarded for **structured flow**, not random points. Always begin with "The first step is to assemble the HACCP team…"
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step HACCP Implementation in a Catering Industry

## 3.1 Problem Statement (Model Scenario for KTU Exam)

> **Scenario:** A 200-room 5-star hotel operates a 24-hour coffee shop serving 800 covers/day. The hotel wants to implement HACCP for its **chicken biryani preparation line**. Demonstrate the **step-by-step implementation of HACCP** for this process.

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation follows the **Codex 12-stage implementation path** rigorously. Each step is explained with the **decision logic** (the "Why") and the **operational output** (the "How").

---

### STEP 1: Assemble the HACCP Team

**Decision Logic:** A multi-disciplinary team ensures that no single functional silo dominates the analysis. Each member brings domain expertise.

**Team Composition:**

| Member | Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Executive Chef | Team Leader | Authority, decision-making |
| Sous-Chef (Hot Kitchen) | Technical expert | Operational realities of cooking |
| Food Safety Officer | HACCP trained | Codex knowledge |
| Store Keeper | Raw material expert | Storage, receiving practices |
| Steward / Cleaning In-charge | Sanitation expert | Cleaning, pest control |
| Purchase Manager | Supplier expert | Raw material sourcing |

---

### STEP 2: Describe the Product

| Parameter | Description |
|---|---|
| Product Name | Hyderabadi Chicken Biryani |
| Composition | Chicken (40%), Basmati rice (35%), Onion, Spices, Yogurt, Ghee, Salt |
| Packaging | Stainless steel serving dish, banana leaf lining |
| Shelf-life | 4 hours at hot holding (≥ 63°C); 24 hours refrigerated at ≤ 4°C |
| Intended Consumer | Hotel guests, walk-in customers, including elderly and tourists |
| Distribution | Direct service at coffee shop / room service |

---

### STEP 3: Identify Intended Use

The biryani will be **consumed immediately after service** by the customer. The product is expected to be **hot and fully cooked**. Vulnerable groups (elderly, pregnant women, immunocompromised guests) are part of the target consumer base.

---

### STEP 4: Construct the Process Flow Diagram

```
Raw Material Receipt
      ↓
Cold Storage (≤ 4°C)
      ↓
Thawing (Running water, ≤ 10°C)
      ↓
Cutting & Marination (with yogurt + spices, 4–8 hours)
      ↓
Pre-frying Onions
      ↓
Cooking Chicken (Pressure cooking, ≥ 75°C core)
      ↓
Rice Boiling (100°C for 12 minutes)
      ↓
Layering & Dum Cooking (Steam, ≥ 90°C, 25 min)
      ↓
Hot Holding (≥ 63°C, ≤ 2 hours)
      ↓
Plating & Service
```

---

### STEP 5: On-Site Verification of the Flow Diagram

The HACCP team physically walks through the kitchen from receiving to service. They verify:
- The cold storage is functional and the thermometer is calibrated.
- The pressure cooker has a calibrated pressure gauge.
- The dum cooking vessel has a working thermometer.
- The hot holding counter is maintained at ≥ 63°C.

Any discrepancy is corrected and the diagram updated.

---

### STEP 6: Conduct Hazard Analysis (Principle 1)

| Process Step | Hazard Type | Hazard Description | Severity | Likelihood | Significant? | Control Measure |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raw Material Receipt (Chicken) | Biological | *Salmonella*, *Campylobacter* in raw chicken | High | High | **YES** | Supplier approval, cold-chain delivery |
| Thawing at room temperature | Biological | Multiplication of pathogens | High | High | **YES** | Thaw under running water ≤ 10°C |
| Marination | Biological | Pathogen growth in yogurt | Medium | Medium | **YES** | Marinate at ≤ 4°C |
| Cooking (CCP-1) | Biological | Survival of *Salmonella* if under-cooked | High | High | **YES** | Cook to ≥ 75°C core for ≥ 2 min |
| Dum Cooking (CCP-2) | Biological | Heat penetration in rice layer | Medium | Medium | **YES** | Steam at ≥ 90°C for 25 min |
| Hot Holding (CCP-3) | Biological | *S. aureus* toxin formation | High | High | **YES** | Hold at ≥ 63°C, max 2 hours |
| Plating & Service | Physical | Glass/metal fragments from equipment | Medium | Low | **YES** | Visual inspection, staff training |
| Spices (Cumin, Coriander) | Chemical | Pesticide residues | Medium | Medium | **YES** | Supplier CoA, periodic testing |

---

### STEP 7: Determine Critical Control Points (Principle 2)

**Application of the Codex CCP Decision Tree (Q1–Q4):**

For the **Cooking Step:**
- Q1: Do preventive control measures exist? **YES** (cooking temperature, time)
- Q2: Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the likely occurrence of the hazard to an acceptable level? **YES** (cooking destroys *Salmonella*)
- → **CCP-1: Cooking (≥ 75°C core, ≥ 2 min)**

For the **Dum Cooking Step:**
- Q1: Do preventive control measures exist? **YES**
- Q2: Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the hazard? **YES** (steam heat penetrates rice)
- → **CCP-2: Dum Cooking (≥ 90°C for 25 min)**

For the **Hot Holding Step:**
- Q1: Do preventive control measures exist? **YES**
- Q2: Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the hazard? **NO** (holding does not eliminate, only prevents multiplication)
- Q3: Could contamination with the identified hazard(s) occur in excess of acceptable level(s), or could it increase to unacceptable level(s)? **YES** (*S. aureus* growth)
- Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard to an acceptable level? **NO** (no further cooking before service)
- → **CCP-3: Hot Holding (≥ 63°C, ≤ 2 hours)**

---

### STEP 8: Establish Critical Limits (Principle 3)

| CCP | Critical Limit | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **CCP-1: Cooking** | Core temperature ≥ **75°C** held for ≥ 2 minutes | Codex/FSSAI guideline for poultry; destroys *Salmonella* (D-value 70°C = 1.5 min) |
| **CCP-2: Dum Cooking** | Steam temperature ≥ **90°C** for ≥ 25 minutes | Ensures heat penetration to rice core |
| **CCP-3: Hot Holding** | Hot holding temperature ≥ **63°C**; maximum hold time ≤ **2 hours** | FSSAI Schedule 4; prevents *S. aureus* toxin formation |

---

### STEP 9: Establish Monitoring Procedures (Principle 4)

| CCP | What to Monitor | How to Monitor | Frequency | Who Monitors |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CCP-1: Cooking | Core temperature | Calibrated probe thermometer | Every batch | Line Cook |
| CCP-2: Dum Cooking | Steam temperature & time | Built-in thermometer + timer | Every batch | Sous-Chef |
| CCP-3: Hot Holding | Holding counter temperature | Counter thermometer | Every 30 minutes | Steward |

---

### STEP 10: Establish Corrective Actions (Principle 5)

| CCP | Deviation | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| CCP-1: Cooking | Core temperature < 75°C | Continue cooking until ≥ 75°C; record in deviation log |
| CCP-2: Dum Cooking | Steam < 90°C or time < 25 min | Extend dum cooking until limits met; re-check |
| CCP-3: Hot Holding | Temperature drops < 63°C or held > 2 hours | Discard product OR reheat to 75°C; if reheat fails, dispose |

---

### STEP 11: Establish Verification Procedures (Principle 6)

| Verification Activity | Frequency | Responsible Person |
|---|---|---|
| Internal audit of HACCP records | Monthly | Food Safety Officer |
| Microbiological testing of cooked biryani | Weekly | In-house/External lab |
| Calibration of probe thermometer | Monthly | QA team |
| Review of corrective action records | Monthly | Executive Chef |
| Re-validation of HACCP plan | Annually / on any change | HACCP Team |

---

### STEP 12: Establish Documentation and Record-Keeping (Principle 7)

**Mandatory Records:**
- HACCP plan (master document)
- CCP monitoring logs (cooking, dum, holding)
- Corrective action reports
- Thermometer calibration certificates
- Supplier approval records and Certificates of Analysis (CoA)
- Cleaning and sanitation logs
- Staff training records
- Internal audit reports

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** The 12-stage implementation is a **logical chain**. Skipping a stage (e.g., not doing on-site verification) invalidates the entire HACCP plan. The exam expects a **chronological, structured answer** — not a random list of points.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: HACCP Implementation Flow in a Catering Industry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HACCP Team Formation] --> B[Product Description]
    B --> C[Intended Use Identification]
    C --> D[Process Flow Diagram Construction]
    D --> E[On-Site Verification of Flow]
    E --> F[Hazard Analysis - Principle 1]
    F --> G[CCP Determination - Principle 2]
    G --> H[Critical Limits - Principle 3]
    H --> I[Monitoring Procedures - Principle 4]
    I --> J[Corrective Actions - Principle 5]
    J --> K[Verification Procedures - Principle 6]
    K --> L[Documentation and Records - Principle 7]
    L --> M{HACCP Audit}
    M -->|Pass| N[Certified HACCP System]
    M -->|Fail| F
    N --> O[Continuous Improvement - PDCA Cycle]
    O --> F
```

```mermaid
    A[Receiving Bay] -->|Temp Check at Receipt| B[Walk-In Cold Room]
    B -->|Temperature Logging| C[Display Chiller]
    C -->|Periodic Replenishment| D[Customer Pick-up]
    B -->|Reject if 4 deg C crossed| E[Quarantine Room]
    C -->|Expiry Date Check| F[Promotional Discount]
    C -->|Expiry Crossed| G[Disposal as Per FSSAI]
    H[Thermometer] -.->|Calibration Monthly| A
    H -.->|Calibration Monthly| B
    H -.->|Calibration Monthly| C

    I -.->|Daily Cleaning| B
    I -.->|Daily Cleaning| C
    J[QA Supervisor] -.->|Daily Verification| B
    J -.->|Daily Verification| C
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Food Industry CCP Decision Tree (Codex)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Q1: Do preventive control measures exist at this step?}
    Q1 -- No --> X1{Is this a CCP?}
    X1 -- Yes --> CCPFinal1[Modify step or process to include control]
    X1 -- No --> NotCCP1[Not a CCP]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Q2: Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> CCP1[CRITICAL CONTROL POINT]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Q3: Could contamination occur or increase to unacceptable level?}
    Q3 -- No --> NotCCP2[Not a CCP - Go to next step]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the hazard?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> NotCCP3[Not a CCP - Control at next step]
    Q4 -- No --> CCP2[CRITICAL CONTROL POINT]
```

```mermaid
gantt
```
