<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define HACCP. List the seven principles of HACCP as per Codex Alimentarius.  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point)** is a systematic, science-based, preventive food safety management system that identifies, evaluates, and controls hazards (biological, chemical, physical, radiological) that are significant for food safety, as defined by the Codex Alimentarius Commission (1963, revised 2020).

**The Seven Principles of HACCP (Codex Alimentarius):**

| Sl. No. | Principle |
|---|---|
| P1 | Conduct a hazard analysis and identify control measures |
| P2 | Determine the Critical Control Points (CCPs) |
| P3 | Establish critical limits for each CCP |
| P4 | Establish monitoring procedures for each CCP |
| P5 | Establish corrective actions to be taken when monitoring shows a deviation |
| P6 | Establish verification procedures to confirm HACCP is working |
| P7 | Establish documentation and record-keeping for all procedures |

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a CCP and a CP (Control Point). Give one example from a catering industry.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | CCP (Critical Control Point) | CP (Control Point) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A step where control is **essential** to prevent, eliminate, or reduce a food safety hazard to an acceptable level | A step where a hazard is **controlled** but loss of control **does not** lead to an unacceptable health risk |
| **Criticality** | Critical — mandatory under HACCP | Non-critical — best practice, often under PRP or OPRP |
| **Failure Consequence** | Direct health risk to consumer | Quality or regulatory risk, not immediate health risk |
| **Example (Catering)** | Cooking chicken to ≥ 75°C core temperature | Visual inspection of garnish for foreign matter |

**Catering Industry Example:**
- **CCP:** Cooking biryani at ≥ 75°C core (failure → *Salmonella* outbreak)
- **CP:** Display of salad at ambient (failure → quality deterioration, not necessarily a safety hazard)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Discuss in detail the step-by-step implementation of HACCP in a Food Industry. Illustrate with a suitable example of a dairy processing plant producing pasteurized milk.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Discuss the preliminary steps and Principles 1–3.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Preliminary Steps (Stages 1–5):**

**Step 1: Assemble the HACCP Team**
A multi-disciplinary team is formed comprising the Plant Manager (Team Leader), Quality Assurance Manager, Production Supervisor, Lab Chemist, Microbiologist, Engineering In-charge, Store Keeper, and Cleaning Supervisor. The team receives formal HACCP training.

**Step 2: Describe the Product**
- Product: Pasteurized Toned Milk (3% fat, 8.5% SNF)
- Composition: Cow milk standardized to 3% fat and 8.5% SNF
- Packaging: 500 mL food-grade LDPE pouches
- Shelf-life: 2 days at ≤ 4°C; 7 days if UHT-treated
- Intended consumer: General public
- Distribution: Refrigerated vans to retail outlets

**Step 3: Identify Intended Use**
Direct consumption by the general public, including children and elderly. To be boiled by the consumer if desired.

**Step 4: Construct the Process Flow Diagram**

```
Raw Milk Reception → Chilling (≤ 4°C) → Clarification → Standardization → Pasteurization (72°C × 15 s) → Homogenization → Cooling (≤ 4°C) → Pouch Packaging → Cold Storage → Distribution
```

**Step 5: On-Site Verification**
The HACCP team physically walks through the plant to verify the flow, identify any deviation, and correct the diagram.

---

**2. Principles 1–3:**

**Principle 1: Hazard Analysis**

| Step | Hazard | Severity × Likelihood | Significant? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raw milk reception | *Salmonella*, *E. coli*, antibiotic residues | High × High | **YES** |
| Chilling | Pathogen multiplication if > 4°C | High × Medium | **YES** |
| Pasteurization | Pathogen survival if under-processed | High × High | **YES** |
| Homogenization | None significant | Low × Low | NO |
| Packaging | Cross-contamination from machine | Medium × Low | **YES** |
| Cold storage | Pathogen growth if > 4°C | High × High | **YES** |

**Principle 2: Determine CCPs**
- **CCP-1: Pasteurization** (only step that destroys pathogens)
- **CCP-2: Cold storage** (prevents re-contamination and growth)
- **CCP-3: Cold-chain distribution**

**Principle 3: Establish Critical Limits**

| CCP | Critical Limit |
|---|---|
| CCP-1: Pasteurization | ≥ 72°C for ≥ 15 seconds (HTST) |
| CCP-2: Cold storage | ≤ 4°C throughout storage |
| CCP-3: Distribution | Refrigerated van at ≤ 4°C |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Discuss Principles 4–7.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle 4: Monitoring Procedures**

| CCP | What to Monitor | How | Frequency | Who |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CCP-1 | Pasteurization temp and time | Automatic chart recorder + thermometer | Continuous (every 15 s) | Plant Operator |
| CCP-2 | Cold room temperature | Digital data logger | Continuous | Cold Room In-charge |
| CCP-3 | Distribution van temperature | GPS-enabled data logger | Real-time | Logistics Manager |

**Principle 5: Corrective Actions**
- **CCP-1 Deviation** (Temp < 72°C or Time < 15 s): Divert the milk back to the raw milk tank; restart pasteurizer; check the chart recorder; investigate the cause.
- **CCP-2 Deviation** (Cold room > 4°C): Shift stock to another cold room; identify equipment failure; do not dispatch until restored.
- **CCP-3 Deviation** (Van > 4°C): Recall the van; check products; reject if temperature history compromised.

**Principle 6: Verification Procedures**
- **Phosphatase test** weekly to confirm pasteurization effectiveness.
- **Coliform and total plate count** testing daily.
- **Thermometer calibration** monthly against a standard reference thermometer.
- **Internal HACCP audit** quarterly.
- **External audit** annually for FSSAI/FSSC 22000 certification.

**Principle 7: Documentation and Record-Keeping**
- HACCP plan document (master file)
- Pasteurization chart recorder logs (5-year retention)
- Lab test reports (microbiological, chemical)
- Corrective action reports
- Calibration certificates
- Cleaning and sanitation records
- Pest control records
- Staff training records
- Internal and external audit reports

**Conclusion:** A dairy plant implementing HACCP ensures microbiological safety of pasteurized milk, regulatory compliance with FSSAI, and consumer protection against foodborne illness.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the implementation of HACCP in a Retail Food Establishment with special reference to the handling, display, and sale of raw fish in a supermarket.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Preliminary steps and CCP identification.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Preliminary Steps (Stages 1–5):**

**Step 1: HACCP Team**
Fishmonger (Team Leader), Store Manager, Cold-Chain In-charge, Sanitation Supervisor, Procurement Officer, Quality Officer.

**Step 2: Product Description**
- Product: Fresh raw fish (Pomfret, Mackerel, Prawns) — whole and filleted
- Packaging: Food-grade plastic trays with cling wrap on crushed ice
- Shelf-life: 1 day on ice, 2 days refrigerated at ≤ 0°C
- Intended consumer: General public; cook at home
- Distribution: Sold at the fish counter; consumer carries home

**Step 3: Intended Use**
Cooked thoroughly by the consumer before consumption. Vulnerable groups (pregnant women) advised to avoid certain species.

**Step 4: Process Flow Diagram**
```
Receiving at Loading Bay → Weighing & Grading → Cold Storage (0–2°C) → Cutting/Filleting → Display on Crushed Ice → Customer Selection → Packing → Sale → Home
```

**Step 5: On-Site Verification**
The HACCP team verifies cold-chain integrity, ice replenishment schedule, segregation from other raw foods, and customer handling.

---

**2. CCP Identification:**

| Step | Hazard | Significant? | CCP? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Receiving | *Salmonella*, *Vibrio*, histamine (scombroid poisoning) | YES | **CCP-1: Cold-chain at receipt (≤ 2°C)** |
| Cold storage | Pathogen multiplication | YES | **CCP-2: Cold storage (0–2°C)** |
| Display on ice | Pathogen growth if ice melts | YES | **CCP-3: Display temperature (≤ 2°C, ice maintained)** |
| Cross-contamination | Allergen transfer, *Listeria* spread | YES | **CCP-4: Segregation from ready-to-eat foods** |
| Packing | Physical: bone, scale, plastic | YES | **CP (not CCP)** |

**Critical Limits:**
- CCP-1: Fish received at ≤ 2°C
- CCP-2: Cold room at 0–2°C
- CCP-3: Display counter with continuous ice, product temp ≤ 2°C
- CCP-4: Physical separation of raw fish from cooked/ready-to-eat foods

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Monitoring, Corrective Action, Verification, and Records.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle 4: Monitoring**

| CCP | What | How | Frequency | Who |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CCP-1 | Receiving temperature | Probe thermometer | Every supplier delivery | Cold-Chain In-charge |
| CCP-2 | Cold room temperature | Digital data logger | Continuous | Cold-Chain In-charge |
| CCP-3 | Display temperature + ice level | Probe thermometer + visual | Every 2 hours | Fishmonger |
| CCP-4 | Segregation | Visual inspection | Hourly | Sanitation Supervisor |

**Principle 5: Corrective Actions**
- **CCP-1 Deviation** (Fish > 2°C on receipt): Reject the batch and return to supplier; if borderline, accept with priority dispatch.
- **CCP-2 Deviation** (Cold room > 2°C): Shift stock to backup freezer; investigate compressor fault.
- **CCP-3 Deviation** (Display > 2°C): Replenish ice immediately; reduce display depth; remove older stock.
- **CCP-4 Deviation** (Segregation breach): Re-segregate immediately; disinfect counter.

**Principle 6: Verification**
- **Histamine testing** weekly for scombroid species (mackerel, tuna).
- **Microbiological testing** (TVC, *Salmonella*, *Vibrio*) weekly.
- **Thermometer calibration** monthly.
- **Internal audit** monthly.
- **Customer complaint review** weekly.

**Principle 7: Documentation**
- Daily temperature log (receiving, cold room, display)
- Ice replenishment log
- Supplier CoA and delivery receipts
- Calibration certificates
- Cleaning and sanitation log
- Pest control log
- Customer complaint log
- Internal audit report

**Conclusion:** HACCP implementation in a retail fish counter ensures cold-chain integrity, prevents histamine formation and pathogen multiplication, and protects the consumer from scombroid poisoning and bacterial gastroenteritis.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in KTU 2024 Exams (Lose 3–5 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Skipping Preliminary Steps:** Jumping directly to "7 Principles" without mentioning team formation, product description, and flow diagram. **Loss: 3–4 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing CCP with CP:** Calling a "visual inspection" step a CCP. Visual inspection is a Control Point (CP), not a CCP, unless it eliminates a hazard.
> 3. **Wrong Critical Limit Units:** Writing "75°C" without specifying **"core temperature ≥ 75°C held for ≥ 2 minutes."** Critical limits must include **time, temperature, AND the measurement location** (core vs surface).
> 4. **No Sector-Specific Examples:** Writing a generic answer without using the **specific sector** (dairy, fish counter, biryani kitchen). KTU examiners look for **sector-specific hazard lists and critical limits**.
> 5. **Missing the "Verification vs Monitoring" Distinction:** Writing "lab testing daily" under monitoring. Lab testing is **verification**, not monitoring. Monitoring is **real-time** (e.g., every 30 minutes).
> 6. **Ignoring Documentation:** Ending the answer at corrective actions. Always close with **Principle 7 (Records)** — this is a frequently tested high-yield point.
> 7. **No Conclusion / Practical Relevance:** Ending abruptly. Always add a one-line **conclusion** linking HACCP to **FSSAI compliance, consumer safety, and the specific sector**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ HACCP = **Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point** — Codex Alimentarius, 1963, revised 2020.
- ✅ HACCP is **mandatory** in India under **FSSAI Schedule 4 (2011)** for all Food Business Operators.
- ✅ Implementation has **12 stages** (5 preliminary + 7 principles).
- ✅ **Preliminary Steps:** Team → Product → Intended Use → Flow Diagram → On-Site Verification.
- ✅ **Seven Principles:** Hazard Analysis → CCP → Critical Limits → Monitoring → Corrective Actions → Verification → Records.
- ✅ **Food Industry CCPs** are usually **thermal processes** (pasteurization, sterilization, cooking).
- ✅ **Retail Food Establishment CCPs** are usually **cold-chain** (refrigeration, display, segregation).
- ✅ **Catering Industry CCPs** are usually **cooking and hot holding** (≥ 75°C cooking, ≥ 63°C holding).
- ✅ **Critical Limits** must be **measurable** (temperature, time, pH, water activity, salt concentration).
- ✅ **Monitoring** is **real-time and continuous**; **Verification** is **periodic** (daily/weekly/monthly).
- ✅ **Corrective Action** must address **4 things:** isolate product, identify cause, restore control, prevent recurrence.
- ✅ **Records** must be retained for **at least 5 years** as per FSSAI.
- ✅ HACCP is a **living system** — must be **re-validated** on any change in raw material, process, equipment, or regulation.
- ✅ The **CCPs Decision Tree** uses **4 questions (Q1–Q4)** to determine if a step is a CCP.
- ✅ **Biological hazards** (pathogens) are the **most common** significant hazard across all three sectors.
- ✅ **PDCA Cycle:** Plan (design HACCP) → Do (implement) → Check (verify) → Act (improve).
- ✅ The 14-mark question answer **must follow the 12-stage chronology** — marks are awarded for structure, not content alone.
- ✅ Always conclude with a **sector-specific benefit** (e.g., "HACCP ensures histamine control in retail fish display," or "HACCP prevents *S. aureus* toxin in catering biryani").

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Implementation of HACCP in a Food Industry / Retail Food Establishment / Catering Industry**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT868 - HACCP PLANNING AND IMPLEMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of HACCP Case Studies

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **HACCP Case Study** is a structured, retrospective (or prospective) forensic analysis of a real or simulated food manufacturing/processing/handling operation in which the **seven Codex Alimentarius principles** (Principle 1: Hazard Analysis; Principle 2: Critical Control Point Determination; Principle 3: Establishment of Critical Limits; Principle 4: Monitoring Procedures; Principle 5: Corrective Actions; Principle 6: Verification; Principle 7: Record-Keeping) are systematically applied to identify hazards, establish Critical Control Points (CCPs), define critical limits, prescribe monitoring, and document outcomes.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, case studies are classified as:

- **Type I — Crisis/Lapse Case Studies:** Real-world outbreaks where HACCP failed or was absent (e.g., *Jack in the Box*, *Maple Leaf Foods*).
- **Type II — Exemplar/Success Case Studies:** Operations where HACCP successfully prevented or controlled a hazard (e.g., seafood processors after the FDA's 1997 mandatory HACCP rule).
- **Type III — Industry-Specific Illustrative Case Studies:** Sector-wise model HACCP plans (dairy, bakery, beverage, RTE foods).

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Module 2 Learning Outcome Mapping
> Students must be able to: (i) dissect an outbreak into hazard-cause-CCP failures, (ii) reconstruct a HACCP plan from a process-flow narrative, and (iii) prescribe corrective actions aligned with Codex Principle 5.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Forensic Detective and the Food Plant"

Imagine a food processing line as a **city's water supply network**. A HACCP case study is like a **disease outbreak investigation**:
- **Hazard Analysis** is the detective searching for the contaminant source (like John Snow's cholera map of 1854 London).
- **CCPs** are the few critical valves where contamination must be intercepted.
- **Critical Limits** are the safe operating pressure/temperature windows.
- **Monitoring** is the periodic gauge reading.
- **Corrective Actions** are the emergency shutdown and flush protocols.

Just as you cannot monitor every drop of water at every pipe every second, you cannot test every gram of food for every pathogen. So HACCP concentrates control at **strategic chokepoints** — this is the essence of every case study you will analyze.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in HACCP Case Studies

The following numerical thresholds appear repeatedly in real-world case studies and must be memorized:

> [!NOTE] Critical Numerical Benchmarks
> - **75 °C (167 °F)** — Internal temperature for safe cooking of comminuted meat (USDA FSIS mandate post-1993 Jack in the Box).
> - **57 °C (135 °F)** — Minimum hot-holding temperature to prevent *Salmonella* and *Staph. aureus* growth.
> - **4 °C (40 °F)** — Maximum cold-holding temperature for refrigerated RTE foods.
> - **−18 °C (0 °F)** — Standard frozen storage temperature.
> - **5 log reduction** — Required lethality for *E. coli* O157:H7 in juice (FDA 21 CFR 120, post-Odwalla 1996).
> - **7-log reduction** — Required for *Listeria monocytogenes* in RTE meats (USDA-FSIS 9 CFR 430, post-Maple Leaf 2008).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Universal Case-Study Dissection Framework (UCCDF)

Every HACCP case study, regardless of industry or geography, can be dissected using this six-step logical ladder. This framework is the *single most important analytical tool* for scoring KTU Part B questions.

**Step 1 — Define the Product and Process Flow**
Articulate the raw materials, the unit operations (receipt → storage → processing → packaging → distribution), and the intended consumer (general population, infants, immunocompromised).

**Step 2 — Construct the Hazard Matrix**
For each step, classify hazards as **Biological (B)**, **Chemical (C)**, or **Physical (P)** and rank severity × likelihood on a 1–4 scale.

**Step 3 — Apply the Codex Decision Tree to Identify CCPs**
Ask: (a) Do preventive control measures exist? (b) Does this step eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level? (c) Could contamination occur in excess of acceptable levels or increase to unacceptable levels? (d) Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the hazard? — leading to CCP identification.

**Step 4 — Specify Critical Limits, Monitoring, and Corrective Actions**
Quantitative (temperature, pH, *a*<sub>w</sub>) or qualitative (visual inspection, supplier certification) thresholds must be established.

**Step 5 — Verification and Record-Keeping**
Calibration logs, microbial testing, end-product testing, internal audits, and supplier COAs.

**Step 6 — Root-Cause Forensic Loop**
For failure cases, identify which of the 7 principles broke down — and which prerequisite program (PRP) was missing.

### 2.2 The Hazard Risk-Decision Matrix (Universal Scoring)

A standard KTU 14-mark case study question often supplies a process flow and asks you to identify hazards and propose CCPs. Use the matrix below.

| Hazard Class | Severity (S) | Likelihood (L) | Risk Score R = S × L | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Biological | 4 | 4 | 16 (Critical) | CCP mandatory |
| Biological | 4 | 3 | 12 (Critical) | CCP mandatory |
| Chemical | 3 | 3 | 9 (High) | CCP or control measure |
| Physical | 2 | 3 | 6 (Medium) | Control point (CP) |
| Chemical | 3 | 1 | 3 (Low) | PRP only |

> [!TIP] KTU Valuation Tip: Always carry the calculation `R = S × L` forward and quote the score beside each hazard. Examiners allocate partial marks for this explicit reasoning.

### 2.3 Industry-Specific High-Yield CCP Map (Cheat Sheet)

The table below summarizes typical CCPs encountered in KTU case-study questions. **Commit this to memory — it appears in 80 % of Module 2 questions.**

| Industry / Product | Step | Hazard Targeted | Critical Limit | Monitoring Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Comminuted meat (hamburger patty) | Cooking | *E. coli* O157:H7 | ≥ **75 °C** internal for 15 s | Continuous (probe every 30 min) |
| Pasteurized milk | HTST pasteurization | *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, *Brucella* | 72 °C × 15 s (or 63 °C × 30 min) | Continuous chart recorder |
| Apple juice / cider | Pasteurization | *E. coli* O157:H7, *Cryptosporidium* | 5-log reduction | Each batch thermocouple |
| RTE deli meat | Post-lethality | *Listeria monocytogenes* | 7-log reduction or antimicrobial + 1-log | Daily environmental swabs |
| Canned low-acid food | Retort | *Clostridium botulinum* | F₀ ≥ 3 min (121 °C equivalent) | Every retort run |
| Shellfish | Receiving | Marine biotoxins (PSP, DSP) | ≤ **0.8 mg/kg** saxitoxin equiv. | Each lot |
| Bread/bakery | Metal detection | Metal fragments | Fe ≤ **2.0 mm**, Non-Fe ≤ **2.5 mm**, SS ≤ **3.0 mm** | Continuous inline |
| Fresh produce | Wash water | *E. coli*, *Salmonella* | Chlorine 50–200 ppm; ORP ≥ **650 mV** | Continuous |

> [!IMPORTANT] Note on Formula Symbols
> The `|` (vertical pipe) symbol is used in the table above exclusively as a markdown table column separator; do not confuse it with mathematical "such that" notation. Within LaTeX math blocks, use `\vert` for absolute value to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

HACCP case-study analysis is the operational core of:
- **Regulatory compliance audits** (FSSAI, USFDA, EFSA, FSANZ)
- **Third-party certifications** (SQF, BRCGS, FSSC 22000, ISO 22000)
- **Plant commissioning** (new product launches)
- **Litigation and recall forensics** (foodborne illness law suits)
- **Insurance underwriting** for food businesses

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix — Three Major Case Studies

This section applies the UCCDF framework rigorously to three landmark case studies. Every step is shown explicitly.

---

### 3.1 Case Study I — The Jack in the Box E. coli O157:H7 Outbreak (USA, 1993)

#### 3.1.1 Background and Incident Timeline

In January 1993, a multi-state outbreak of *Escherichia coli* O157:H7 infections was traced to undercooked hamburger patties served at **Jack in the Box** restaurants in the Western United States.

- **Confirmed cases:** 732 (lab-confirmed)
- **Hospitalizations:** 178
- **Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS):** 55 cases
- **Deaths:** 4 (all children under 8)
- **Trigger recall:** Approximately **20 million pounds** of hamburger patties (largest ground-beef recall in US history at that time).

#### 3.1.2 Process Flow and Hazard Matrix

The hamburger patty production chain is summarized below. Apply hazard analysis step by step.

**Step 1: Receiving — Beef Trimmings**
- Biological hazard: *E. coli* O157:H7 from cattle intestines and feces.
- Severity **S = 4** (causes HUS, death in children).
- Likelihood **L = 4** (prevalence in cattle ≈ 10–28 %).
- **R = 16** → Critical.

**Step 2: Storage (Refrigerated)**
- Biological hazard: Pathogen multiplication if temperature abused.
- Critical limit: ≤ **4 °C**.
- S = 4, L = 2 → **R = 8** (Controlled via PRP — sanitation and cold chain).

**Step 3: Grinding and Forming (Comminution)**
- Biological hazard: Surface contamination distributed throughout the patty. *E. coli* now present not just on surface but throughout — **cooking must penetrate fully.**
- Chemical hazard: Allergen cross-contact (none significant here).
- S = 4, L = 4 → **R = 16** → Critical.

**Step 4: Cooking (the crucial CCP)**
- Biological hazard: Survival of *E. coli* O157:H7 if undercooked.
- Critical limit established post-outbreak: ≥ **71 °C (160 °F)** at the geometric center of the patty for **at least 15 seconds** (USDA mandate, 1993 onwards). Internal time-temperature combinations:
  - 70 °C for 2 minutes
  - 68 °C for 6 minutes
  - 65 °C for 12 minutes
- S = 4, L = 3 → **R = 12** → CCP mandatory.

**Step 5: Holding/Hot-Box**
- Biological hazard: Pathogen growth if held below 57 °C.
- Critical limit: ≥ **57 °C (135 °F)**.
- S = 3, L = 2 → **R = 6** → CCP.

#### 3.1.3 What Went Wrong (HACCP Forensic Loop)

The Jack in the Box chain **did not have an effective HACCP plan** in 1992–93. Specifically:

- The **cooking CCP** had a critical limit of **68 °C (155 °F)** at the time — too low to reliably kill *E. coli* O157:H7 (the D-value at 65 °C is approximately 0.4–1.5 minutes depending on strain).
- Jack in the Box headquarters instructed franchisees to **lower cooking temperatures** to improve product taste and texture.
- **Monitoring** was not continuous; spot checks only.
- **Corrective action** plans for temperature deviation did not exist.

#### 3.1.4 The HACCP Plan That Should Have Existed

| CCP | Critical Limit | Monitoring | Corrective Action | Verification | Records |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cooking of patties | ≥ **71 °C** internal, ≥ 15 s | Continuous digital probe, chart recorder, every 30 min manual check | Re-cook or discard; calibrate probe | Micro testing of finished product every 1 h | Cooking charts, probe calibration logs |
| Hot-holding | ≥ **57 °C** | Continuous probe | Discard after 4 h deviation | Thermometer calibration daily | Hot-holding logs |

#### 3.1.5 Lessons and KTU-Exam Takeaway

- The outbreak was the **catalyst for USDA's Pathogen Reduction; HACCP Systems Final Rule (1996)** mandating HACCP for meat and poultry.
- **Lesson 1:** Critical limits must be validated by *microbial* inactivation kinetics, not just visual doneness.
- **Lesson 2:** Continuous monitoring is non-negotiable.
- **Lesson 3:** Top-management quality decisions cannot override validated food-safety thresholds.

---

### 3.2 Case Study II — Maple Leaf Foods Listeriosis Outbreak (Canada, 2008)

#### 3.2.1 Background

In summer 2008, Canada experienced its deadliest foodborne outbreak in decades — *Listeria monocytogenes* linked to **Maple Leaf Foods** ready-to-eat (RTE) deli meats produced at the Bartor Road plant in Toronto.

- **Confirmed deaths:** 22
- **Total cases:** 57
- **Product recall:** Approximately **220 products**, > 50 countries affected.
- **Root cause:** Contaminated slicing equipment — likely the **portal/transfer conveyor and slicing blade area** behind production lines, where condensate dripped onto exposed product.

#### 3.2.2 Process Flow and Hazard Analysis

**Step 1: Receiving — Pre-cooked meat**
- Biological hazard: *L. monocytogenes* may already be present at low levels.
- Severity **S = 4** (mortality rate of listeriosis in vulnerable populations ≈ 20–30 %).
- Likelihood **L = 2** (lot-level prevalence low).
- **R = 8** → Controlled at receiving via supplier COA + testing.

**Step 2: Post-Lethality Slicing (CCP-1)**
- Pre-cooked meat entering the slicing room carries residual *Listeria* survivors or acquires contamination from the environment.
- Biological hazard: *Listeria monocytogenes* on RTE surface.
- Severity **S = 4**, Likelihood **L = 4** → **R = 16** → CCP.
- **Critical limit:** Achieve a **7-log reduction** of *L. monocytogenes*, OR apply a post-lethality treatment (e.g., hot-water deluge, steam pasteurization) giving **≥ 1-log reduction** in combination with a growth inhibitor.

**Step 3: Packaging (CCP-2 — Secondary)**
- Hazard: Recontamination post-slice.
- Critical limit: Environmental *Listeria* test results **negative**, ORP/sanitizer concentration in condensate.

**Step 4: Refrigerated Storage and Distribution**
- *Listeria* grows at refrigeration temperatures — doubling time ≈ **1.5 days at 4 °C**.
- Critical limit: ≤ **4 °C** end-to-end cold chain; **maximum 14-day shelf life** to limit ≤ **1-log growth**.

#### 3.2.3 What Went Wrong

- **Equipment design failure:** The slicers had inaccessible areas (e.g., cracked stainless-steel surfaces, hollow rollers with condensate accumulation) that could not be properly cleaned.
- **Verification gap:** Routine environmental monitoring was performed, but **root-cause investigation** after positive swabs did not trigger line shutdown.
- **Senior management:** CEO Michael McCain publicly apologized — the crisis became a textbook example of executive accountability.
- **Regulatory response:** Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) was reformed; *Listeria* policy in Canada tightened.

#### 3.2.4 The HACCP Plan That Should Have Existed

| CCP | Critical Limit | Monitoring | Corrective Action | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Post-lethality treatment (CCPb) | ≥ 1-log *L. monocytogenes* reduction verified daily | Daily inoculated-pack studies; ATP swabbing | Adjust temperature/dwell; shut line | Monthly challenge studies |
| Slicing area sanitation (CP) | Negative *Listeria* swab in 5 sites per week | Weekly swab composite | Disassemble, sanitize, retest | Quarterly environmental mapping |
| Cold chain (CCP) | ≤ **4 °C** at all points | Continuous datalogger | Quarantine lot, investigate cause | Annual third-party audit |

#### 3.2.5 Lesson

HACCP alone is insufficient — it must be **embedded in a robust Food Safety Management System (FSMS)** with **environmental monitoring, sanitation, equipment design validation, and a strong food-safety culture**.

---

### 3.3 Case Study III — Odwalla Apple Juice E. coli Outbreak (USA, 1996)

#### 3.3.1 Background

In October 1996, an outbreak of *E. coli* O157:H7 was traced to **Odwalla Inc.** unpasteurized fresh apple juice.

- **Cases:** 70 (primarily children)
- **Hospitalizations:** 25
- **HUS cases:** 14
- **Deaths:** 1 (a 16-month-old child)
- **Root cause:** Use of **fallen "windfall" apples** contaminated with deer feces; no pasteurization step.

#### 3.3.2 Hazard Analysis and Process Flow

**Step 1: Apple Receiving**
- Biological hazard: *E. coli* O157:H7 from animal feces on apples.
- Severity **S = 4**, Likelihood **L = 4** → **R = 16**.

**Step 2: Washing**
- Physical/biological hazard: Insufficient to remove deeply adhered fecal matter.
- S = 3, L = 3 → **R = 9** → Control via brush-wash + sanitizer (100–200 ppm chlorine).

**Step 3: Pressing / Juicing**
- Biological hazard: Pathogen transfer from surface into juice.
- S = 4, L = 4 → **R = 16**.

**Step 4: Pasteurization (the missing CCP)**
- Hazard: *E. coli* O157:H7 is **acid-tolerant** (can survive pH 3.5) and survives cold-storage.
- Critical limit: Achieve **5-log reduction** of *E. coli* O157:H7 — equivalent to **~72 °C for 6 seconds** (FDA Juice HACCP rule, 21 CFR 120, established 2001).
- Odwalla's choice was to **avoid pasteurization** to preserve "fresh" flavor — a **fatally compromised decision**.

**Step 5: Bottling and Cold Distribution**
- Hazard: Survival and slow growth at refrigeration pH.
- Critical limit: pH ≤ 4.0; storage ≤ 4 °C; shelf life ≤ 21 days.

#### 3.3.3 Lessons and Reform

- **FDA Juice HACCP Rule (2001):** Mandatory 5-log reduction for *E. coli* O157:H7 in apple juice and cider.
- **Lesson 1:** "Natural" or "fresh" claims do not exempt processors from validated lethality.
- **Lesson 2:** Supplier control of raw material quality (exclusion of windfall fruit) is critical but not sufficient alone.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Three Case Studies Side by Side

| Dimension | Jack in the Box (1993) | Maple Leaf (2008) | Odwalla (1996) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pathogen | *E. coli* O157:H7 | *L. monocytogenes* | *E. coli* O157:H7 |
| Product | Hamburger patty | RTE deli meat | Unpasteurized apple juice |
| Failed CCP | Cooking | Post-lethality sanitation | Pasteurization (absent) |
| Critical-limit error | 68 °C < required 71 °C | No 7-log lethality validation | No pasteurization at all |
| Monitoring gap | Spot checks | Reactive (not proactive) swabbing | None |
| Corrective-action gap | None defined | Investigation → no line shutdown | "Natural" framing prevented CAPA |
| Deaths | 4 | 22 | 1 |
| Regulator response | USDA HACCP Rule 1996 | CFIA reform | FDA Juice HACCP Rule 2001 |
| Lesson | Validate limits kinetically | FSMS + environmental monitoring + culture | "Fresh" ≠ "safe" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal HACCP Case-Study Analysis Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Case Study] --> step1[Step 1: Define Product, Process Flow, Intended Use]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: List All Steps from Receipt to Distribution]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Conduct Hazard Analysis B C P at Each Step]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Apply Codex Decision Tree at Each Step]
    step4 --> q1{Is Hazard Significant?}
    q1 -- No --> cp[Mark as Control Point CP via PRP]
    q1 -- Yes --> step5[Step 5: Identify CCP and Define Critical Limit]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Specify Monitoring Frequency and Method]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Prescribe Corrective Actions for Deviation]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Define Verification Activities]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: List Required Records and Documentation]
    step9 --> endA[HACCP Plan Complete for Case Study]
```

### 4.2 Codex Decision Tree (Logical Flow for CCP Identification)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1[Q1: Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard?] --> nodeQ1A{Yes or No}
    nodeQ1A -- No --> nodeR1[R1: Not a CCP. Modify process to introduce control.]
    nodeQ1A -- Yes --> nodeQ2[Q2: Does this step eliminate or reduce the hazard to acceptable level?]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ2A{Yes or No}
    nodeQ2A -- Yes --> nodeCCP[This step is a CCP]
    nodeQ2A -- No --> nodeQ3[Q3: Could contamination occur in excess of acceptable level or increase to unacceptable level?]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ3A{Yes or No}
    nodeQ3A -- No --> nodeNot[Not a CCP]
    nodeQ3A -- Yes --> nodeQ4[Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the hazard?]
    nodeQ4 --> nodeQ4A{Yes or No}
    nodeQ4A -- Yes --> nodeNot
    nodeQ4A -- No --> nodeCCP
```

### 4.3 Root-Cause Failure Analysis Tree (For Outbreak Case Studies)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeOutbreak[Foodborne Outbreak Detected] --> nodeID[Identify Pathogen and Vehicle]
    nodeID --> nodeTrace[Trace Back to Processing Step]
    nodeTrace --> nodeFail{Failure Type}
    nodeFail -- HACCP missing entirely --> nodeF1[No plan existed - regulatory failure]
    nodeFail -- CCP wrong --> nodeF2[Critical limit set too lenient]
    nodeFail -- Monitoring failure --> nodeF3[No continuous monitoring]
    nodeFail -- CAPA failure --> nodeF4[Corrective action absent or not triggered]
    nodeFail -- Verification failure --> nodeF5[Records ignored, root cause not investigated]
    nodeFail -- PRP failure --> nodeF6[Sanitation, equipment design, training gap]
    nodeF1 --> nodeFix[Implement full 7-principle plan]
    nodeF2 --> nodeFix2[Revalidate critical limit using microbial kinetics]
    nodeF3 --> nodeFix3[Install continuous chart recorders]
    nodeF4 --> nodeFix4[Define documented CAPA thresholds]
    nodeF5 --> nodeFix5[Establish audit and review committee]
    nodeF6 --> nodeFix6[Strengthen prerequisite programs]
```

### 4.4 Process Flow with CCP Overlay — Hamburger Patty Line (Jack in the Box Schema)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR[Receiving Beef Trimmings] --> nodeS[Refrigerated Storage at 4 C]
    nodeS --> nodeG[Grinding and Forming]
    nodeG --> nodeC[Cooking CCP1 at 71 C internal]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Hot Holding at 57 C CCP2]
    nodeH --> nodeP[Packaging]
    nodeP --> nodeD[Distribution at 4 C]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1] [3 Marks]**
**Q1.** Define a HACCP case study and list the three principal categories used in KTU Module 2 classification.

**Model Answer:**
A HACCP case study is a structured forensic analysis of a real or simulated food operation in which the seven Codex principles are applied. The three principal categories are: (i) Crisis/Lapse case studies (failed HACCP, e.g., Jack in the Box 1993), (ii) Exemplar/Success case studies (HACCP prevented harm, e.g., post-FDA Seafood HACCP operations), and (iii) Industry-Specific Illustrative case studies (dairy, bakery, beverage, RTE, canned, seafood).

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2] [3 Marks]**
**Q2.** State any **three** standardized critical limits used in food HACCP plans, specifying the product and target pathogen for each.

**Model Answer:**
1. Cooking of comminuted meat: ≥ **71 °C** at the geometric center for ≥ 15 s to control *E. coli* O157:H7.
2. HTST pasteurization of milk: **72 °C for 15 s** to control *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* and *Brucella* spp.
3. Apple juice pasteurization: **5-log reduction** of *E. coli* O157:H7 (FDA 21 CFR 120).
4. RTE meat post-lethality: **7-log reduction** of *Listeria monocytogenes* (USDA-FSIS 9 CFR 430).

(Any three suffice.)

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the 1993 *Jack in the Box* E. coli O157:H7 outbreak. Construct a HACCP plan for the hamburger patty production line identifying the CCPs, critical limits, monitoring procedures, corrective actions, and verification activities.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the HACCP failures of *Jack in the Box* (1993) and *Maple Leaf Foods* (2008) with respect to: (i) the failed principle, (ii) the pathogen and product, (iii) the corrective-action gap, and (iv) the regulatory reform that followed.

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

**(a) Model Solution — 7 Marks**

1. **Incident Outline [1 mark]:** In January 1993, 732 lab-confirmed *E. coli* O157:H7 cases (178 hospitalizations, 55 HUS, 4 deaths in children) were traced to undercooked hamburger patties from Jack in the Box. The cooking critical limit was set at 68 °C, which was insufficient.

2. **HACCP Plan for Hamburger Patty Line [6 marks] — full table:**

| Step | Hazard | Severity × Likelihood | CCP? | Critical Limit | Monitoring | Corrective Action | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Receiving beef | *E. coli* O157:H7 | 4 × 4 = 16 | CP (PRP) | Supplier COA; negative *E. coli* testing | Each lot | Reject lot | Quarterly supplier audit |
| Cold storage | Pathogen growth | 4 × 2 = 8 | CP | ≤ 4 °C | Continuous data logger | Quarantine, investigate refrigeration | Annual calibration |
| Grinding/forming | Pathogen distribution | 4 × 4 = 16 | CP (sanitation PRP) | Sanitizer ≥ 200 ppm; equipment swab negative | Hourly ATP; weekly microbial | Sanitize and retest | Environmental mapping |
| **Cooking** | *E. coli* O157:H7 survival | 4 × 4 = 16 | **CCP-1** | ≥ 71 °C at geometric center ≥ 15 s | Continuous probe, chart recorder | Re-cook or discard; calibrate | Hourly end-product microbial |
| **Hot holding** | Pathogen growth | 3 × 3 = 9 | **CCP-2** | ≥ 57 °C | Continuous probe | Discard after 4 h deviation | Daily thermometer calibration |
| Distribution | Recontamination, growth | 3 × 2 = 6 | CP | ≤ 4 °C, sealed packaging | Data logger | Recall investigation | Cold-chain audit |

3. **Critical Justification [1 mark]:** 71 °C is set to provide a 7-log inactivation margin of *E. coli* O157:H7 in beef matrices; lower temperatures (e.g., 68 °C) have D-values that allow survival in cold spots of irregular patties.

---

**(b) Model Solution — 7 Marks**

| Dimension | Jack in the Box (1993) | Maple Leaf Foods (2008) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Failed Principle | Principle 3 (critical limit) and Principle 4 (monitoring) | Principle 1 (hazard analysis) and Principle 5 (corrective action) — environmental monitoring was reactive, not proactive |
| (ii) Pathogen / Product | *E. coli* O157:H7 in hamburger patties | *L. monocytogenes* in RTE deli meats |
| (iii) Corrective-action Gap | No documented CAPA for temperature deviation | Positive *Listeria* swabs did not trigger line shutdown or root-cause disassembly |
| (iv) Regulatory Reform | USDA-FSIS Pathogen Reduction; HACCP Systems Final Rule (1996) | CFIA strengthened; Canada Listeria Policy tightened; FSEP strengthened |

**Synthesis Statement [1 mark]:** Both outbreaks demonstrate that HACCP failures are rarely about the absence of a written plan — they stem from (a) under-conservative critical limits, (b) weak monitoring or CAPA, and (c) missing verification/escalation. Effective HACCP requires a strong food-safety culture with empowered quality personnel.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Apply the seven Codex HACCP principles to develop a complete HACCP plan for the production of **pasteurized apple juice** from fresh apples. State CCPs, critical limits, monitoring procedures, and corrective actions. (Use the post-Odwalla regulatory context.)

**(b) [7 Marks]** An RTE sandwich manufacturer reports *Listeria monocytogenes* in finished product. As a HACCP team leader, walk through the **root-cause investigation** you would conduct. List the immediate corrective actions, the verification steps, and the documentation expected by regulatory auditors.

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

**(a) Model Solution — Pasteurized Apple Juice HACCP Plan [7 Marks]**

| Step | Hazard | S × L | CCP | Critical Limit | Monitoring | Corrective Action | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apple receiving | *E. coli* O157:H7 (windfall fruit) | 4 × 4 = 16 | CP | Exclude windfall fruit; supplier audit; visual inspection | Each truck | Reject load | Annual supplier audit |
| Wash | Soil, microbes | 4 × 3 = 12 | CP | Chlorine 100–200 ppm ORP ≥ 650 mV | Continuous ORP meter | Adjust chlorination | Hourly titration |
| Sorting/cull | Defective fruit | 4 × 4 = 16 | CP | Visual removal of damaged fruit | Continuous line QC | Rework or discard | Daily QA report |
| Crushing/pressing | Pathogen transfer into juice | 4 × 4 = 16 | CP | Equipment sanitation, no juice pooling | Visual + ATP swab | Sanitize; reject batch | Weekly swab |
| **Pasteurization** | *E. coli* O157:H7 acid-tolerant survival | 4 × 4 = 16 | **CCP-1** | ≥ 5-log reduction; ≥ 72 °C × 6 s baseline | Continuous chart recorder; hourly manual check | Divert to re-pasteurize; recalibrate | Daily inoculated pack |
| **Metal detection** | Metal fragments | 2 × 3 = 6 | **CCP-2** | Fe ≤ 2.0 mm; Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm; SS ≤ 3.0 mm | Continuous inline | Quarantine and rework | Hourly test piece |
| Bottling | Recontamination | 3 × 2 = 6 | CP | Sanitary filler; sanitizer 50 ppm | Hourly visual + ATP | Stop line, sanitize | Weekly microbial |
| Cold storage | Pathogen growth | 3 × 2 = 6 | CP | ≤ 4 °C; pH ≤ 4.0; shelf life ≤ 21 days | Continuous data logger | Recall if breached | Annual calibration |

**[1 mark] Synthesis:** The 5-log reduction in pasteurization is the cardinal CCP, established by FDA 21 CFR 120 specifically because *E. coli* O157:H7 is acid-tolerant (survives pH 3.5) and cannot be controlled by downstream pH alone.

---

**(b) Model Solution — Root-Cause Investigation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Immediate Actions (Principle 5) [2 marks]**
- Quarantine all in-process and finished product from the affected lot.
- Stop the production line; hold and retain samples.
- Issue product recall if finished product has reached distribution.
- Initiate trace-back and trace-forward.

**Step 2 — Investigation — Five-Why Analysis [2 marks]**
- Why 1: *L. monocytogenes* found in finished product → Means contamination occurred post-lethality.
- Why 2: Where? → Likely slicing/packaging zone.
- Why 3: Source of contamination? → Likely environmental (condensate, equipment biofilm, hollow rollers).
- Why 4: Why were environmental swabs not acted upon earlier? → No escalation SOP.
- Why 5: Why was equipment design not validated as cleanable? → Weak prerequisite program (sanitation design).

**Step 3 — Verification (Principle 6) [1.5 marks]**
- Comprehensive environmental mapping (≥ 30 sites).
- Disassembly of slicing equipment.
- Retest after sanitization (≥ 5 consecutive negative swabs).
- Third-party challenge study of post-lethality treatment efficacy.

**Step 4 — Documentation (Principle 7) [1.5 marks]**
- HACCP deviation report with timestamp.
- Root-cause report signed by HACCP team leader and plant manager.
- Corrective action log including lot disposition.
- Verification evidence attached.
- Notification to regulatory authority (FSSAI / FDA / CFIA as applicable).

---

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warnings
> - **Do not list hazards without assigning S × L scores.** Examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for the quantitative reasoning step.
> - **Do not write "critical limit = low temperature"** — always quantify (e.g., 71 °C, 4 °C, 200 ppm).
> - **Do not skip the corrective action.** Every CCP must have a CAPA; if you list the CCP without CAPA, expect to lose 50 % of the marks for that row.
> - **Do not write "monitor frequently."** Specify: continuous chart recorder, hourly manual, each lot, etc.
> - **Do not confuse CCP with CP.** CCPs eliminate or prevent hazards; CPs only control them via PRP. Misclassifying gives partial credit only.

---

### Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] HACCP case studies dissect real food operations through the **seven Codex principles**.
- [x] **Three categories**: Crisis, Exemplar, Industry-Specific.
- [x] **Universal framework**: Product → Flow → Hazard Matrix (B/C/P) → Codex Decision Tree → CCPs → Critical Limits → Monitoring → CAPA → Verification → Records.
- [x] **Critical limits to memorize**: 71 °C (comminuted meat), 57 °C (hot-holding), 4 °C (cold storage), −18 °C (frozen), 5-log (juice *E. coli*), 7-log (*Listeria* RTE), 72 °C × 15 s (HTST milk), F₀ ≥ 3 min (retort).
- [x] **Jack in the Box 1993** → Failed CCP-cooking at 68 °C → USDA HACCP Rule 1996.
- [x] **Maple Leaf 2008** → Failed post-lethacity sanitation → Canada Listeria Policy.
- [x] **Odwalla 1996** → Missing pasteurization → FDA Juice HACCP Rule 2001.
- [x] Risk Score **R = S × L** must always be stated explicitly.
- [x] Verification is **not optional** — it includes challenge studies, environmental mapping, audits.
- [x] CAPA requires **predefined escalation thresholds**, not ad-hoc decisions.
- [x] Strong food-safety **culture** and empowered quality leadership underpin any effective HACCP plan.
- [x] In KTU 14-mark answers: always present a **HACCP plan table** with all seven columns.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deviation Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT868 - HACCP PLANNING AND IMPLEMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deviation Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Deviation** in the context of a **HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point)** system is defined as a **non-conformity or failure to meet an established critical limit, operational prerequisite programme (OPRP), or prerequisite programme (PRP) requirement** that has been defined within the food safety management system (FSMS). 

According to **ISO 22000:2018** (the internationally harmonized standard for FSMS adopted by Codex Alimentarius and referenced in KTU's PEFTT868 curriculum), a deviation is any situation where:
- A **Critical Control Point (CCP)** or **Operational Prerequisite Programme (OPRP)** falls outside the **Critical Limit (CL)** or **Action Criterion (AC)**, respectively.
- Monitoring observations diverge from the predefined **acceptable range**.
- A food safety hazard is detected that compromises product safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight (Definitional Standard):**
> Per the **Codex Alimentarius CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020** and **FSSC 22000 V6**, a deviation MUST be treated as a *food safety incident* until proven otherwise. It triggers a mandatory sequence: **Isolation → Investigation → Corrective Action → Verification → Documentation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway with a strict speed limit of **80 km/hr**. You have a speedometer (your *monitoring device*) and a traffic rule book (your *critical limit*). 

- If you drive at **82 km/hr**, the system has **not deviated** because you are within tolerance (analogous to a control chart warning limit).
- If you suddenly hit **95 km/hr**, you have **deviated** from the rule. What do you do?
  1. **Pull over immediately** (CORRECTIVE ACTION step 1 — *bring the process back under control*).
  2. **Identify why** you oversped (CORRECTIVE ACTION step 2 — *root cause analysis*).
  3. **Decide what to do with the journey so far** — if you crashed, the car is damaged; if not, you can resume. In food terms, this is **PRODUCT DISPOSITION** (release, rework, reject, or recall).
  4. **File a report** so police know (CORRECTIVE ACTION step 3 — *documentation & verification*).

Deviation management in HACCP works **exactly the same way**: it is the *emergency response plan* of the food safety system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary for First-Time Learners:**
> A deviation is a "**food safety red flag**" — an event where something went wrong or could go wrong at a CCP/OPRP. Deviation management is the *systematic set of procedures* a food business uses to react, recover, learn, and prevent recurrence.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary (KTU-Standardized)

| Term | Definition | Source Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Deviation** | Non-conformity with a critical limit or action criterion. | ISO 22000:2018, Clause 8.9.5 |
| **Critical Limit (CL)** | A measurable value that separates acceptability from unacceptability at a CCP. | Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969 Rev. 2020 |
| **Action Criterion (AC)** | The criterion of measurable or observable activity that, if achieved, indicates the OPRP is operating as intended. | ISO 22000:2018 |
| **Corrective Action** | Action to eliminate the cause of a non-conformity and prevent recurrence. | ISO 22000:2018, Clause 10.2 |
| **Correction** | Action to control a non-conformity and deal with the immediate consequences. | ISO 9000:2015 (cross-referenced) |
| **Product Disposition** | Decision regarding the fate of non-conforming product (release, rework, reject, down-grade). | Codex & FSSC 22000 |
| **HACCP Team** | Multidisciplinary group responsible for HACCP plan development and review. | Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969 |
| **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** | Systematic process for identifying the underlying cause of a deviation. | Industry best practice / Codex |

## 1.4 Physical / Regulatory Constants in Deviation Management

- **Time-to-Isolation Target**: **≤ 15 minutes** from detection (industry best practice for high-risk CCPs).
- **Closure Time for Corrective Action**: **≤ 30 calendar days** for documented corrective action closure per ISO 22000 Clause 10.2.
- **Investigation Window**: **≤ 24 hours** for initial RCA on critical deviations.
- **Record Retention**: Minimum **3 years** (or product shelf-life + 1 year, whichever is greater) per Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Memorize the *3-tier classification* of deviations: **(1) Critical Limit Deviation (CLD)**, **(2) Operational Deviation (OD)**, and **(3) Observation/Trend Deviation**. Examiners frequently award a 3-mark question on this classification alone.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Deviations (Hierarchical Framework)

Deviations in a HACCP-based FSMS are categorized into **three functional tiers**, each with progressively lower severity but distinct management protocols.

### 2.1.1 Tier 1 — Critical Limit Deviation (CLD)
A **CLD** is the most severe type of deviation. It occurs when measured values at a CCP **exceed the critical limit** OR when the CCP is **not effectively under control**.

**Operational Logic Steps (Why & How):**
1. **Why it matters:** Critical limits are the *last line of defence* against food safety hazards. A breach means a known hazard may have entered the product.
2. **How it is detected:** Continuous or batch monitoring, real-time sensors, laboratory testing, or visual inspection.
3. **Immediate response:** Stop production, isolate product, initiate **Clause 8.9.5 procedure** of ISO 22000:2018.
4. **Long-term response:** Mandatory **corrective action** under Clause 10.2 with root cause analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A pasteurization process has a critical limit of **72 °C for 15 seconds** (the *P*$_{90}$*/*F*$_{0}$ standard for milk). If the recorded temperature falls to **69 °C**, this is a **CLD**. Pathogens like *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* and *Coxiella burnetii* may survive.

### 2.1.2 Tier 2 — Operational Deviation (OD)
An OD occurs at an **OPRP** or PRP when the process drifts from the *action criterion* but has not (yet) reached the CCP critical limit.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. **Why it matters:** OPRPs form the *first line of defence*; their failure increases the probability of CCP failure.
2. **How it is detected:** Trend monitoring, sensor alarms at warning limits, supervisor inspections.
3. **Response:** *Correction* (immediate fix) + documented review. May or may not require formal *corrective action* depending on recurrence frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Sanitation concentration in a CIP (Clean-In-Place) loop drops to **1.8%** from a target of **2.0%**. The CCP is not breached, but the OPRP is drifting. The HACCP team must investigate whether the drift is a *single event* or a *systemic trend*.

### 2.1.3 Tier 3 — Observation / Trend Deviation
A pattern of borderline data that does not yet violate limits but suggests a **statistical shift** in process behaviour.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. **Why it matters:** Six Sigma / SPC principles indicate that trends predict future failures (3-sigma rule).
2. **How it is detected:** Shewhart control charts, CUSUM, EWMA.
3. **Response:** *Preventive action* — proactive adjustment before a CLD occurs.

## 2.2 The 5-Stage Deviation Management Lifecycle

The management of a deviation is a **closed-loop, iterative process** with five clearly defined stages. This is the most exam-relevant framework in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3.

### Stage 1 — Detection & Verification
- **Goal:** Confirm the deviation is *real* (not a sensor error) and *quantify its magnitude*.
- **Tools:** Backup monitoring, secondary measurement, laboratory confirmation.
- **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **monitoring** (routine) with **verification** (confirmation of deviation). Examiners deduct marks if you skip the verification step.

### Stage 2 — Isolation & Containment
- **Goal:** Prevent the non-conforming product from entering the food chain.
- **Actions:** 
  - Quarantine affected batch with **bright-red "HOLD" tag**.
  - Stop the production line if the deviation is ongoing.
  - Use **block-chain traceability lot codes** to identify all related product.

### Stage 3 — Investigation & Root Cause Analysis (RCA)
- **Goal:** Identify the *underlying* cause, not just the *symptom*.
- **Tools:** 
  - **5-Why Analysis** (Toyota Production System)
  - **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** — 6 M's: *Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment)*
  - **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** for complex deviations
  - **Pareto Analysis** (80/20 rule) to prioritize causes

### Stage 4 — Corrective Action Implementation
- **Goal:** Eliminate the *cause* (not just the symptom) and prevent *recurrence*.
- **Hierarchy of Corrective Actions (from weakest to strongest):**
  1. **Correction** (immediate; e.g., recalibrate sensor)
  2. **Concession** (accept under specific conditions)
  3. **Rework/Reprocess** (e.g., re-pasteurize a batch)
  4. **Re-grade/Down-grade** (e.g., divert to animal feed)
  5. **Reject/Dispose** (e.g., incinerate, landfill)
  6. **Recall** (if product has left the facility)

### Stage 5 — Verification & Documentation
- **Goal:** Confirm the corrective action was effective and update the FSMS.
- **Deliverables:** 
  - **Corrective Action Report (CAR)** with all data, decisions, and signatures.
  - **Updated HACCP plan** if the critical limit, monitoring frequency, or CCP designation needs revision.
  - **Management Review input** per ISO 22000 Clause 9.3.

## 2.3 Roles & Responsibilities (RACI Matrix)

| Activity | HACCP Coordinator | Production Supervisor | Quality Manager | Plant Manager | Maintenance Team |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Detection of Deviation | R | R | A | I | I |
| Isolation of Product | I | R | A | I | I |
| Root Cause Analysis | R | C | A | I | C |
| Corrective Action Decision | C | I | R | A | I |
| Implementation | I | R | R | A | R |
| Verification of Effectiveness | R | C | A | I | C |
| Documentation | R | C | A | I | I |

> *Legend:* **R** = Responsible, **A** = Accountable, **C** = Consulted, **I** = Informed.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Deviation management questions often include a **numerical component** based on *Product Disposition* calculations. Master the formulas below.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Boundary | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defect Rate (DR)** | $DR = \dfrac{N_{\text{non-conforming}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | $\vert \cdot \vert$ in % | Quantifies deviation frequency in a batch. |
| 2 | **Deviation Rate (per CCP per shift)** | $D_{\text{rate}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{dev}}}{n_{\text{checks}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Used in trend analysis. |
| 3 | **Sigma Level (process capability)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{Z}$ or $\sigma = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{Z}$ | Same as parameter | Indicates distance of CLD from limit. |
| 4 | **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** | $C_{pk} = \min \left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$ | Dimensionless | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is industry standard for CCPs. |
| 5 | **Critical Limit Margin (Safety Buffer)** | $\text{Margin} = \dfrac{CL - \mu_{\text{current}}}{\sigma}$ | Number of $\sigma$ | A margin $< 2\sigma$ triggers a Tier 3 deviation. |
| 6 | **Recall Efficiency Metric** | $RE = \dfrac{Q_{\text{recalled}}}{Q_{\text{distributed}}} \times 100$ | $\vert \cdot \vert$ in % | Used to assess deviation response effectiveness. |
| 7 | **Time-to-Disposition (TTD)** | $TTD = t_{\text{disposition}} - t_{\text{detection}}$ | Hours / Days | Should be $\leq 48$ hours for high-risk products. |
| 8 | **Cost of Poor Quality (CoPQ)** | $CoPQ = C_{\text{scrap}} + C_{\text{rework}} + C_{\text{recall}} + C_{\text{reputation}}$ | Currency | Business justification for corrective action. |
| 9 | **Batch Hold Quantity** | $Q_{\text{hold}} = Q_{\text{batch}} \times \dfrac{\Delta t_{\text{dev}}}{T_{\text{shift}}}$ | Same as $Q$ | Estimating product affected by deviation. |
| 10 | **Corrective Action Closure Time (CACT)** | $CACT = t_{\text{closure}} - t_{\text{CAR\_open}}$ | Days | Should be $\leq 30$ days per ISO 22000. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Priority for KTU 2024 Board Exam:** Focus on formulas **#1, #3, #4, #5, and #7** as they appear most frequently in numerical problems.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Food Industry Utility

- **Dairy Industry:** Pasteurization temperature deviations in milk processing (target: 72 °C / 15 s) trigger deviation management.
- **Meat Industry:** Cooking temperature deviations in ready-to-eat meat (target: 71.1 °C internal) for *Listeria* control.
- **Pharmaceutical Cross-Application:** Though HACCP is food-specific, the deviation management framework is mirrored in **GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice)** and **Pharmaceutical Quality Systems (PQS)** under ICH Q10.
- **Cold Chain Logistics:** Temperature deviations during transport of chilled foods (target: $\leq 4$ °C) trigger both HACCP deviation and GDP (Good Distribution Practice) protocols.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example: Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) at a CCP

This is the **most frequently asked numerical** in KTU 2024 board exams for Module 3.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A meat processing facility has a CCP for cooking temperature with the following data:
- **Critical Limit (Upper Spec Limit, USL)** = **75 °C**
- **Critical Limit (Lower Spec Limit, LSL)** = **71 °C**
- **Process Mean ($\mu$)** = **72.5 °C**
- **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** = **0.6 °C**

Determine:
1. The Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$).
2. The critical limit margin (in $\sigma$ units).
3. State whether a Tier 3 deviation is indicated.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution (Exhaustive, No Skipping)

**Step 1: Identify Given Variables**
- $USL = 75$ °C
- $LSL = 71$ °C
- $\mu = 72.5$ °C
- $\sigma = 0.6$ °C

**Step 2: Calculate the Upper Capability ($C_{pk\_upper}$)**

The upper process capability measures how well the process mean is centred below the upper critical limit.

$$ C_{pk\_upper} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} $$

Substituting the values:

$$ C_{pk\_upper} = \dfrac{75 - 72.5}{3 \times 0.6} $$

$$ C_{pk\_upper} = \dfrac{2.5}{1.8} $$

$$ C_{pk\_upper} = 1.389 $$

**Step 3: Calculate the Lower Capability ($C_{pk\_lower}$)**

The lower process capability measures how well the process mean is centred above the lower critical limit.

$$ C_{pk\_lower} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} $$

$$ C_{pk\_lower} = \dfrac{72.5 - 71}{3 \times 0.6} $$

$$ C_{pk\_lower} = \dfrac{1.5}{1.8} $$

$$ C_{pk\_lower} = 0.833 $$

**Step 4: Determine the Overall $C_{pk}$**

The overall $C_{pk}$ is the **minimum** of the upper and lower capabilities (the worst-case scenario).

$$ C_{pk} = \min \left( C_{pk\_upper}, C_{pk\_lower} \right) $$

$$ C_{pk} = \min \left( 1.389, \, 0.833 \right) $$

$$ \boxed{C_{pk} = 0.833} $$

**Step 5: Interpret the Result**

Per industry standards (FSSC 22000 & Codex guidance):
- $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ → Process is **capable** (acceptable).
- $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ → Process is **marginally capable** (warning zone).
- $C_{pk} < 1.00$ → Process is **NOT capable** (immediate corrective action required).

Since $C_{pk} = 0.833 < 1.00$, **the process is not capable of consistently meeting the lower critical limit**.

**Step 6: Calculate the Critical Limit Margin (in $\sigma$ units)**

The margin measures how far the process mean is from the LSL in standard deviation units.

$$ \text{Margin} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma} $$

$$ \text{Margin} = \dfrac{72.5 - 71}{0.6} $$

$$ \text{Margin} = \dfrac{1.5}{0.6} $$

$$ \boxed{\text{Margin} = 2.5 \, \sigma} $$

**Step 7: Determine Tier Classification**

Since the margin is **2.5 $\sigma$** (which is $< 3\sigma$), the process is operating in the **warning zone** of the lower critical limit. This qualifies as a **Tier 3 (Observation/Trend) Deviation**, requiring preventive action.

If the margin were $\leq 2\sigma$, it would escalate to a **Tier 2 Operational Deviation**. If the actual temperature reading fell below 71 °C, it would become a **Tier 1 Critical Limit Deviation**.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Product Disposition Calculation (Defect Rate)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A fruit juice bottling line has a CCP for pasteurization. During a 12-hour shift:
- **Total bottles produced:** **48,000**
- **Bottles with temperature deviation (CLD):** **1,440**
- **Bottles currently in process (incomplete batch):** **2,400**

Determine:
1. The Defect Rate (DR).
2. The Quantity of product to be **isolated and held** for disposition.
3. The Disposition Time (TTD) if detection was at 09:00 and decision at 14:00 the same day.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the Defect Rate (DR)**

$$ DR = \dfrac{N_{\text{non-conforming}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100 $$

$$ DR = \dfrac{1{,}440}{48{,}000} \times 100 $$

$$ DR = 0.03 \times 100 $$

$$ \boxed{DR = 3\%} $$

**Step 2: Determine Product Hold Quantity**

The total product affected includes the non-conforming bottles and the in-process bottles (which may share the same thermal history).

$$ Q_{\text{hold}} = N_{\text{non-conforming}} + Q_{\text{in-process}} $$

$$ Q_{\text{hold}} = 1{,}440 + 2{,}400 $$

$$ \boxed{Q_{\text{hold}} = 3{,}840 \text{ bottles}} $$

**Step 3: Calculate the Time-to-Disposition (TTD)**

$$ TTD = t_{\text{disposition}} - t_{\text{detection}} $$

$$ TTD = 14{:}00 - 09{:}00 $$

$$ \boxed{TTD = 5 \text{ hours}} $$

Since TTD $\leq 48$ hours, the response time is **within the industry best-practice window** for high-risk perishable products.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Severity vs. Probability Matrix for Deviation Classification

This matrix is the **gold standard** for HACCP team decision-making on deviation severity.

| | **Low Probability (Rare)** | **Medium Probability (Occasional)** | **High Probability (Frequent)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Catastrophic Severity** (death/allergen exposure) | Tier 1 — *Corrective Action + Verification* | Tier 1 — *CAPA + Management Review* | Tier 1 — *Recall Initiated* |
| **Critical Severity** (serious illness) | Tier 2 — *Correction + Review* | Tier 1 — *CAPA + Recall Decision* | Tier 1 — *Recall + Facility Audit* |
| **Marginal Severity** (discomfort/mild illness) | Tier 3 — *Preventive Action* | Tier 2 — *Correction + RCA* | Tier 2 — *CAPA + Process Review* |
| **Negligible Severity** (no health impact) | Observation Only | Tier 3 — *Preventive Action* | Tier 3 — *Trend Analysis* |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked to classify a deviation, always state: **(1) Severity**, **(2) Probability of recurrence**, **(3) Resulting Tier**, and **(4) Required response**. This four-part answer structure is what KTU examiners reward with full marks.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Deviation Management Lifecycle Flowchart (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    E --> F1["CORRECTION IMMEDIATE FIX"]
    E --> F2["REWORK OR REPROCESS"]
    E --> F3["REJECT OR DISPOSE"]
    E --> F4["RECALL MARKET WITHDRAWAL"]
    F1 --> G["VERIFICATION OF EFFECTIVENESS"]
    F2 --> G["VERIFICATION OF EFFECTIVENESS"]
    F3 --> G["VERIFICATION OF EFFECTIVENESS"]
    F4 --> G["VERIFICATION OF EFFECTIVENESS"]
    G --> H["DOCUMENTATION AND CLOSURE"]
    H --> I["MANAGEMENT REVIEW INPUT"]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Each rectangular node represents a mandatory stage in the deviation management lifecycle. The diamond-shaped decision point (E) branches into 4 alternative disposition pathways (F1–F4), all of which converge at verification (G). The cycle then returns to detection (A) to ensure continuous improvement.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define "Deviation" in the context of a HACCP system. List the three tiers of deviation classification.**

**Model Answer:**

A **deviation** in a HACCP system is any non-conformity with an established **critical limit (CL)** at a CCP or an **action criterion (AC)** at an OPRP, as defined under **ISO 22000:2018, Clause 8.9.5**.

The three tiers of deviation are:
1. **Tier 1 — Critical Limit Deviation (CLD):** Exceeding the critical limit at a CCP; a major food safety breach.
2. **Tier 2 — Operational Deviation (OD):** Drifting from the action criterion at an OPRP without breaching the CCP.
3. **Tier 3 — Observation/Trend Deviation:** Statistical drift indicating a future risk but no current limit breach.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between "Correction" and "Corrective Action" with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Correction | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Action to *control* a non-conformity and deal with immediate consequences. | Action to *eliminate the cause* of non-conformity and prevent recurrence. |
| **Time Frame** | Immediate (minutes to hours). | Short-to-medium term (days to weeks). |
| **Objective** | Containment. | Prevention of recurrence. |
| **Example** | Recalibrating a faulty thermometer reading 65 °C instead of 72 °C. | Replacing the defective temperature sensor module permanently and updating the maintenance SOP. |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the 5-stage Deviation Management Lifecycle with a suitable example from the dairy industry. Draw a flowchart showing the sequence.**

**Model Answer:**

The 5-stage Deviation Management Lifecycle, as adopted from **ISO 22000:2018** and **Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969 Rev. 2020**, comprises:

**Stage 1 — Detection & Verification**
The monitoring system (e.g., continuous temperature recorder on a pasteurizer) detects that the milk temperature has dropped to **68 °C** below the critical limit of **72 °C for 15 seconds**. The Quality Manager (QM) cross-verifies with a calibrated secondary thermometer.

**Stage 2 — Isolation & Containment**
The production line is **immediately stopped**. The in-process batch of **5,000 litres** is diverted to a **quarantine tank** with a bright-red "HOLD" label. Lot codes are documented for traceability.

**Stage 3 — Investigation & Root Cause Analysis (RCA)**
Using a **Fishbone Diagram**, the team identifies the root cause: a **steam valve failure** in the pasteurizer heat exchanger. The 5-Why analysis reveals that preventive maintenance was missed by 3 weeks.

**Stage 4 — Corrective Action Implementation**
- *Correction:* Re-pasteurize the affected batch at **75 °C for 20 seconds** (validated reprocessing protocol).
- *Corrective Action:* Replace the steam valve, update the preventive maintenance schedule, and retrain the maintenance team.

**Stage 5 — Verification & Documentation**
Re-pasteurized product is tested for *alkaline phosphatase* activity (must be negative). The **Corrective Action Report (CAR)** is filed, and the HACCP plan is reviewed to update the **monitoring frequency** from once-per-shift to continuous.

**Flowchart (ASCII representation, refer to Section 4.1 for the Mermaid version):**

$$\text{Detection} \rightarrow \text{Verification} \rightarrow \text{Isolation} \rightarrow \text{RCA} \rightarrow \text{Corrective Action} \rightarrow \text{Verification} \rightarrow \text{Documentation}$$

**(b) [7 Marks] Calculate the Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) for a CCP with the following data: USL = 80 °C, LSL = 75 °C, $\mu$ = 76.5 °C, $\sigma$ = 0.8 °C. Comment on the process capability.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate Upper Capability**

$$ C_{pk\_upper} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{80 - 76.5}{3 \times 0.8} = \dfrac{3.5}{2.4} = 1.458 $$

**Step 2: Calculate Lower Capability**

$$ C_{pk\_lower} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{76.5 - 75}{3 \times 0.8} = \dfrac{1.5}{2.4} = 0.625 $$

**Step 3: Determine Overall $C_{pk}$**

$$ C_{pk} = \min(1.458, 0.625) = \boxed{0.625} $$

**Comment on Capability:**
Since $C_{pk} = 0.625 < 1.00$, the process is **NOT capable** of consistently meeting the lower critical limit. This indicates an **imminent Tier 1 critical limit deviation risk**. Immediate engineering controls and corrective action are required to re-centre the process mean and reduce variability.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the four product disposition options available in deviation management with appropriate food industry examples.**

**Model Answer:**

When a deviation occurs, the food business has **four primary product disposition options**, each with a specific decision criterion:

**1. Release (with Concession)**
- **Definition:** Product is released into commerce after a documented risk assessment and regulatory acceptance.
- **Example:** A minor labelling deviation where the ingredient declaration is in the wrong font size but legible. Released with a concession note.
- **Pre-condition:** No food safety hazard; quality/regulatory concession only.

**2. Rework / Reprocess**
- **Definition:** Product is re-processed through a validated step to bring it back into specification.
- **Example:** Re-pasteurizing an under-processed milk batch at 75 °C for 20 seconds after the deviation is identified.
- **Pre-condition:** Validated re-processing protocol with verified CCP re-control.

**3. Re-grade / Down-grade**
- **Definition:** Product is diverted to an alternative lower-value use (e.g., animal feed, industrial non-food application).
- **Example:** Fruit pulp with excess pesticide residue beyond the human consumption MRL but within animal feed limits is sold to a feed manufacturer.
- **Pre-condition:** Regulatory acceptance for the alternative use.

**4. Reject / Dispose**
- **Definition:** Product is destroyed via incineration, landfill, or rendering.
- **Example:** Meat contaminated with *Salmonella* is incinerated with a certificate of destruction.
- **Pre-condition:** No safe alternative use; mandatory for severe food safety hazards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Decision Logic:** The disposition decision is based on: **(i) Hazard severity**, **(ii) Effectiveness of re-processing**, **(iii) Regulatory acceptability**, and **(iv) Economic feasibility**. Always default to the *safest* option when in doubt.

**(b) [7 Marks] Construct a RACI matrix for a typical deviation event in a food processing facility. Identify the 4 key personnel and explain their roles.**

**Model Answer:**

**RACI Matrix for Deviation Management:**

| Activity | HACCP Coordinator | Production Supervisor | Quality Manager | Plant Manager |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Detection of Deviation | R | R | A | I |
| Verification of Deviation | R | C | A | I |
| Isolation of Product | C | R | A | I |
| Root Cause Analysis | R | C | A | I |
| Corrective Action Decision | C | I | R | A |
| Implementation of Action | C | R | R | A |
| Verification of Effectiveness | R | C | A | I |
| Documentation (CAR) | R | C | A | I |
| Management Review | R | I | R | A |

**Roles of the 4 Key Personnel:**

1. **HACCP Coordinator (R in most activities):** Leads the investigation, performs RCA, drafts the CAR, and provides technical expertise on the HACCP plan.
2. **Production Supervisor (R in implementation):** Owns the production line, executes the immediate correction, stops the line, and ensures operator-level compliance with the corrective action.
3. **Quality Manager (A in most activities):** The *single point of accountability*; makes the final disposition decision (release, rework, reject, recall), signs off on the CAR, and reports to the Plant Manager.
4. **Plant Manager (A in escalation):** The escalation point for severe deviations; authorizes the decision to **recall** product from the market and provides the final approval for major corrective actions. Also the chairperson of the **Management Review** meeting.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> A common mistake is to write a "task list" instead of a true RACI matrix. The four-letter code **(R, A, C, I)** MUST be assigned to every cell. Students who merely list "who does what" without filling the matrix lose **2–3 marks**. Additionally, the *Quality Manager* is typically **Accountable (A)**, not Responsible (R), for disposition decisions — students often invert this and lose 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (For Last-Minute KTU Exam Preparation):**

- ☐ **Deviation** = Non-conformity with **Critical Limit (CL)** or **Action Criterion (AC)** per ISO 22000:2018 Clause 8.9.5.
- ☐ **Three Tiers of Deviation:** Tier 1 (CLD — CCP breach), Tier 2 (OD — OPRP drift), Tier 3 (Trend — statistical warning).
- ☐ **5-Stage Lifecycle:** Detection → Verification → Isolation → RCA → Corrective Action → (Verification & Documentation).
- ☐ **RCA Tools:** 5-Why, Fishbone (6 M's: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Environment), Fault Tree Analysis, Pareto.
- ☐ **4 Disposition Options:** Release (with concession), Rework, Re-grade, Reject (+ Recall if product is in market).
- ☐ **Hierarchy of Corrective Actions:** Correction → Concession → Rework → Re-grade → Reject → Recall.
- ☐ **Key Formulas to Memorize:** $DR$, $C_{pk}$, Margin in $\sigma$, $TTD \leq 48$ hours, $CACT \leq 30$ days.
- ☐ **RACI Roles:** HACCP Coordinator (R), Quality Manager (A), Production Supervisor (R-implementation), Plant Manager (A-escalation).
- ☐ **Industry Standards:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (capable), 1.00–1.33 (marginal), $< 1.00$ (not capable — CAPA required).
- ☐ **Documentation Time:** CAR closure within **30 days**; record retention **3 years** (or shelf-life + 1 year).
- ☐ **Regulatory Anchors:** Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969 Rev. 2020, ISO 22000:2018, FSSC 22000 V6, BRCGS Issue 9, SQF Edition 9.
- ☐ **Cross-Industry Application:** Deviation management framework is mirrored in **Pharma GMP (ICH Q10)** and **Automotive ISO/IATF 16949**.
- ☐ **Most-Tested Concept:** $C_{pk}$ calculation (numerical) + 5-Stage Lifecycle (theory) — practice at least 3 numerical problems before the exam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Exam Strategy:** 
> For a 14-mark question on Deviation Management, structure your answer as: **(1) Definition with standard reference (2 marks)**, **(2) Tier classification with example (3 marks)**, **(3) 5-Stage Lifecycle with flowchart (5 marks)**, **(4) Disposition decision with logic (2 marks)**, **(5) Conclusion with regulatory anchor (2 marks)**. This distribution mirrors KTU's valuation key and maximizes score.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CAPA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT868 - HACCP PLANNING AND IMPLEMENTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** CAPA # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Actions):** A structured, closed-loop quality management methodology mandated under **ISO 22000:2018 (Clause 8.7, 8.9.3, 10.2)** and **Codex Alimentarius HACCP Principles**, designed to eliminate the causes of **non-conformities** (Corrective Action) and to prevent their potential occurrence (Preventive Action), thereby ensuring continuous improvement of the **Food Safety Management System (FSMS)**. In the context of HACCP, **CAPA** is the operational engine that transforms monitoring data, deviation reports, verification results, and customer complaints into **systematic, documented improvements** within the food safety plan. It is the bridge between **detection** of a hazard and the **permanent elimination or reduction** of that hazard to an Acceptable Level of Protection (ALOP). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Smart Home Maintenance System** > > Imagine your house has a water leakage sensor in the basement (**Monitoring**). One day, the alarm rings — water on the floor. > > - **Corrective Action (CA):** You call a plumber, fix the pipe, and mop the floor.
