> Although HACCP is not a calculus-based subject, KTU examiners expect **structured logical derivations**. Below, we walk through the canonical **"Cooked Chicken Nugget Case Study"** — a frequently-asked KTU case — applying the **12-stage logic** in a step-by-step analytical derivation.

## 3.1 Worked Case Study: Frozen Cooked Chicken Nuggets (Process Flow)

### 3.1.1 Product Description
- **Product:** Frozen, fully cooked, breaded chicken nuggets.
- **Packaging:** Polyethylene pouches, vacuum-sealed, 1 kg.
- **Shelf life:** **12 months** at $\leq -18^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Target consumer:** General public, including children.

### 3.1.2 Intended Use
- Reheat in oven/microwave to internal temperature of $\geq 74^\circ\text{C}$ before consumption. May be eaten by immune-compromised, hence **Class A risk product**.

### 3.1.3 Process Flow Diagram (Textual Block Representation)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Raw Chicken Receiving} \rightarrow \text{Storage} (-18^\circ\text{C}) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Thawing} (4^\circ\text{C}, 24\text{ h}) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Boning, Skinning, Grinding} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Formulation} (\text{minced meat + seasoning + binder}) \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Shaping / Molding} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Breading (predust + batter + crumbs)} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{Pre-frying} (180^\circ\text{C}, 30\text{ s}) \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \text{Flash Frying} (175^\circ\text{C}, \text{to internal } 74^\circ\text{C}) \\
\text{Step 9:} \quad & \text{Cooling} (\text{ambient}, 15\text{ min}) \\
\text{Step 10:} \quad & \text{Freezing} (\text{blast freezer, } -35^\circ\text{C}) \\
\text{Step 11:} \quad & \text{Metal Detection} \\
\text{Step 12:} \quad & \text{Packaging} \\
\text{Step 13:} \quad & \text{Storage} (-18^\circ\text{C}) \\
\text{Step 14:} \quad & \text{Distribution}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 1: Hazard Analysis (HA)

We tabulate hazards at each step using the **$R = S \times L$** semi-quantitative model.

> **Scoring Legend:** Severity: $S = 1$ (negligible), $S = 2$ (minor), $S = 3$ (catastrophic, life-threatening). Likelihood: $L = 1$ (rare), $L = 2$ (occasional), $L = 3$ (frequent).

| **Step** | **Biological (B)** | **Chemical (C)** | **Physical (P)** | **Significant?** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Raw chicken receiving | *Salmonella* (S=3, L=3, **R=9**) | Antibiotic residues (S=2, L=2, **R=4**) | Bone fragments (S=2, L=3, **R=6**) | YES (B,P) |
| 2. Thawing | *Salmonella* growth (S=3, L=3, **R=9**) | None significant | None | YES (B) |
| 3. Boning/Grinding | Cross-contamination (S=3, L=2, **R=6**) | Lubricant leak (S=2, L=1, R=2) | Metal fragments (S=3, L=2, **R=6**) | YES (B,P) |
| 4. Formulation | None new | Allergen (soy) (S=3, L=2, **R=6**) | None | YES (C) |
| 5. Shaping | None | None | None | No |
| 6. Breading | *Listeria* from crumbs (S=3, L=2, **R=6**) | Allergen cross-contact (S=3, L=2, **R=6**) | None | YES (B,C) |
| 7. Pre-frying | *Salmonella* kill start (S=3, L=2, **R=6**) | Acrylamide formation (S=2, L=2, **R=4**) | None | YES (B) |
| 8. Flash frying | **Final kill step** (S=3, L=1, **R=3** with proper CL) | Acrylamide (S=2, L=2, **R=4**) | None | **YES — CCP-1** |
| 9. Cooling | Recontamination (S=3, L=3, **R=9**) | None | None | **YES — CCP-2** |
| 10. Freezing | Microbial stasis | None | None | No |
| 11. Metal detection | None | None | Metal fragments (S=3, L=3, **R=9**) | **YES — CCP-3** |
| 12. Packaging | None | Allergen labelling error (S=3, L=2, **R=6**) | Foreign body (S=2, L=2, **R=4**) | YES (C) |
| 13. Storage | None | None | None | No |
| 14. Distribution | Cold-chain breach (S=3, L=2, **R=6**) | None | None | YES (B) |

### 3.1.5 Step 2: CCP Determination Using Codex Decision Tree

At **Step 8 (Flash Frying)**, we apply the Codex Decision Tree:

- **Q1:** Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard? **YES** (heat treatment).
- **Q2:** Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the likely occurrence of the hazard to an acceptable level? **YES** ($\geq 74^\circ\text{C}$ kills *Salmonella* with a $7D$ reduction).
- $\Rightarrow$ **CCP-1** (Critical Control Point for *Salmonella* destruction).

At **Step 9 (Cooling)**, we apply:

- **Q1:** Do preventive control measures exist? **YES** (controlled temp/time).
- **Q2:** Could contamination with the hazard(s) identified occur in excess of acceptable level(s), or could these increase to unacceptable level(s)? **YES** (*Listeria* growth in 20–45 °C "Danger Zone").
- **Q3:** Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the hazard to acceptable level? **NO** (freezing only arrests growth).
- **Q4:** Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard to an acceptable level? **NO**.
- $\Rightarrow$ **CCP-2** (Critical Control Point for preventing recontamination/growth).

At **Step 11 (Metal Detection)**, the hazard is **physical** (metal fragments), and the decision tree yields:

- **Q1:** Do preventive control measures exist? **YES** (metal detector).
- $\Rightarrow$ **CCP-3** (Critical Control Point for foreign body).

### 3.1.6 Step 3: Critical Limits, Monitoring, Corrective Actions, Verification

| **CCP** | **Critical Limit** | **Monitoring** | **Corrective Action** | **Verification** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CCP-1** (Frying) | Internal temp $\geq 74^\circ\text{C}$ for $\geq 15\text{ s}$ | Continuous probe (every 30 min calibration check) | Divert batch to rework; calibrate fryer; retrain operator | Daily microbial plate count (APC, *Salmonella* absence in 25 g) |
| **CCP-2** (Cooling) | $74^\circ\text{C} \rightarrow 4^\circ\text{C}$ in $\leq 90$ min; product never in $20-45^\circ\text{C}$ for $\geq 30$ min | Continuous data logger (1-min interval) | Hold product; assess CCP-1; destroy if Listeria suspected | Weekly *Listeria* environmental swabs |
| **CCP-3** (Metal Detection) | Fe $\leq 1.5\text{ mm}$, Non-Fe $\leq 2.0\text{ mm}$, SS $\leq 2.5\text{ mm}$ | Continuous; hourly test-piece check | Quarantine last "good" check; re-screen; reject | 4-hourly calibration; daily audit of reject bin |

### 3.1.7 Risk Score Numerical Derivations (Selected Examples)

For *Salmonella* at **Step 1** (Raw chicken receiving):

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{Salmonella, Step 1}} &= S \times L \\
&= 3 \times 3 \\
&= 9
\end{aligned}$$

Since $R = 9 \geq 4$, the hazard is **significant** and must be addressed by a **CCP, oPRP, or PRP**.

For *Salmonella* at **Step 8** (post-CCP-1 kill):

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{Salmonella, Step 8}} &= S \times L_{\text{post-kill}} \\
&= 3 \times 1 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}$$

Since $R = 3 \leq 3$, the hazard has been **reduced to acceptable level** by CCP-1.

> [!TIP]
> **Validation Logic Derivation:**
> A CCP-1 cook is **validated** if the process achieves a $7\text{-}\log_{10}$ reduction of *Salmonella*. Starting load $N_0 = 10^4\text{ CFU/g}$:
> $$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{final}} &= N_0 \times 10^{-7} \\
&= 10^4 \times 10^{-7} \\
&= 10^{-3}\text{ CFU/g} \quad \text{(i.e., effectively zero)}
\end{aligned}$$
> This is the standard **process validation** mathematics.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Case Study Benchmarking

| **Case Study** | **Year** | **Hazard** | **CCP Missed** | **Consequence** | **Regulatory Outcome** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Jack in the Box** | 1993 | *E. coli* O157:H7 | Cooking temp verification (CL = 155 °F) | 732 illnesses, 4 child deaths, $>\$50$ million losses | USDA-FSIS Pathogen Reduction/HACPRule (1996) |
| **Odwalla Apple Juice** | 1996 | *E. coli* O157:H7 | No pasteurization CCP | 70 illnesses, 1 death | FDA mandated HACCP for juice (21 CFR 120) |
| **Peanut Corp. of America** | 2008–09 | *Salmonella* Typhimurium | Roasting CL not monitored; lab records falsified | 714 illnesses, 9 deaths, $\sim$\$1 billion recall cost | Federal prison sentences; new FDA Food Safety Modernization Act (FSMA, 2011) |
| **Fonterra Botulism Scare** | 2013 | *Clostridium botulinum* (false positive) | Communication failure | Recall of infant formula globally | Industry-wide communication protocol overhaul |
| **Blue Bell Creameries** | 2015 | *Listeria monocytogenes* | Environmental monitoring & GMP failure | 10 illnesses, 3 deaths | FDA shut down production; criminal conviction |
| **Cargill Turkey (Salmonella)** | 2011 | *Salmonella* Heidelberg | Insufficient cooking CL; CCP not validated | 136 illnesses, 1 death | Largest ground turkey recall in US history (36 million lbs) |
| **India: Maggi Noodles Lead** | 2015 | Lead $\geq 7\text{ ppm}$ (limit = 2.5 ppm) | Vendor non-compliance, no incoming CL | FSSAI nationwide ban, $\sim$₹320 crore recall | Strengthened FSSAI testing protocols (2016) |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards, the following **Mermaid diagrams** use purely alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `stepA`, `node1`) and raw uppercase text in labels — no bold/italic markdown or HTML inside quoted labels.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: HACCP Case-Study Application Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([KTU CASE STUDY INTAKE]) --> ps1[STAGE 1: ASSEMBLE HACCP TEAM]
    ps1 --> ps2[STAGE 2: DESCRIBE PRODUCT]
    ps2 --> ps3[STAGE 3: IDENTIFY INTENDED USE]
    ps3 --> ps4[STAGE 4: CONSTRUCT PROCESS FLOW DIAGRAM]
    ps4 --> ps5[STAGE 5: ON SITE VERIFICATION OF FLOW]
    ps5 --> p1[PRINCIPLE 1: HAZARD ANALYSIS HA]
    p1 --> p2[PRINCIPLE 2: CCP DETERMINATION USING CODEX DECISION TREE]
    p2 --> p3[PRINCIPLE 3: ESTABLISH CRITICAL LIMITS CL]
    p3 --> p4[PRINCIPLE 4: ESTABLISH MONITORING PROCEDURES]
    p4 --> p5[PRINCIPLE 5: ESTABLISH CORRECTIVE ACTIONS CA]
    p5 --> p6[PRINCIPLE 6: ESTABLISH VERIFICATION PROCEDURES]
    p6 --> p7[PRINCIPLE 7: DOCUMENTATION AND RECORD KEEPING]
    p7 --> audit1{INTERNAL AUDIT PASS}
    audit1 -- NO --> caLoop[ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS AND PLAN REVISION]
    caLoop --> p1
    audit1 -- YES --> cert[RELEASE FOR IMPLEMENTATION]
    cert --> thirdparty[THIRD PARTY CERTIFICATION FSSC22000 BRCGS SQF]
    thirdparty --> liveop([LIVE OPERATION WITH CONTINUAL IMPROVEMENT])
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Worked Chicken Nugget CCP Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawChick[RAW CHICKEN RECEIVING] --> thaw[THAWING]
    thaw --> process[GRINDING FORMULATION SHAPING]
    process --> bread[BREADING]
    bread --> prefry[PREFYING]
    prefry --> ccp1Node((CCP1 FLASH FRYING 74C))
    ccp1Node --> cool[COOLING STEP]
    cool --> ccp2Node((CCP2 COOLING TIME TEMP))
    ccp2Node --> freeze[BLAST FREEZING]
    freeze --> ccp3Node((CCP3 METAL DETECTION FE 1.5MM NONFE 2.0MM SS 2.5MM))
    ccp3Node --> pack[PACKAGING LABELLING]
    pack --> store[STORAGE MINUS 18C]
    store --> ship[DISTRIBUTION]
```

## 4.3 Codex Decision Tree — Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Q1: DO PREVENTIVE CONTROL MEASURES EXIST FOR THE IDENTIFIED HAZARD}
    q1 -- NO --> notCCP[NOT A CCP - CONTROL VIA PRPS OPRPS]
    q1 -- YES --> q2{Q2: IS THIS STEP SPECIFICALLY DESIGNED TO ELIMINATE OR REDUCE THE LIKELY OCCURRENCE OF THE HAZARD TO AN ACCEPTABLE LEVEL}
    q2 -- YES --> ccpYES[CCP IDENTIFIED]
    q2 -- NO --> q3{Q3: COULD CONTAMINATION WITH THE HAZARD IDENTIFIED OCCUR IN EXCESS OF ACCEPTABLE LEVELS OR COULD THESE INCREASE TO UNACCEPTABLE LEVELS}
    q3 -- NO --> notCCP2[NOT A CCP]
    q3 -- YES --> q4{Q4: WILL A SUBSEQUENT STEP ELIMINATE OR REDUCE THE IDENTIFIED HAZARD TO AN ACCEPTABLE LEVEL}
    q4 -- NO --> ccpYES2[CCP IDENTIFIED]
    q4 -- YES --> notCCP3[NOT A CCP - ADDRESSED LATER]
```

## 4.4 Risk Decision Matrix (Semi-Quantitative)

| **Likelihood $\downarrow$ \ Severity $\rightarrow$** | **S = 1 Negligible** | **S = 2 Minor** | **S = 3 Catastrophic** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L = 3 Frequent** | $R=3$ (Acceptable, PRP) | $R=6$ (Significant) | $R=9$ (CCP) |
| **L = 2 Occasional** | $R=2$ (Acceptable) | $R=4$ (Significant) | $R=6$ (CCP) |
| **L = 1 Rare** | $R=1$ (Negligible) | $R=2$ (Acceptable) | $R=3$ (Acceptable) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Rule:** $R \geq 4 \Rightarrow$ Significant hazard; must be controlled by a **CCP, oPRP, or PRP**. $R \leq 3 \Rightarrow$ Acceptable level after standard PRPs.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a HACCP case study. List any three famous real-world foodborne illness outbreaks that led to mandatory HACCP implementation. (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A HACCP case study is a documented, systematic investigation of a specific food operation in which the seven principles of HACCP are applied, audited, or critically evaluated to derive actionable learning.

Three real-world outbreaks:
1. **Jack in the Box E. coli O157:H7 outbreak (1993)** — led to USDA-FSIS Pathogen Reduction / HACCP Rule (1996).
2. **Odwalla Apple Juice E. coli O157:H7 (1996)** — led to FDA Juice HACCP regulation 21 CFR 120.
3. **Peanut Corporation of America Salmonella Typhimurium (2008–09)** — led to FSMA (2011).

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between a CCP and an oPRP with one industry example each. (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **CCP (Critical Control Point)** | **oPRP (Operational Prerequisite Programme)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Level** | High; significant hazard | Moderate; controls likelihood of contamination |
| **Measurable CL** | Yes (mandatory quantitative CL) | May be qualitative or action-level |
| **Decision Tree Outcome** | Identified as CCP | Not classified as CCP but control measure needed |
| **Example (Poultry)** | Cooking to internal $\geq 74^\circ\text{C}$ to kill *Salmonella* | Sanitation of cutting boards to control cross-contamination |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **frequently lose 3–4 marks** by *forgetting the Codex Decision Tree application* and merely listing hazards without CCP logic.
> - Always quote the **specific CL value** (e.g., $74^\circ\text{C}/15\text{ s}$, $1.5\text{ mm Fe}$) — vague answers are penalized.
> - The **5 preliminary tasks** must be explicitly mentioned before the 7 principles for full marks.
> - Do NOT confuse **oPRP (operational Prerequisite Programme)** with **PRP** — KTU examiners specifically look for this distinction since **ISO 22000:2018** introduced the term.
> - **Verification** must include *both* internal audit AND end-product microbiological testing, not just one.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Real-Time Dairy Case Study**
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`

A private dairy in Kerala produces **pasteurized toned milk (3% fat, 8.5% SNF)** with a 7-day refrigerated shelf life at $4-6^\circ\text{C}$. Raw milk is sourced from local farmers, transported in cans, received at $8-10^\circ\text{C}$, tested for **antibiotic residues, somatic cell count, and total plate count**, then standardized, pasteurized at $72^\circ\text{C}/15\text{ s}$ (HTST), homogenized, cooled to $4^\circ\text{C}$, and packed in 500 mL LDPE pouches.

**(a)** Construct the complete HACCP plan for this operation, applying the **7 principles** and identifying at least **3 CCPs** with their **critical limits, monitoring procedures, corrective actions, and verification activities**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **lessons learned** from the **2013 Fonterra botulism scare** and the **2015 Maggi noodles lead contamination case**, highlighting how a robust HACCP plan would have prevented each. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Preliminary Steps:**

- **Team:** Plant manager, QA head, production supervisor, microbiologist, veterinarian.
- **Product:** Pasteurized toned milk, 3% fat, 8.5% SNF, pH $\approx 6.6 - 6.8$, $a_w \approx 0.99$.
- **Intended use:** General consumption, including children, after refrigerated storage.
- **Flow diagram:** Receiving $\rightarrow$ Pre-storage $\rightarrow$ Standardization $\rightarrow$ Pasteurization $\rightarrow$ Homogenization $\rightarrow$ Cooling $\rightarrow$ Packaging $\rightarrow$ Cold storage $\rightarrow$ Distribution.

**Hazard Analysis:**

| **Step** | **Hazard** | **Significant?** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Receiving | *Brucella*, *Mycobacterium bovis*; antibiotic residue | YES (B,C) | $R = 9$ for *Brucella*; antibiotic inhibits starter cultures |
| Pre-storage | Pathogen growth at 8-10°C | YES (B) | $R = 6$ |
| Pasteurization | Pathogen survival if CL breached | YES (B) | $R = 9$ without CL control |
| Homogenization | None significant | NO | — |
| Cooling | Recontamination | YES (B) | $R = 6$ |
| Packaging | Allergen mislabel; foreign body | YES (C,P) | $R = 6$ |
| Distribution | Cold-chain breach | YES (B) | $R = 6$ |

**CCP Determination (Codex Decision Tree):**

- **CCP-1: Pasteurization** — Q1=YES, Q2=YES $\Rightarrow$ CCP.
  - **CL:** $72^\circ\text{C} \geq T \geq 75^\circ\text{C}$ for $\geq 15\text{ s}$; $\text{Flow rate} \leq 5000\text{ L/h}$.
  - **Monitoring:** Continuous chart recorder + thermograph; flow diversion valve auto-activates if CL breached.
  - **CA:** Divert to raw milk silo; check thermograph; reprocess; investigate cause.
  - **Verification:** Daily alkaline phosphatase test (must be negative); weekly coliform / *Salmonella* testing of finished milk.

- **CCP-2: Antibiotic Residue Testing at Receiving** — Q1=YES, Q2=YES $\Rightarrow$ CCP.
  - **CL:** Negative on **Delvotest** or **Charm Rosa** strip.
  - **Monitoring:** Every tanker, before unloading.
  - **CA:** Reject tanker; communicate to farmer; quarantine.
  - **Verification:** Quarterly third-party lab cross-checks.

- **CCP-3: Cold Storage Temperature** — Q1=YES, Q2=YES $\Rightarrow$ CCP (controls psychrotrophic *Listeria* / *Pseudomonas*).
  - **CL:** $\leq 4^\circ\text{C}$ at all times.
  - **Monitoring:** Continuous data logger, 5-min interval; alarm at $5^\circ\text{C}$.
  - **CA:** Quarantine stock; investigate refrigeration failure; assess shelf-life remaining.
  - **Verification:** Daily log review; weekly calibration of probes.

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Fonterra 2013:**
- The scare was triggered by a false-positive detection of *Clostridium botulinum* in whey protein concentrate. The actual root cause was *never a confirmed contamination*, but **communication failure** between Fonterra's quality team and supply chain.
- **HACCP Lesson:** A robust HACCP system requires not only control of biological hazards but also **Prerequisite Programmes (PRPs)** for traceability, supplier control, and crucially, **internal communication protocols**. The verification principle (Principle 6) must include review of communication channels. Fonterra lacked a documented escalation matrix for ambiguous test results.

**Maggi Noodles 2015:**
- FSSAI found lead at $7\text{ ppm}$ against a regulatory limit of $2.5\text{ ppm}$ in instant noodles. Root cause: **supplier non-compliance** for lead in wheat flour, and inadequate **incoming material control**.
- **HACCP Lesson:** A CCP for **incoming raw material inspection** (heavy metal testing) would have been appropriate. Principle 1 (Hazard Analysis) would have identified lead as a significant chemical hazard in the wheat flour supply chain. The corrective action for failed raw material must be a documented **vendor rejection and supplier audit escalation**, not a silent acceptance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Seafood HACCP Case Study (Alternative Choice)**
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`

A shrimp processing plant in Kerala exports **frozen raw peeled and deveined (PD) shrimp** to Japan and the EU. Process flow: Receiving (iced shrimp at $0-2^\circ\text{C}$) $\rightarrow$ Washing $\rightarrow$ Deheading $\rightarrow$ Peeling $\rightarrow$ Deveining $\rightarrow$ Sorting/Grading $\rightarrow$ Freezing (individual quick frozen at $-35^\circ\text{C}$) $\rightarrow$ Glazing $\rightarrow$ Metal detection $\rightarrow$ Weighing/Packing $\rightarrow$ Cold storage ($-20^\circ\text{C}$) $\rightarrow$ Dispatch.

**(a)** Identify **at least 4 significant hazards** (B/C/P) at different process steps, apply the **Codex Decision Tree**, and establish the **HACCP plan table** (CCP, CL, monitoring, CA, verification). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyze the **Jack in the Box (1993)** outbreak and explain how it shaped the modern HACCP regulatory framework globally, including the specific **critical limit** that was violated. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Significant Hazards & CCPs:**

1. **CCP-1: Receiving Temperature**
   - **Hazard:** *Vibrio cholerae*, *V. parahaemolyticus*, histamine formation (biogenic amine).
   - **CL:** Shrimp temperature $\leq 2^\circ\text{C}$ at receiving; histamine $\leq 50\text{ mg/kg}$ (EU Reg. 2073/2005).
   - **Monitoring:** Probe temperature per lot; histamine testing every batch.
   - **CA:** Reject lot if CL breached; investigate cold chain.

2. **CCP-2: Metal Detection**
   - **Hazard:** Metal fragments from wire baskets / conveyor wear.
   - **CL:** Fe $\leq 1.5\text{ mm}$, Non-Fe $\leq 2.0\text{ mm}$, SS $\leq 2.5\text{ mm}$.
   - **Monitoring:** Continuous; test-piece hourly.
   - **CA:** Re-screen; isolate; root-cause investigation.

3. **CCP-3: Freezing**
   - **Hazard:** *Listeria* growth, histamine if not rapid.
   - **CL:** Reach $-18^\circ\text{C}$ core within $\leq 2\text{ h}$ of freezing tunnel exit.
   - **Monitoring:** Continuous probe; time-temperature recorder.
   - **CA:** Hold lot; assess; release only if CL met.

4. **oPRP: Sanitation of Peeling Tables**
   - **Hazard:** Cross-contamination by *Salmonella*, *Listeria*.
   - **Control:** Chlorine 100 ppm; swab test weekly.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

The **Jack in the Box outbreak (1993)** involved undercooked hamburgers contaminated with *E. coli* O157:H7. **732 cases, 4 child deaths.** The **critical limit violated** was the cooking temperature: USDA's standard was $155^\circ\text{F}$ ($68.3^\circ\text{C}$) for 15 seconds, but Jack in the Box's CL was lower, and patties were undercooked.

**Regulatory aftermath:**
- USDA-FSIS issued the **Pathogen Reduction / HACCP Final Rule (1996)** — mandatory HACCP for meat and poultry.
- Established **Salmonella** performance standards.
- Mandated **Generic E. coli** testing as an indicator organism.
- Triggered international adoption — including the **FSSAI's mandate** for HACCP-based Food Safety Management Systems in India (FSS Act 2006).

The case demonstrated that a *CCP must have a scientifically-validated, measurable, and enforced CL* — and that *without it*, the entire food safety system collapses.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Recap — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **HACCP = 7 Principles** built on **5 Preliminary Tasks** (Team, Product, Intended Use, Flow Diagram, On-site Verification).
- **BCPR** = Biological, Chemical, Physical, Radiological hazards.
- **Risk Score Formula:** $R = S \times L$; $R \geq 4 \Rightarrow$ Significant.
- **Codex Decision Tree** has **4 sequential questions** — committing this to memory is non-negotiable.
- **Critical Limit** must be **measurable, quantitative, and validated** (e.g., $74^\circ\text{C}/15\text{ s}$ for poultry).
- **Monitoring must be CONTINUOUS wherever possible** (especially for CCPs).
- **Corrective Action** = "What do we do when CL is breached?" — must include **isolation, investigation, and prevention**.
- **Verification = audit + testing + calibration**, NOT just paper review.
- **Real-world case studies** to remember:
  - Jack in the Box (1993) → USDA HACCP Rule (1996)
  - Odwalla (1996) → 21 CFR 120 Juice HACCP
  - PCA (2008) → FSMA 2011
  - Fonterra (2013) → Communication protocols
  - Maggi (2015) → Supplier / incoming-material HACCP
- **FSSAI legal anchor:** Food Safety and Standards Act 2006 + FSS (Food Safety Management Systems) Regulations 2011.
- **Global standards to know:** **Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969 Rev. 2020**, **ISO 22000:2018**, **GFSI-benchmarked schemes** (BRCGS Issue 9, SQF Ed. 9, FSSC 22000 V6).
- **Distinguish CCP vs oPRP vs PRP** — a 3-mark question loves this.
- **India-specific CLs:** Aflatoxin $\leq 20\text{ ppb}$ (FSSAI), Lead in noodles $\leq 2.5\text{ ppm}$, Listeria absence in 25 g (RTE foods).

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-Second Mnemonic for the 7 Principles:** "**H**ave **C**ontrol **L**imits **M**aintained; **C**orrect **V**iolations **D**iligently." → **H**azard, **C**CP, **L**imits, **M**onitoring, **C**orrective, **V**erification, **D**ocumentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT868 - HACCP PLANNING AND IMPLEMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HACCP Based Approach Towards Food Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points)** is a systematic, science-based, and preventive food safety management system that addresses biological, chemical, and physical hazards through the analysis and control of raw material production, procurement, handling, manufacturing, distribution, and consumption of the finished product.

According to the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)**, HACCP is internationally recognized as the gold standard for ensuring food safety across the entire farm-to-fork continuum. It is a **risk-based** rather than a **reactivity-based** approach, meaning hazards are controlled *before* they reach the consumer.

The formal definition can be expanded as follows:

$$\text{HACCP} = f(\text{Hazard Identification}, \text{CCPs}, \text{Critical Limits}, \text{Monitoring}, \text{Corrective Actions}, \text{Verification}, \text{Record Keeping})$$

### 1.1.1 Historical Genesis of HACCP

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** HACCP was conceived in the **1960s** by the **Pillsbury Company**, the **U.S. Army Natick Laboratories**, and **NASA**. The driving motive was to ensure "zero-defect" food for astronauts in the space program — where a single crumb or microbial contamination could be catastrophic.

Initially developed as a **zero-defect quality assurance concept** for space missions, HACCP evolved through three developmental eras:

| Era | Decade | Defining Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| Pioneering Era | 1970s | Pillsbury + NASA + Natick Lab development |
| Standardization Era | 1980s–1990s | Adoption by **NACMCF (1989, 1992, 1997)**, **Codex (1993)**, **EU Directive 93/43/EEC** |
| Globalization Era | 2000s–Present | **ISO 22000**, **FSSC 22000**, **GFSI benchmarking**, mandatory regulatory framework worldwide |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Highway Patrol" Model

Imagine a long highway from a **farm (raw material)** to your **dinner plate (consumer)**. Along this highway, there are several checkpoints. Some checkpoints are routine (e.g., toll booths), while a few are **critical** — places where, if a hazard (a thief, a broken bridge, a contaminated truck) passes through, the entire destination (the consumer) is at risk.

**HACCP works exactly like this:**

- The **highway** = the entire food chain
- The **hazards** = thieves, broken bridges (biological, chemical, physical)
- The **routine checkpoints** = Good Manufacturing Practices (GMPs), Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures (SSOPs), Prerequisite Programs (PRPs)
- The **critical checkpoints (CCPs)** = the strategic, high-stakes security gates where a single failure would be catastrophic
- The **monitoring team** = the HACCP team
- The **incident response protocol** = Corrective Actions

> [!TIP]
> **Student Takeaway:** HACCP is NOT a quality system. It is purely a **food safety** system. Quality (taste, appearance, texture) is handled by **ISO 9001** or **TQM**, while HACCP is laser-focused on *preventing consumer harm*.

### 1.3 Distinction: HACCP vs Other Food Safety Systems

| Parameter | HACCP | ISO 22000 | FSSC 22000 | GHP/GMP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Scope | Food safety only | Food safety + Management | Food safety + PRPs | Hygiene practices only |
| Mandatory globally? | Yes (Codex) | Voluntary | Voluntary | Often mandatory |
| Includes PRPs? | No (assumes them) | Yes | Yes | N/A |
| Certifiable? | No (system itself) | Yes | Yes | Sometimes |
| Developed by | Pillsbury/NASA | ISO | GFSI | National regulators |

## 1.4 Why HACCP is Mandatory in Modern Food Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSSAI, India)** makes HACCP-based Food Safety Management Systems (FSMS) **mandatory** for all high-risk food businesses in India, including dairy, meat, seafood, and packaged food processors.

Key drivers behind global HACCP adoption:

1. **Shift from end-product testing to preventive control**
2. **Reduction in foodborne illness outbreaks** (e.g., *Salmonella*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *E. coli* O157:H7)
3. **International trade facilitation** (Codex HACCP harmonizes global trade)
4. **Legal liability protection** for food business operators (FBOs)
5. **Cost efficiency** — recalls are 10–100× more expensive than prevention

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of a HACCP System

A working HACCP system rests on **two distinct foundations**:

### Pillar 1: Prerequisite Programs (PRPs)
These are the **universal hygiene and operational practices** that *must* be in place *before* HACCP can function. HACCP cannot compensate for broken foundational systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7 Categories of PRPs (per ISO/TS 22002-1):**
> 1. Construction and layout of buildings
> 2. Layout of premises and workspace
> 3. Utilities (water, air, energy)
> 4. Waste disposal
> 5. Equipment suitability and cleaning
> 6. Management of purchased materials
> 7. Pest control, hygiene, and personnel training

### Pillar 2: The 7 Principles of HACCP (Codex Alimentarius)

The **7 Principles** are the procedural heart of HACCP. They were refined by the **National Advisory Committee on Microbiological Criteria for Foods (NACMCF)** and adopted by the **Codex Committee on Food Hygiene (CCFH)**.

## 2.2 The 7 Principles — Detailed Theoretical Framework

### **Principle 1: Conduct a Hazard Analysis**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A systematic evaluation of all potential biological, chemical, and physical hazards associated with each raw material, processing step, and finished product.

Three hazard categories must be considered:

$$
H_{\text{total}} = H_{\text{biological}} \cup H_{\text{chemical}} \cup H_{\text{physical}}
$$

**Biological hazards (B):** *Salmonella spp.*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *E. coli* O157:H7, *Clostridium botulinum*, *Staphylococcus aureus*, *Bacillus cereus*, parasites, viruses (norovirus, hepatitis A), prions.

**Chemical hazards (C):** Pesticide residues, veterinary drug residues, heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, As), food additives (overdose), allergens (peanut, gluten, milk, soy, egg, fish, shellfish, sesame, sulphites), mycotoxins (aflatoxin B1 in groundnuts, ochratoxin in cereals), cleaning agent residues, allergens (Big-9 in the US, Big-14 in EU).

**Physical hazards (P):** Metal fragments, glass shards, bone fragments, plastic pieces, stones, wood splinters, personal items (hair, jewelry).

**Hazard analysis requires:**
- Identification of all potential hazards
- Assessment of **severity** (consequence if it occurs)
- Assessment of **probability** (likelihood of occurrence)
- Determination of **risk score** (severity × probability)
- Identification of **control measures**

### **Principle 2: Determine the Critical Control Points (CCPs)**

A **CCP** is defined as a *step at which control can be applied and is essential to prevent or eliminate a food safety hazard, or to reduce it to an acceptable level*.

CCPs are determined using the **Codex CCP Decision Tree**, which is a four-question logical flow:

```
Q1: Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard?
    → YES → Q2
    → NO  → NOT a CCP (modify process, product, or stop)

Q2: Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce 
    the likely occurrence of the hazard to an acceptable level?
    → YES → CCP
    → NO  → Q3

Q3: Could contamination with the identified hazard(s) occur 
    in excess of acceptable level(s), or could these increase 
    to unacceptable level(s)?
    → YES → Q4
    → NO  → NOT a CCP

Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified 
    hazard to an acceptable level?
    → YES → NOT a CCP
    → NO  → CCP
```

> [!TIP]
> **CCPs are NOT the same as Control Points (CPs).** A CP is a step where a hazard *can be controlled* but a loss of control would NOT necessarily result in an unacceptable risk. CCPs are non-negotiable safety gates; CPs are quality gates.

### **Principle 3: Establish Critical Limits**

**Critical Limits** are the *maximum and/or minimum values* to which a biological, chemical, or physical parameter must be controlled at a CCP to prevent, eliminate, or reduce a hazard to an acceptable level.

Critical limits are measurable and observable. Common metrics:

| CCP Type | Critical Limit (Examples) |
|---|---|
| Thermal pasteurization | $\geq 72^{\circ}\text{C}$ for $\geq 15$ seconds (HTST milk) |
| Sterilization (retort) | $F_0 \geq 3$ minutes, $121.1^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Refrigerated storage | $\leq 4^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Frozen storage | $\leq -18^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Metal detection | Fe $\leq 2.0$ mm, Non-Fe $\leq 2.5$ mm, SS $\leq 3.0$ mm |
| pH control (acidified foods) | $\leq 4.6$ |
| Water activity ($a_w$) | $\leq 0.85$ for *S. aureus* control |
| Salt concentration | $\geq 6\%$ in brine for pathogen inhibition |
| Chlorine in rinse water | $50$–$200$ ppm free residual chlorine |

Critical limits can be expressed in terms of:
- **Temperature**, **time**, **pH**, **$a_w$**, **moisture**, **salt**, **viscosity**, **preservative concentration**, **sensory parameters** (color, texture), or **physical measurements** (size, absence of foreign body).

### **Principle 4: Establish Monitoring Procedures**

**Monitoring** is the scheduled measurement or observation of a CCP relative to its critical limits. The purpose is to detect a *loss of control* at the CCP **before** the hazard reaches the consumer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key principle:** Monitoring is **continuous** wherever possible. For continuous processes, instruments (thermocouples, pH probes, metal detectors) provide continuous data. For batch processes, defined testing frequencies apply.

Each monitoring procedure must specify:
- **What** is monitored (parameter)
- **How** it is monitored (method/instrument)
- **How often** (frequency)
- **Who** is responsible (person/role)

### **Principle 5: Establish Corrective Actions**

**Corrective Actions** are the pre-planned, documented procedures to be followed when monitoring indicates a **deviation from a critical limit** (i.e., loss of control).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A deviation means: **(i)** isolate and quarantine the affected product, **(ii)** determine the product's disposition (rework, reject, destroy), **(iii)** identify and correct the root cause, **(iv)** verify the corrective action's effectiveness, and **(v)** document everything.

### **Principle 6: Establish Verification Procedures**

**Verification** evaluates whether the HACCP system is working *as designed*. It answers: *"Are we doing the right things, and doing them right?"*

Verification activities include:
- Internal audits of the HACCP plan
- Review of monitoring records and corrective action logs
- Calibration of monitoring instruments
- **End-product microbiological testing**
- **Validation of critical limits** (using scientific literature, challenge studies)
- Reassessment of the HACCP plan at least **annually**, or whenever there is a process change

### **Principle 7: Establish Record-Keeping and Documentation**

Every HACCP plan must be **documented** and **records retained** for verification, regulatory inspection, and traceability. The classical document hierarchy is:

$$
\underbrace{\text{HACCP Plan}}_{\text{Master Document}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{SOPs}}_{\text{Standard Operating Procedures}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Forms \& Logs}}_{\text{Evidence Records}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** A HACCP plan *without* documented records is **not a valid HACCP plan**. *"If it isn't written down, it didn't happen."* This is the most cited phrase by FSSAI auditors.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Framework Cheat Sheet

| Framework Element | Definition | Key Metric / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| Risk Score ($R$) | $R = S \times P$ | $S$ = Severity (1–4), $P$ = Probability (1–4); $R$ ranges 1–16 |
| Risk Classification | $R \leq 3$: Low, $4 \leq R \leq 9$: Moderate, $R \geq 10$: High | CCP required if High |
| $D$-value | Time to reduce microbial load by 1 log (90\%) | Minutes at given $T$ |
| $Z$-value | Temperature change to alter $D$-value by 1 log | $^\circ$C |
| $F$-value | Equivalent sterilization time at reference $T$ | Minutes at $121.1^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($z = 10^{\circ}\text{C}$) |
| $F_0$ | Reference sterilization value for low-acid canned food | $\geq 3$ min for commercial sterility |
| $a_w$ threshold | Water activity below which pathogens cannot grow | $a_w \leq 0.86$ for most pathogens |
| pH threshold | pH below which *Clostridium botulinum* cannot grow | $\text{pH} \leq 4.6$ |
| Pasteurization (HTST milk) | Time-temperature combo | $72^{\circ}\text{C}$ for $15$ s ($P > 0.99$ for *Coxiella burnetii*) |
| UHT (Ultra-High Temp) | Commercial sterility at ambient storage | $135$–$150^{\circ}\text{C}$ for $2$–$5$ s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D-value, Z-value, F-value are explained here in their KTU-context application to HACCP critical limit setting.** The 5-log reduction rule for *Listeria monocytogenes* in ready-to-eat (RTE) meats and the 7-log reduction for *Salmonella* in poultry products are the most common regulatory applications in HACCP validation studies.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the HACCP Framework

HACCP is the **backbone** of every major food safety certification:

- **Codex Alimentarius** (the international food standards reference)
- **FSSC 22000** (Foundation FSSC, based on ISO 22000 + ISO/TS 22002-1)
- **BRCGS (British Retail Consortium Global Standard)**
- **SQF (Safe Quality Food Institute)**
- **IFS (International Featured Standards)**
- **GFSI (Global Food Safety Initiative)** — benchmarks all of the above

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Indian food exports to the EU, US, and Japan *require* a HACCP-based FSMS. A B.Tech Food Technology graduate is expected to design, implement, audit, and continuously improve a HACCP plan as a core competency.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework: Building a HACCP Plan

## 3.1 The 12 Sequential Steps of HACCP Implementation

HACCP is implemented through a **12-step logical sequence**. Steps 1–5 are *prerequisite activities*, and Steps 6–12 correspond to the *7 Principles*.

| Step # | Activity | Type | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Assemble the HACCP team | Prerequisite | Team charter |
| 2 | Describe the product | Prerequisite | Product description sheet |
| 3 | Identify intended use | Prerequisite | Target consumer profile |
| 4 | Construct flow diagram | Prerequisite | Process flow diagram |
| 5 | Verify flow diagram on-site | Prerequisite | Verified flow |
| **6** | **List all potential hazards (Principle 1)** | **HACCP** | Hazard analysis worksheet |
| **7** | **Determine CCPs (Principle 2)** | **HACCP** | CCP decision tree |
| **8** | **Establish critical limits (Principle 3)** | **HACCP** | Critical limit table |
| **9** | **Establish monitoring (Principle 4)** | **HACCP** | Monitoring SOPs |
| **10** | **Establish corrective actions (Principle 5)** | **HACCP** | CA procedures |
| **11** | **Establish verification (Principle 6)** | **HACCP** | Verification schedule |
| **12** | **Establish records (Principle 7)** | **HACCP** | Master record file |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Pasteurized Milk HACCP Plan

To make the framework **tangible**, we walk through a complete HACCP plan for **Pasteurized Bottled Milk** — a KTU-favorite case study.

### Step 1: Assemble the HACCP Team
A multidisciplinary team including:
- Team leader (Plant Manager)
- Production Supervisor
- Quality Assurance Manager
- Microbiologist
- Maintenance Engineer
- Sanitation Supervisor
- External HACCP consultant (optional)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The team must include members from *each* operational function. A HACCP team with only QA staff will fail validation.

### Step 2: Product Description
- **Product:** Pasteurized, homogenized, standardized cow milk
- **Packaging:** 500 mL HDPE bottle, food-grade
- **Shelf life:** 3 days at $4 \pm 1^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **$a_w$:** $\approx 0.99$
- **pH:** $6.6$–$6.8$
- **Target consumer:** General public, including children and elderly

### Step 3: Intended Use
Direct consumption without further cooking. Vulnerable groups (infants, immunocompromised, pregnant) included.

### Step 4: Flow Diagram

$$
\text{Raw Milk Reception} \to \text{Pre-heating} \to \text{Standardization} \to \text{Homogenization} \to \text{HTST Pasteurization} \to \text{Cooling} \to \text{Storage} \to \text{Bottling} \to \text{Cold Storage} \to \text{Distribution}
$$

### Step 5: On-Site Verification
Walk the production floor; cross-check each step with the actual line.

### Step 6: Hazard Analysis (Principle 1)

A detailed **Hazard Analysis Worksheet** is prepared. Example excerpt:

| Process Step | Hazard | Category | Severity ($S$) | Probability ($P$) | Risk ($R = S \times P$) | Control Measure |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raw milk reception | *Salmonella*, *Listeria*, *Brucella* | B | 4 | 3 | **12 (High)** | Vendor certification, antibiotic residue test, plate count |
| Raw milk reception | Antibiotic residues | C | 4 | 2 | **8 (Mod.)** | Delvo test on each tanker |
| Pre-heating | None significant | — | — | — | — | — |
| Pasteurization | Pathogen survival (inadequate $T/t$) | B | 4 | 2 | **8 (Mod.)** | HTST regime, flow diversion device |
| Cooling | Post-pasteurization contamination | B | 4 | 2 | **8 (Mod.)** | Closed system, CIP, sanitary design |
| Bottling | Glass/metal fragments | P | 3 | 2 | **6 (Mod.)** | Visual inspection, metal detector on line |
| Cold storage | Psychrotrophic growth (refrigeration failure) | B | 3 | 2 | **6 (Mod.)** | Continuous $T$ monitoring with alarm |

### Step 7: CCP Determination (Principle 2)

Apply the **Codex Decision Tree** to each step with a hazard:

**For Pasteurization:**
- Q1: Do preventive controls exist? **YES** (heat)
- Q2: Is this step *specifically designed* to eliminate hazards? **YES** → **CCP-1** ✓

**For Raw Milk Reception:**
- Q1: Do preventive controls exist? **YES** (testing, supplier audit)
- Q2: Specifically designed to eliminate the hazard? **NO** (testing detects, doesn't eliminate)
- Q3: Could contamination exceed acceptable level? **YES**
- Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate the hazard? **YES** (pasteurization)
- → **NOT a CCP** (control point, not CCP)

**For Metal Detection at Bottling:**
- Q1: Do preventive controls exist? **YES** (metal detector)
- Q2: Specifically designed to eliminate the hazard? **YES** → **CCP-2** ✓

**For Cold Storage:**
- Q1: Do preventive controls exist? **YES** (refrigeration)
- Q2: Specifically designed to eliminate the hazard? **NO** (slows growth, doesn't eliminate)
- Q3: Could hazard exceed acceptable level? **YES**
- Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate? **NO** (cold storage is the end)
- → **CCP-3** ✓

### Step 8: Critical Limits (Principle 3)

| CCP | Critical Limit | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| CCP-1: HTST Pasteurization | $T \geq 72^{\circ}\text{C}$ for $t \geq 15$ s | FDA PMO, EU Reg. 853/2004 |
| CCP-2: Metal Detection | Fe $\leq 2.0$ mm, Non-Fe $\leq 2.5$ mm, SS $\leq 3.0$ mm | Internal standard, regulatory guidance |
| CCP-3: Cold Storage | $T \leq 4^{\circ}\text{C}$ | FSSAI, Codex |

### Step 9: Monitoring (Principle 4)

| CCP | What | How | How Often | Who |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CCP-1 | Temperature, time | Chart recorder + flow diversion device | Continuous | Pasteurizer operator |
| CCP-2 | Metal detector sensitivity | Test piece before start, every 1 h | Continuous + hourly test | Line operator |
| CCP-3 | Storage temperature | Digital probe + data logger | Continuous (5-min interval) | Cold room attendant |

### Step 10: Corrective Actions (Principle 5)

**For CCP-1 (Pasteurization):**
1. **Flow diversion device** automatically diverts substandard milk back to balance tank.
2. Operator checks chart, identifies time of deviation.
3. Quarantine product from that period.
4. Recycle or dispose of product based on QA evaluation.
5. Repair pasteurizer, recalibrate.
6. Document the incident and CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action).

**For CCP-2 (Metal Detection):**
1. Line stop.
2. Isolate and retain last 30 minutes of product.
3. Run rejection test, recalibrate detector.
4. Inspect rejected product, rework or destroy.
5. Document.

**For CCP-3 (Cold Storage):**
1. Alarm alerts.
2. Transfer product to backup refrigeration.
3. Investigate root cause.
4. Assess product safety (exposure time × temperature).
5. Document.

### Step 11: Verification (Principle 6)

| Activity | Frequency | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Plate count + coliform test on finished product | Daily | QC Lab |
| Phosphatase test (alkaline phosphatase negative) | Every batch | QC Lab |
| Calibration of pasteurizer temperature gauge | Monthly | Engineering |
| Endotoxin / challenge test for pasteurization | Annually | External lab |
| Internal HACCP audit | Quarterly | QA Manager |
| Full HACCP plan review | Annually | HACCP Team |

### Step 12: Record-Keeping (Principle 7)

Records to maintain:
- **Master HACCP Plan** (signed, dated, version-controlled)
- **Product description** sheet
- **Hazard analysis worksheet**
- **CCP determination records** (with decision tree)
- **Critical limit specifications**
- **Monitoring logs** (charts, data logger printouts)
- **Corrective action reports**
- **Verification reports** (audit, calibration, lab test results)
- **Training records** for HACCP team and operators
- **Review and revision log**

> [!TIP]
> **Record retention period:** Regulatory minimum is typically **3 years** (FSSAI), but high-risk operations retain records for the **shelf life of the product + 1 year** (often 2–3 years total).

## 3.3 Quantitative Risk Scoring — Detailed Calculation

The **Risk Score ($R$)** is mathematically defined as:

$$
R = S \times P
$$

Where the scoring scales are:

| Score | Severity ($S$) Descriptor | Probability ($P$) Descriptor |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | Catastrophic (death, hospitalization) | Frequent (occurs daily/weekly) |
| 3 | Major (serious illness, hospitalization) | Likely (occurs monthly) |
| 2 | Moderate (mild illness, self-limiting) | Occasional (occurs yearly) |
| 1 | Minor (no illness, cosmetic only) | Rare (occurs < once in 5 years) |

**Worked example** for **Pathogen survival in pasteurization**:
- Severity ($S$): A *Salmonella* outbreak causing hospitalization → $S = 4$
- Probability ($P$): Improper pasteurization occurs ~2× per year → $P = 2$
- Risk: $R = 4 \times 2 = 8$ → **Moderate**

**Decision rule:**
- If $R \geq 10$ (High) → Mandatory CCP
- If $4 \leq R \leq 9$ (Moderate) → CCP if no downstream control
- If $R \leq 3$ (Low) → Control Point (CP) only

## 3.4 Validation vs. Verification — The Subtle Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation** answers: *"Are we doing the RIGHT things?"* (proves the HACCP plan is scientifically sound for the specific product/process).
> **Verification** answers: *"Are we doing things RIGHT?"* (proves the system is operating as designed day-to-day).

| Aspect | Validation | Verification |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | Once (or after major change) | Ongoing (daily, weekly, monthly) |
| Purpose | Confirm scientific basis | Confirm operational compliance |
| Methods | Challenge studies, literature review, pathogen inoculation studies | Audits, sampling, calibration, record review |
| Performed by | Independent expert or external lab | Internal HACCP team / QA |

This distinction is **frequently tested** in KTU ESE papers.

```mermaid
    J -. CCP-3 .-> JFLAG[Critical Limit below 4C]

    style E fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    style I fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    style J fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    style EFLAG fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style IFLAG fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style JFLAG fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
